experiments.title: How gift array options in a high urgency sticky bar impact donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array to the HUC sticky bar for IMB website visitors will increase engagement and donor conversion by minimizing the number of steps on the donation page, reducing friction and decision-making, resulting in more gifts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/15/2026 - 01/19/2026
During IMB’s 2026 Winter Olympics campaign, we re-tested the sticky bar’s gift array buttons to understand their impact on donor behavior.
In reviewing the original execution, we identified opportunities to improve how the sticky bar guided visitors toward giving—particularly around campaign value proposition and giving array amounts. We re-tested this approach to determine whether modifying the sticky bar could more effectively encourage gifts and to gather data needed to inform future fundraising tactics.
For this test, 50% of site traffic was shown the standard sticky bar experience featuring a single call-to-action button. The remaining 50% received a treatment version with three buttons mirroring the suggested gift amounts on the donation page, allowing visitors to engage with a giving option earlier in their journey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment shows that adding a gift array to the IMB website’s sticky bar significantly increased both visitor engagement and donor conversion. The treatment version—featuring three suggested gift amount buttons—outperformed the standard sticky bar by 448.4% in Clicks for All Traffic, with 99.9% statistical confidence.
These results suggest that introducing giving options earlier in the donor journey reduces friction and simplifies decision-making on the donation page—leading to more completed gifts.
Downstream Impact on Donations:
To understand how increased engagement translated into giving behavior, we analyzed downstream donation metrics. During the test period, the control version generated 1 gift, while the treatment generated 16 gifts, representing a 1,495% increase in transactions (100% LOC).
This confirms that the gift array not only drives more clicks but also meaningfully motivates visitors to complete a donation.
Gift Amount Performance:
We also evaluated which gift array buttons led to the most donations:
$25 Button: 6 gifts, all at $25
 	$50 Button: 3 gifts, all at $50
 	$100 Button: 3 gifts (2 at $100, 1 at $150)
 	More Options: 4 gifts, resulting in varied amounts ($20, $50, $50, $200)
Notably, most donors retained the amount they selected in the sticky bar through to the completion of the donation, reinforcing the value of early commitment.
Conclusion & Recommendation:
Overall, the gift array on the sticky bar effectively removes a key decision point from the donation page, leading to higher engagement and more completed gifts.
For future testing, IMB should continue exploring gift arrays and similar friction-reducing elements in high-visibility areas of the site. Ongoing iteration will help further optimize the giving experience and maximize donor conversion and revenue.

experiments.title: How Interrupting the Browsing Experience with a Gift Selection Slide-up Impacts Online Donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing a gift selection slide-up for website visitors on Enter the Bible will achieve an increase in donation conversions because interrupting passive browsing with a clear and immediate giving opportunity reduces friction and prompts action through attention capture and choice simplification.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/19/2025 - 03/20/2026
Luther Seminary operates several high-traffic digital properties, with Enter the Bible receiving approximately 450,000 visitors within 30 days—which is more than its other platforms. Given this scale, the team identified Enter the Bible as the most viable environment to test new on-site fundraising tactics that could generate meaningful learning and results.
Historically, the site has relied on relatively passive donation opportunities, which do not actively interrupt or redirect user attention. This raises an important question: are visitors motivated to give when presented with a more prominent and immediate fundraising prompt, or does this type of interruption create friction that reduces engagement?
To explore this, the team is testing the impact of a more attention-grabbing on-site element: a gift selection slide-up. This element appears during the browsing experience and is designed to actively divert user attention toward making a donation.
The experiment includes two variations:
Control: The existing website experience with no additional fundraising interruption
Treatment: A site-wide slide-up featuring three predefined donation amounts and an additional “other” option
This test is being run across general website visitors to Enter the Bible, leveraging the platform’s high traffic volume to reach a large and diverse audience.
The goal of this experiment is to better understand donor motivation in a passive content environment. Specifically, whether introducing a more assertive, attention-directing prompt increases donation activity, or whether it disrupts the user experience in a way that suppresses giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test demonstrates that a more interruptive, attention-grabbing donation prompt can drive incremental giving on a high-traffic content site.

The gift selection slide-up (treatment) generated 8 donations totaling $347.80, with an average gift of $43.48, while the control generated no recorded donations. This suggests that proactively prompting users can convert a small portion of otherwise passive visitors into donors.

While VWO reported a +0.03% lift with 99.5% confidence, the results required additional validation. Because users were redirected from the Enter the Bible domain to a Luther Seminary donation page without cross-domain tracking enabled, conversions were not fully captured within the testing platform.

To account for this, a backup campaign tracking setup within the donation system was used to verify results. Although originally implemented to keep the test clean, this approach ultimately ensured accurate attribution and confirmed that all donations came from the treatment experience.

From a behavioral perspective, the results suggest that capturing attention and simplifying the giving decision through preset gift options can prompt action, even when initial donor intent is low. However, the low overall conversion rate indicates that attention alone is not enough—stronger motivational framing will likely be required to drive meaningful scale.

Next Steps
Keep the Donation Experience Within a Single Domain: Future tests will prioritize directing users to donation pages within the Enter the Bible environment. Keeping the full journey within a single, first-party domain will improve tracking accuracy, reduce attribution gaps, and enable more confident decision-making.
 	Test Alternative On-Site Donation Prompts: Build on this learning by testing additional formats, such as a sticky donation slide-out, to understand how different levels of persistence and visibility impact conversion.
 	Strengthen Motivational Framing: Pair attention-grabbing elements with messaging that emphasizes urgency, impact, or donor identity to increase both conversion rate and overall revenue.

experiments.title: What Drives More Donations on a Pop-up: a Content Offer or a Direct Ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesized that offering something of value first, a downloadable Lenten cookbook, would build engagement and trust, ultimately leading to higher downstream conversion rates than presenting a direct donation ask upfront.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2025 - 04/14/2025
This experiment was conducted for Catholic Extension Society, a nonprofit organization dedicated to supporting Catholic communities in under-resourced areas across the United States. The test took place during Lent and aimed to evaluate the effectiveness of two distinct homepage pop-up strategies: a value-first approach offering a downloadable cookbook versus a direct donation ask.
This was the second iteration of the experiment. In the initial version, we compared a direct ask to a content offer featuring Gospel reflections. For this follow-up test, we wanted to determine whether the nature of the content—specifically, a cookbook—would drive more donor conversions than a straightforward donation appeal.

experiments.title: How an exit intent vs a slideup affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that testing a slide up vs an exit intent for site profiles will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the slide up reduced the friction of a user having to select the gift amount on the donation page, and the exit intent encouraged the user to help CB meet the match as others have done.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/09/2026
For CaringBridge, we had previously tested two slide up versions and three exit-intent versions.
For this test, we wanted to compare the winning versions of the slide up and exit intent to see how they would perform.
This was tested on both mobile and desktop and on site profiles.
experiments.keyLearnings:
From the results of this experiment, we can see that the slide up had a 16.1% increase in transactions, with a 96.8% level of confidence. Therefore, this test is valid.
What's interesting here is that, when looking at the results of the experiment by desktop and mobile traffic, there was a slight decrease in transactions for the slideup on desktop traffic, but a significant increase in donations on mobile traffic.
This helps determine that the slide up is a more optimized experience for mobile, and there could be opportunities to optimize the exit intent for mobile users to help mobile traffic respond better.

experiments.title: How a softer ask on a sticky bar impacts click rate and downstream donor metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the softer language on the sticky bar for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks and downstream metrics because it leads to a larger pool of warm prospects who are more likely to donate once on the page to sign his card.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/26/2025
For Dr. Carson’s birthday this year, we tested the language on their website sticky bars to see which message resonated more with the audience: a direct ask like “Make my gift” or a softer invitation like “Sign the card.”
The test ran for about three weeks, with traffic split evenly between the two versions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using softer language on the sticky bar for homepage visitors, such as "Sign the card", resulted in a significant increase in clicks compared to a direct ask like "Make my gift". The treatment version saw a 425.7% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the softer approach resonated better with the audience.
More importantly, though, we wanted to see the downstream metrics on this test to measure the true impact of it.
Control:
21 gifts, $1,594 $75 Average gift
Treatment:
31 gifts, $876$28 Average gift
These results indicated a 46.7% lift in transactions (82.8% LOC), which is encouraging to see, but they also indicate a substantial drop in average gift.
In future experimentation, we should continue to test the use of softer language and invitations in our calls to action. By creating a more inviting and inclusive message, we can attract a larger pool of warm prospects and potentially increase the likelihood of donations. It is important to remember that the tone and language used in our communications can have a significant impact on audience engagement and conversion rates. By leveraging these learnings, we can optimize our messaging to better connect with our audience and drive meaningful actions.

experiments.title: How Having a Humanized Exit Intent pop up during Calendar Year End affect conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized version of the exit intent for users wanting to exit out of the main donation page will achieve an increase in donations because seeing a message from someone from Time of Grace would motivate them to want to participate in the CYE efforts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2024 - 12/16/2024
For Time of Grace’s Calendar Year End, we wanted to test two variations of an exit intent: a regular one and a humanized version that would come from a person. We ran this experiment on their main donation page to capture users leaving the page and reinforce why they should donate. The exit intent would appear when the user hovered over the exit button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the humanized version of the exit intent for users wanting to leave the main donation page did not achieve the desired outcome. In fact, it resulted in a significant decrease in transactions for all traffic, with a 100% statistical confidence level, and an even larger reduction of one-time donations.
In future experimentation, carefully considering the messaging and timing of exit intents is crucial. While adding a human touch can often be beneficial in marketing and communication strategies, in this case, it did not resonate with users in a way that encouraged donations.
It may be beneficial to test different messaging variations, including humanized and more traditional approaches, to see what resonates best with users. Additionally, considering the timing of the exit intent and ensuring it is strategically placed in the user journey may also impact its effectiveness.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing and iterating on different strategies to determine what motivates users to donate, ultimately leading to more successful fundraising efforts for Time of Grace Ministry.

experiments.title: How “Yes/No” Autonomy Language on a CYE Sticky Bar Impacts Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a yes/no CTA on the MAF homepage will increase conversion rate because users will be more likely to give when their autonomy is preserved and they are given a “micro-decision” at the top of the funnel before being asked to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2023 - 12/31/2023
One simple but powerful way to encourage donors to take action is by giving them a small choice up front. In this test, the idea was to use “yes/no” language in a sticky bar at the top of the homepage. Rather than jumping straight to a donation ask, the message first invited visitors to make a micro‑decision—a quick yes or no—before leading into the giving opportunity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that incorporating a yes/no CTA that provides users with a micro-decision at the top of the funnel significantly increased the donor conversion rate. The treatment group saw a 62.4% increase in donations with high statistical confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue exploring the impact of autonomy and micro-decision making on user behavior. This approach of giving users a sense of control and involvement in the donation process could be applied to other areas of the website or fundraising campaigns.

Overall, this experiment's results highlight the value of providing users with autonomy and decision-making opportunities in driving conversions. By understanding and leveraging this psychology, future experimentation efforts can focus on creating more engaging and compelling user experiences.

experiments.title: How a slide up with a gift array performs against a pop-up with a simple donate button
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering people specific gift asks for the fund drive will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/30/2025 - 08/13/2025
During a special fund drive for OPB, the public media broadcasting station for Oregon and Southwest Washington, we wanted to test how a standard pop-up with a simple donate button (what OPB traditionally uses during a fund drive) would perform against a slide-up that included specific gift arrays.
This test was run on article pages only; a separate test was run on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There were some exciting results from this experiment. The unique fundraising environment for this test is not something that cannot be easily repeatable, unless there is another urgent/emergency campaign. That being said, the increase in conversion rate from the slide up gift array version is huge and statistically valid. The results above can be directly attributed to people clicking the button/array and completing their gift.

A couple of interesting downstream results to be aware of:
The control version had a slightly higher average gift than the treatment version; given the lower volume of gifts on the control, that could be the influence of a larger gift or two, or the fact that we were showing donors a gift array up front in the treatment version, leading them to give a lower average gift (gift arrays on the actual donation forms were the same for both control and treatment).
 	Regarding the overall influence both had on total giving, the gift array version had a slightly larger influence, showing a 23% increase in conversion rates overall with a 96% level of confidence. This means that people who saw the gift array version and then made a gift at some point to a donation form on the website (not necessarily the one linked directly from the pop-up) were 23% more likely to convert.

experiments.title: How does donor-centric language on a match sticky bar impact donor conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that donor-centric language on the match sticky bar for users on the CaringBridge site will achieve an increase in conversion rate because priorities the donor as the hero and highlights the impact of their gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2025
Frequently throughout the year, CaringBridge will have short, 2-day match campaigns. These sprints are driven by urgency and the opportunity for donors to have their gift doubled—dollar for dollar. During these campaigns, CaringBridge has specific language they use regarding the match, but we hypothesized that it paints the provider of the match, rather than the donor themselves, as the hero. We believe that by adjusting the language, making it centered around the donor and their impact—versus leading with who has provided the match and for how much—will increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that donor-centric language on the match sticky bar led to a significant increase in donations on the CaringBridge site. By prioritizing the donor as the hero and highlighting the impact of their gift, the conversion rate increased by 40.5%. This indicates that donors respond positively to messaging that focuses on their role and the significance of their contribution.

Perhaps the most impactful part of this experiment is that it's a learning CaringBridge can now begin to leverage every time they have a match sticky bar running. In addition to the increase in donor conversion rate, it also worth documenting the increase in clicks and revenue as well.

The treatment saw a 16% increase in clicks with a valid level of confidence, alongside a 33.2% increase in evenue with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky, top navigation bar in mobile impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky upper navigation bar that prioritizes donate and search for for mobile users on the homepage will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it highlights donate as a clickable button, while keeping this call-to-action present at the top.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/16/2025 - 07/19/2025
On Time of Grace’s homepage, the mobile experience presents a vastly different display to users. The donate button is removed from the navigation bar, in favor of a lengthwise “banner style” button along with a separate call-to-action: Make a donation. We hypothesized that separating the navigation bar into a top and bottom split, with a sticky upper portion that presented Donate and Search functions would increase conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing a sticky upper navigation bar that prioritizes the donate and search functions for mobile users on the homepage significantly increased donor conversion rate. In this case, the treatment resulted in a 118.1% increase in transactions for all traffic with a 96.2% statistical level of confidence. Revenue also experienced a 90.3% increase with a 100% level of confidence.
This experiment underscores the importance of the mobile experience. Often, the desktop layout is tested extensively , only for key elements to be left off in the mobile view due to lack of space: different donate CTAs, hiding the button in hamburger menus, changing it's size, functionality, etc.
Few can prioritize all calls to action in one mobile navigation, but splitting the experience into a top and bottom allowed Time of Grace to prioritize the donate experience while still maintaining a clean display and user experience.

experiments.title: How a donation form with an in-line payment module performs against a pop-up module.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a in-line payment module for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion dates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/27/2025 - 07/01/2025
This organization’s donation platform launched a new structure to their main donation form (due to enhanced PCI compliance) where the billing info would pop-up in a new screen once you chose your gift array. We wanted to test whether this potential new layer of friction impacted donation conversion rates, so we created a more standard donation form as the treatment variant.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While our original hypothesis was that adding the pop-up would increase friction, and therefore reduce conversion rates, the results of this experiment showed the opposite.

Not only the control in this case have a 32% decrease in conversions with a 100% level of confidence, but those results also translated when looking at one-time versus monthly gifts. The control had a 29% decrease in conversions for one-time gifts, and a 34% decrease in monthly gifts, both with 100% level of confidence.

For future experiments, we will want to test whether the placement of the module on mobile made any difference, as well as the language used to 'click' to get to the payment information.

experiments.title: Using an Exit-Intent Popup to Increase Donor Conversion During a High-Urgency Campaign (May Match 2025)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a lower donation ask in an exit-intent pop-up will encourage more users to complete their gift, capturing donations that might have otherwise been abandoned during a high-urgency campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2025 - 06/03/2025
As part of the May Match 2025 campaign—an urgency-driven effort to acquire new monthly donors (FamilyLife Partners)—we wanted to test whether an exit intent popup on the donation page could reduce abandonment and increase conversions.
To do this, we decided to present an exit-intent popup, which would give users a lower-barrier monthly donation ask if they showed intent to leave the page, and instead encourage them to complete a smaller gift. The treatment experience included a popup triggered by mouse-exit behavior on desktop or time delay on mobile, offering a $10/month ask with emotionally resonant, impact-focused messaging. The control experience maintained the standard donation page with no popup intervention.
We intentionally excluded visitors who had previously interacted with an impact popup earlier in the session, to isolate the effect of this standalone exit-intent experience. The popup emphasized that even a small monthly gift could make a lasting difference—supporting 76 families with Christ-centered resources.
We wanted to focus this test on recapturing users who demonstrated exit behavior, hypothesizing that a timely, emotionally framed, and low-friction ask could nudge hesitant users to commit before abandoning the donation process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test demonstrated that introducing an exit-intent popup with a lower-barrier ask on the donation page can meaningfully reduce abandonment and increase both one-time and recurring donor conversion—especially during high-urgency campaigns like May Match.
The treatment experience, which offered a $10/month giving option when users showed intent to leave, outperformed the control by 44.2% in overall donor conversion (2.7% vs. 1.8%), achieving statistical significance at 99.4% confidence.
Looking at giving types:
Recurring gifts more than doubled, with a 102.1% increase in recurring donor conversion (0.7% vs. 0.3%, 98.3% confidence).One-time gifts also increased by 32.2% (2.1% vs. 1.6%, 94.2% confidence).
These results suggest that a well-timed, emotionally compelling, and low-friction offer can recapture hesitant donors right before they leave—and that framing the donation ask as small but impactful can activate both urgency and achievability.
The success of the recurring donor uplift is particularly important in the context of long-term donor value. This test validates the exit-intent popup as a powerful conversion tool and opens the door for future testing around:
Tailoring the popup message for one-time vs. recurring emphasisOptimizing design and placement for mobile vs. desktopTesting variations in gift amounts and message tone (e.g., more urgent vs. more relational)
This was a high-impact win with broad implications for donation page optimization.

experiments.title: How including a gift array in a high urgency campaign sticky bar affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding gift array buttons to a lower third for Today in the Word homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/10/2025
During Today in the Word’s FYE Campaign, we tested adding gift array buttons into the lower third vs a one-button ask in a lower third. For this test, we tracked CTA clicks on both the control and treatment to measure how effective each one of these web assets persuaded someone to navigate to the donation page, as well as transaction and revenue that came directly from these assets.
This test ran for 8 days and the treatment was shown to 50% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding gift array buttons to a lower third for Today in the Word homepage visitors significantly increased donor conversion metrics. The treatment resulted in a 172.0% increase in Transactions (src=tw_fye25_footer*) for All Traffic with a 98.2% level of confidence, indicating that the treatment was successful in persuading users to navigate to the donation page and complete a transaction. Based on these results, we should be using a lower third with a gift array in it for future lower thirds for Today in the Word.
We also tracked clicks on both the control and treatment lower thirds and the treatment saw a 51.4% lift in clicks ( 97.1% LOC) compared to the control. These results indicate that the treatment not only encouraged more people to navigate to the donation page, but also resulted in an increase in transcations.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of providing users with the opportunity to select a gift amount before reaching the donation page. This can streamline the giving process for users and potentially lead to higher conversion rates. Additionally, it is essential to continue testing different variations of donation prompts and CTAs to optimize donor conversion metrics further.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky navigation bar on mobile affects clicks on Donate button
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a stacked navigation bar for mobile users will achieve an increase in clicks to the donate button because adding that stacked bar would increase clarity among the buttons and look cleaner.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2025 - 05/20/2025
For CaringBridge, their mobile navigation bar consisted of multiple buttons where there was minimal space and all the buttons looked very small and stuck together. For this test, we wanted to change the layout of the navigation bar. We added a space above the original navigation bar and added the donate and search button on the extra space above the nav bar. The stacked part with the search button and donate button was sticky, so users could see it while scrolling through. We wanted to test if doing this would affect clicks to the donate button for mobile users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

 	Adding a stacked navigation bar for mobile users led to a significant increase in clicks to the donate button (72.4% increase). This indicates that making the donate button more prominent and easily accessible can positively impact user engagement with this feature.
 	The navigation search button also saw a substantial increase in clicks (676% increase). This suggests that improving the visibility and usability of buttons in the navigation bar can drive higher interaction rates across multiple features.

In future experimentation, we should consider the following:

 	Continue to prioritize the visibility and accessibility of key features, such as the donate button, by strategically placing them in the navigation bar where they are easily noticeable and clickable.
 	Use A/B testing and other experimentation methods to iteratively optimize the layout and design of the mobile navigation bar to enhance user experience and drive engagement with important features.
 	Consider applying similar strategies to other areas of the website or app to improve overall user interaction and achieve organizational goals, such as increasing donations or promoting specific actions.

experiments.title: How including a premium in slide out copy impacts clicks during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the premium in the fiscal year end slide out copy for Moody Radio’s content page visitors will achieve an increase in click rate because it offers an incentive to give to their fiscal year end campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/05/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year End Campaign, they were offering a physical premium with any gift amount. In order to better understand how their audience responds to premiums, we tested mentioning the premium within the copy of the slide-out. All other factors remained constant.
The treatment was shown to half of the traffic to Moody Radio’s content pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. Including the premium offering in the copy of the fiscal year end slide-out for Moody Radio content pages resulted in a significant increase in clicks.

2. The treatment group experienced a 43.6% increase in clicks compared to the control group, showing the effectiveness of mentioning the premium as an incentive for donations.

3. The statistical significance level of 98.6% provides high confidence in the results of the experiment meaning that the treatment should be used in future campaigns.

Implications for Future Experimentation:

1. Future experiments should continue to explore the impact of premiums or incentives on donor behavior.

2. It is recommended to consistently test different types of premiums and messaging strategies to optimize the effectiveness of incentives in driving donations.

3. Consider implementing similar tactics in other fundraising campaigns or donation drives to leverage the success observed in this experiment.

4. Continuously monitor and analyze the results of experiments to refine strategies and improve performance in fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How including a premium in slide out copy impacts donor conversion metrics during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the premium in the fiscal year end slide out copy for Moody Radio content page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it offers an incentive to give to their fiscal year end campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/05/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year End Campaign, they were offering a physical premium with any gift amount. In order to better understand how their audience responds to premiums, we tested mentioning the premium within the copy of the slide-out. All other factors remained constant.
The treatment was shown to half of the traffic to Moody Radio’s content pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including information about the physical premium in the fiscal year end slide-out copy for Moody Radio content page visitors significantly increased donor conversion metrics. The treatment resulted in a 238.1% increase in donations for all traffic with a 95.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the inclusion of the premium was a successful strategy. Therefore, including the premium in the copy produced not only more clicks to the FYE donation page, but it also produced more motivated traffic to give.

 

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the effectiveness of incentives, such as physical premiums, in encouraging donations. It would be beneficial to explore different types of incentives and how they impact donor behavior. Additionally, considering the success of this experiment, it may be worthwhile to incorporate information about premiums in other fundraising initiatives to further boost donor conversion rates. Regular testing and optimization based on data-driven insights will help Moody Radio continue to improve their fundraising efforts and maximize donor engagement.

experiments.title: How does adding an exclusive offers slide-up to a landing page impact email acquisition rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by presenting users with additional video series options in a slide-up, they will be more likely to find content that resonates with them, which will lead to a higher email acquisition rate. By offering more choices, we expect to reduce friction in the decision-making process and increase the likelihood that users will opt in with their email addresses.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2025 - 03/24/2025
We hypothesized by providing visitors with alternative video series options, visitors would more likely to find content that resonates with them, thereby increasing the likelihood of signing up. The test was not about determining which series resonated more with people but rather whether offering additional options would lead to more email sign-ups.
To test this, we implemented an A/B test in VWO, splitting traffic evenly (50/50) between the Control and Treatment. The Control version of the landing page remained unchanged, while the Treatment featured a slide-up at the bottom of the page displaying three alternative video series options. We tested this on “Proof You Need,” their most successful video series we are running on Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran for 18 days, during which the control acquired 1,249 emails and the treatment acquired 1,155 emails. This resulted in a 6.8% decrease in email acquisition for the Treatment at a 95.6% level of confidence. While we initially hypothesized that offering more options would increase conversions, the results suggest that the slide-up may have unintentionally introduced decision friction. Users who arrived on the landing page were likely already primed to sign up based on the ad they clicked, but the presence of additional choices may have disrupted that intent, leading to hesitation rather than action. Instead of reinforcing their decision, the added options could have created uncertainty, ultimately reducing the overall conversion rate.

Based on these results, we recommend maintaining the current landing page design without the slide-up to avoid introducing additional friction in the sign-up process.

experiments.title: Does a Humanized Video Outperform Traditional Copy?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe the version with the video will outperform the traditional body copy because video has the potential to create a more engaging, emotional connection with the audience. By incorporating visuals, voice, and storytelling, the video humanizes WUNC’s mission, making the impact of public radio feel more personal and immediate. This humanized approach is expected to deepen emotional resonance, create a stronger sense of urgency, and ultimately lead to more transactions during the fund drive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2025 - 04/29/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, an NPR member station serving listeners throughout North Carolina, during their Spring Fund Drive. In previous campaigns, we’ve tested a variety of approaches using a slide-up donation prompt featuring a gift array. For this particular test, we wanted to explore whether incorporating a video in place of traditional body copy would lead to more transactions. The structure of the slide-up remained largely unchanged—donors still saw the same headline and the same suggested gift array amounts. The only variable was the presence of the video in one version, compared to body text in the other. This allowed us to isolate the impact of the video and evaluate whether visual storytelling could better drive engagement and giving behavior.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The control version with body copy outperformed the video treatment in both total gifts and total revenue. The control generated 70 total gifts and raised $3,078, compared to 53 gifts and $1,986 raised by the treatment. Sustaining gifts were higher in the control (46 vs. 38), and it also brought in more first-time sustaining donors (37 vs. 26). However, the video treatment did result in a slightly higher total from sustaining gifts ($851 vs. $813) and attracted more first-time one-time donors (10 vs. 6). While the video showed some promise in reaching brand-new donors, especially one-time givers, it ultimately did not surpass the traditional copy in overall donor response or revenue generation.

Suggested Next Steps:

Refine Video Length and Content: Test shorter, more focused videos or versions that directly highlight donor impact, urgency, or testimonials. It’s possible the current video didn’t clearly resonate with the majority of prospects.
Test Hybrid Messaging: Try combining a short video with a concise supporting line or two of body copy to guide the viewer and reinforce the CTA.
 	Segmented Testing: Run the video test only for new visitors or new users, where the video’s ability to humanize WUNC might have more impact.
 	Button Placement and CTA Testing: Explore different placements or phrasings for the donate button within the slide-up to make it more action-oriented or prominent
 	Follow-Up Engagement Test: Test using the video slide-up not as the initial ask, but as a retargeting element for users who didn’t give on their first visit.

experiments.title: Does adding a slide-up on the homepage impact donation conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a slide-up to the homepage for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates because of the principles of visual salience and immediacy. because  of the principles of visual salience and immediacy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2024 - 11/12/2024
This test explored the psychological impact of adding a slide-up donation module on the Homepage. The goal was to increase engagement and prompt recurring donations by creating a subtle yet persuasive nudge for visitors.
Our hypothesis was that by adding a slide up banner to the Homepage, we will increase donor conversion. This is because by providing users with the option to donate without having to click a donate button first, and then carrying their donation choice over to the donation form, we are reducing friction for the users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Results:
+25% Increase in Recurring Donor Conversion Rate: Demonstrated a statistically significant lift at a 96% confidence level.
+54% Increase in Recurring Revenue per Visitor: Achieved a statistically significant lift with a 99% confidence level.
Overall donor conversion rates increased by 9.8% with a 96% level of confidence. When digging into the data, we saw some interesting trends:
Rev/visitor across the board increased by 1.9% with a 58% level of confidence.
Desktop:
Conversion rates increased by 8.4% with a 81% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased, albeit only by 3% with a 60% level of confidence.
Mobile:
Conversion rates increased by 11.9% with a 91% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased, albeit only by 3.7% with a 61% level of confidence.
It is when looking at one-time versus recurring gifts that we saw the biggest impact:
One-time:
The slide-up had a 6% increase in conversion rates, with a 78% level of confidence. The revenue per visitor was relatively flat, with only a 0.4% decrease and a 52% level of confidence.
Recurring:
The slide-up had a 29% increase in recurring gifts, with a 96% level of confidence. This also translated to a much higher revenue per visitor, with a 53% increase and a 99% level of confidence.
Behavioral Insights:
The slide-up module likely leveraged the principles of visual salience and immediacy, drawing users' attention without disrupting their browsing experience. By introducing a gentle prompt, the module created a sense of immediacy that encouraged users to act on their initial donation intent. Additionally, the default effect—where people tend to stick with recurring actions once initiated—likely contributed to the significant lift in recurring donor conversions and revenue.
The positive impact, particularly among recurring donors, suggests that the slide-up module reinforces commitment by subtly positioning the act of recurring donation as a socially desirable default behavior. The module's presence on the Homepage also supported mere-exposure effect principles, where repeated visibility increases familiarity and perceived trustworthiness of the donation option.
Next Steps:
We will test the slide-up module against the emergency banner for our next iteration. This will allow us to compare which feature better leverages urgency and social proof to maximize visitor engagement and conversion, further refining our understanding of behavioral triggers in the donation journey

experiments.title: How a hyper-short popup affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a hyper-short popup for mobile users will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because since these are mobile users, a short popup would create less friction as there was less to look through, therefore driving the click.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/03/2025
CaringBridge has a popup on their website that has encourages the user to make a recurring gift. For this test, we wanted to test a hyper-short treatment because we were testing the mobile version of the popup. This popup appeared every 4th visit.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the hyper-short popup for mobile users did not lead to an increase in click-through rate. In fact, it resulted in a significant decrease in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that the hypothesis was not supported by the data, and in this case, a shorter popup may have actually deterred users from clicking.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of the changes being implemented. It is possible that in some cases, providing more information or content in the popup may actually be more effective in engaging users and prompting them to take action. Additionally, it is crucial to always test hypotheses with a control group to accurately measure the impact of the changes.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of data-driven decision-making and the need to carefully evaluate the potential outcomes of any changes before implementation.

experiments.title: How a donor goal on a high urgency campaign pushdown impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor goal to the February Sustainers Pushdown for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the potential donor would feel like their monthly gift would have a larger impact on the goal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/03/2025
During The Navigators February Sustainers Campaign, we decided to test into adding a donor goal into the pushdown copy.
The first treatment variation set a blanket donor goal of 100 monthly donors, and the second treatment variation set donor goals for each amount depicted on the gift array (i.e. Will you be one of the 50 monthly donors to make a $15 monthly gift, 30 donors to make a $25 monthly gift, or 20 donors to make a $50 monthly gift today?)
The goal of this test was to determine whether The Navigators’ audience would respond positively to a defined donor goal, and if so, whether they were more motivated by an overarching goal or one broken up into different amounts that felt more attainable.
Our hypothesis was that breaking down the donor goal into smaller, specific amounts would make it feel more achievable, increasing the likelihood of giving. By seeing a clearer, tangible impact, donors would be more inclined to contribute, believing their gift could make a greater difference.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor goal to the February Sustainers Pushdown for homepage visitors significantly increased donor conversion metrics. The treatment variation that set donor goals for each amount depicted on the gift array resulted in a 266.8% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control group, with a 96.8% statistical level of confidence.
It is possible that Treatment #2 caused more confusion and friction on the page rather than the very clear goal of 100 donors in Treatment #1. This suggests that The Navigators audience reacts positively to clarity and set goal amounts.
Furthermore, Treatment #2 had a 301% lift in revenue at a valid 97.4% level of confidence. Not only did the overall donor goal motivate more people to give, but it motivated them to give more than the control.
In future experimentation, we should continue to incorporate donor goals into our fundraising campaigns. By setting specific and attainable donor goals, donors are more likely to feel that their contribution will make a difference and be more motivated to give. Additionally, breaking down the overall donor goal into smaller amounts can make the goal seem more achievable and encourage more donors to participate.
Overall, the success of this experiment demonstrates the importance of setting donor goals and providing donors with a clear understanding of how their contributions can make an impact. This strategy can be utilized in future fundraising efforts to drive donor engagement and increase donations for The Navigators.

experiments.title: Evaluating the Impact of Adding a One-Time Gift Option to a Recurring Gift Slide-Up on Transaction Volume
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a one-time gift option to the slide-up will lead to more transactions overall, while maintaining a similar number of sustaining gifts. This approach is designed to attract individuals who may not be ready to commit to a sustaining gift, while still capturing those who were already inclined to make a sustaining contribution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2025 - 02/14/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, during their February Fund Drive. We aimed to test two different slide-ups: one was a previous version that encouraged prospects to make a sustaining gift, while in the test version, we added an option for a one-time gift to see if it would result in more overall gifts, including sustaining contributions.
Everything else remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In conclusion, the results of this experiment indicate that the control version, which emphasized monthly sustaining donations, outperformed the treatment version, which included both one-time and sustaining gift options. The control version resulted in 53 gifts generating $1,510 in revenue, compared to the treatment version's 23 gifts and $595 in revenue.
Breaking down the results further:
The control version had 15 one-time gifts and 38 recurring gifts.The treatment version had 6 one-time gifts and 17 recurring gifts.The control version also saw more first-time gifts, with 35 first-time gifts compared to the treatment's 13.
A key finding was that the control version resulted in a 58% higher donation volume for all traffic compared to the treatment version, with 100% statistical confidence. This suggests that focusing solely on sustaining donations was more effective in driving contributions, as the addition of a one-time gift option did not increase the total number of donations.
Additionally, four sustainers made another one-time donation, and all of them donated through the control version.
Moving forward, it would be beneficial to continue emphasizing the importance and impact of monthly sustaining gifts, as this approach appears to resonate more effectively with prospects. Further testing could explore different messaging strategies and donation options to identify the most effective combination for future fundraising campaigns. These insights will be key to optimizing WUNC's approach to maximizing donations in the future.

experiments.title: Will having a dynamic sticky bar for Giving Tuesday convert more donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a rotating message in the sticky bar for the Giving Tuesday homepage sticky bar element will achieve a higher donor conversion because the rotating message is eye catching and can attract more attention to the space rather than having a static wiki.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
For CYE 2024, we wanted to test a different approach to have on the homepage for Giving Tuesday, which in turn would inform the control that we would need to have for the next test.
For this test, we wanted to see if having a sticky bar with a rotating message would increase donor conversion, rather than having a static sticky bar with a donor countdown. This test would only show on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. **Rotating Message in the Sticky Bar Increases Donor Conversion**: The experiment showed that having a rotating message in the sticky bar on the Giving Tuesday homepage led to a significant increase in donor conversion. The rotating message was found to be eye-catching and attracted more attention, potentially enticing more donors to give.

2. **Statistical Significance**: The 88.4% increase in donations for all traffic with a 95.6% statistical level of confidence indicates that the results are reliable and not due to chance. This means that the observed impact of the rotating message is likely to hold true in future instances.

3. **Value Proposition Variation**: The rotating message allowed for different value propositions to be presented continually, compared to a static message. This variety in messaging may have catered to a broader range of donor preferences and motivations, leading to increased conversions.

4. **Future Experimentation Use**: Based on these findings, in future experimentation, incorporating rotating messages in sticky bars could be considered for other campaigns or donation drives. This dynamic approach could continue to engage donors and potentially boost conversions in different contexts as well.

In conclusion, the experiment successfully demonstrated the effectiveness of using a rotating message in the sticky bar for the Giving Tuesday homepage. By leveraging this insight and implementing rotating messages strategically in future experiments, Moody Radio can continue to optimize donor conversions and engagement on its website.

 

Along with having a valid increase in donations, we also saw an increase of about $1,500 in revenue. While the increase was not as drastic as that of Radio and Edu.

CaringBridge has the option for authors to set up a gift card so that people visiting their page can send the author a gift card as a way to help. For this test, we tried adding a tooltip that focused on what the user should do and specifying what the option is used for.

The treatment group experienced a 363.9% increase in Save button clicked for gift cards on the What They Heard page for all traffic, with a statistical significance level of 100.0%.
Furthermore, the Tooltip CTA button clicked also saw a significant increase of 1,383.8% with 100% statistical confidence. This indicates that the tooltip providing clarity on what the user should do helped reduce confusion and encourage more users to set up gift cards.
In future experiments, it is important to continue emphasizing the importance of providing clear instructions and guidance for users to navigate the website effectively. This includes using tooltips or other visual cues to assist users in understanding the steps they need to take. Additionally, the success of this experiment highlights the value of testing different strategies for improving user experience and conversion rates. By continuing to experiment and iterate on website design and functionalities, we can further optimize the user experience and achieve better results.

experiments.title: How an identity call to action on an exit intent impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an identity CTA “Yes, I can be one of the 3 donors” for users encountering the exit-intent during a 2-day match will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it empowers the donor with autonomy to answer the question through the CTA “Yes” and emphasizing identity rather than action “I can be one of the donors”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we tested a variety of identity CTA’s throughout the campaign. In this particular experiment, we tested a CTA on the 2-day match exit intent “Yes, I can be one of the 3 donors.” On the Tribute Widget, we saw a similar autonomy CTA emphasizing that the reader was willing to give $25 significantly improved the conversion rate. In this iteration, the language focused more on donor identity ” I can be one of the donors” rather than focusing on a giving amount. We believe this autonomy and donor identity treatment will increase conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the identity CTA "Yes, I can be one of the 3 donors" for users encountering the exit-intent during a 2-day match did not achieve an increase in donor conversion rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment actually saw a 39.0% decrease in donations for all traffic with a high level of statistical significance.

This outcome suggests that in this particular context, emphasizing donor identity and autonomy through the CTA was not as effective in converting donors compared to other approaches. Throughout the holiday appeal, autonomy and identity language required specific parameters in order to be effective; working well in some instances but decreasing results in others. In the future, it would be worthwhile to determine the variable and discover the commonality between when autonomy succeeds and when it detracts from CaringBridge's audience.

Overall, it is important to take away from this experiment that not all hypotheses will yield positive results, and it is essential to continually test and refine strategies based on data and insights gathered from experiments.

Following the success of another sticky bar test during a campaign, we wanted to know if we would see like results during calendar year-end.
Previously, we saw a great response when added an additional sticky bar alert message across the top of the donation page that included a dynamic daily donor countdown and the specific date and time we needed to reach our campaign goal.

2. The treatment group experienced a substantial 69.7% increase in Revenue for All Traffic compared to the control group.

3. The statistical significance level of 99.5% also indicates that the results are highly reliable and not due to chance.

Implications for Future Experimentation:

1. Highlighting a specific need and deadline to reach a campaign goal can effectively motivate donors to take action.

2. Using dynamic elements such as daily donor countdowns can create a sense of urgency and encourage donors to contribute.

3. Future experiments should consider incorporating similar strategies to drive donor conversion and revenue growth.

4. It is essential to continue testing and iterating on different messaging and design elements to optimize donation pages for maximum impact.

Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of clear communication, urgency, and specific goals in fundraising campaigns. By leveraging these learnings in future experimentation, this organization can continue to improve its donor conversion rates and ultimately achieve its campaign objectives more effectively.

experiments.title: Does a sticky bar on premium pages about a match impact donation conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a sticky bar mentioning the match for web traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because the match has shown to be a driver of response.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/25/2024 - 12/06/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas
During June and December PTV does match campaigns. December 2023 we tested a sticky bar on instant donation pages for content offers mentioning the match and that any gift will have double impact.
So this year we decided to test the same sticky bar but on premium book donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar mentioning the match for web traffic on premium book donation pages did not lead to a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 6.9% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control group, with a 95.6% statistical level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the context and placement of messaging about matching campaigns. It may be more effective to focus on other strategies or channels to promote matching campaigns, rather than relying solely on sticky bars on donation pages. Additionally, it is crucial to continue testing different approaches and analyzing results to better understand what resonates with donors and drives conversions.

experiments.title: How a slide-up with gift array vs sticky bar impacts clicks to the donation page during a high-urgency direct appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a slide-up with a gift array for users on Luther’s Working Preacher site will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2024 - 10/17/2024
Luther Seminary was running a high-urgency campaign to support their Working Preacher site, and one of the web elements on the site was a standard direct appeal sticky bar. We wanted to test whether using a slide-up element instead of a sticky bar would drive more user engagement through clicks to the donation page.
The sticky bar and slide-up both used the same language, but the slide-up presented users with 3 gift options. Clicking a gift option would direct users to the donation page and the form would be pre-filled with their selected gift amount.
Our hypothesis was that the slide-up would drive an increase in clicks to the donation page because it lets users begin the process of giving before they even land on the donation page. We also hoped that this would result in a downstream lift in donations from the slide-up.
We split the website traffic 50/50 between the two variants, and the test ran for ~3 days before we ran out of testing credits and had to end it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a slide-up with a gift array on Luther Seminary's Working Preacher site led to a significant increase in clicks to the donation page compared to a standard sticky bar. The treatment resulted in a 43.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with a high statistical level of confidence (99.5%)!
We also found that the slide-up led to a 33.4% increase in donor conversion, but this was only with a 68.3% level of confidence.
From this test we learned that it is important to continue exploring different ways to engage Working Preacher users and streamline the donation process for them. It is clear that providing users with the option to select a gift amount before landing on the donation page can be effective in driving more clicks and potentially increasing donations. For future testing, we want to experiment with the language in the slide-up, as well as the psychology of price anchoring with our gift array amounts.

experiments.title: What Drives More Urgency A Popup or a Wikipedia-style banner?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that switching the style of an ask for the article pages will achieve a higher conversion rate because the new element will draw more attention to it because users have not seen it before.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2024 - 07/02/2024
An organization has been using wiki bars on their article pages for several high urgency campaigns. After an element has been present for a while on the page “banner blindness” can become present and the element might catch the eye of the users for them to take action.
For this test we wanted to see if switching the element on the article pages could increase transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that switching the style of an ask for the article pages actually led to a decrease in transactions. Despite the hypothesis that the new element would draw more attention due to novelty, the treatment group experienced a 38.4% decrease in Transactions - One-Time for All Traffic compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 96.2% confidence level, indicating a high level of certainty.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of changing elements on the page, especially when it comes to high urgency campaigns like the ones run by this organization. It is crucial to balance the need for catching the users' attention with the risk of causing confusion or decreased engagement. Additionally, it is essential to continually monitor and evaluate the performance of different elements to ensure that any changes made are positively impacting the desired outcomes.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing assumptions and continuously learning from the results to inform and improve future strategies and campaigns.

experiments.title: How a slide-up with gift array vs an exit-intent impacts donor conversion during high-urgency appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that slide-up with gift array for users on profile sites during the 2-day match appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because it allows the users to already begin the donation process, by selecting an amount, before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/24/2024 - 09/26/2024
CaringBridge was running a 2-day high-urgency match appeal. Historically, this has been communicated using an exit-intent pop-up that informs users about how many donors are needed as well as that the match will double their impact. We hypothesized that a slide-up with a gift array of three amounts would ultimately be more eye-catching, empower the user with more options up front, and increase revenue by ensuring that whatever amount they selected would be pre-filled on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that utilizing a slide-up with a gift array during high-urgency match appeals can significantly increase donor conversion rates and revenue. In this particular case, the treatment resulted in a 45.5% increase in Donations - Non-Tribute for All Traffic with a high level of statistical confidence, indicating that the new approach was successful in prompting more users to donate.
Moving forward, these learnings should be applied to future experimentation by considering the effectiveness of providing users with donation options upfront, allowing them to start the donation process before reaching the actual donation page. This approach not only streamlines the donation process for users but also serves as a powerful motivator for them to contribute, particularly during time-sensitive campaigns like match appeals.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of user experience and the potential impact of small changes in the donation journey. It is essential to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to optimize fundraising efforts and ultimately drive more support for the organization's mission.
In the future, we would like to re-test this experience during an evergreen phase, outside of the 2-day match appeal to see its impact on donors and revenue.

experiments.title: How a slide out with educational copy increased site follows
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an educational slide out for visitors on an author page will achieve an increase in site follows because it not only draws more attention, but includes copy that addresses the value of following.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2024 - 08/28/2024
To improve site follows, CaringBridge had tested into a Sticky Follow button that kept this CTA pinned to the top of the screen if a user scrolled down. While this resulted in increased follows due to the greater visibility, we hypothesized that a second iteration that called attention to this CTA while also including educational copy around “why” a user should follow would increase this metric even more.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing an educational slide out on the author page led to a significant increase in site follows. The treatment resulted in a 151.1% increase in Site Follows for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating a strong impact on user behavior.

What this indicates is that although increasing visibility was effective, the real question that needed to be addressed for users was why they should follow in the first place. By including copy that emphasizes the follow as a way to stay updated and receive important updates on the user's health journey, more users were likely to follow.

experiments.title: How a pop-up inviting one-time donors to convert their donation to recurring impacts recurring donor conversion on donation pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup inviting one-time donors to convert their one-time donation to a recurring gift will have a more significant impact and will increase recurring giving by making the value proposition of a recurring gift more appealing and transparent to the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2024 - 07/31/2024
This organization uses a tabbed donation form to enable donors to select either a one-time or a recurring donation. Last year, they tested adding a callout to the recurring tab to highlight the greater impact of becoming a recurring donor. This validated an increase in recurring giving and became a part of all their donation pages.
We hypothesized that we could further increase recurring giving on the page by firing a popup when someone clicks the donate button on the form. This popup would invite donors to give a smaller recurring gift to have a bigger impact than the higher one-time donation they had selected. The form would automatically ask for a gift that was 75% of the value of the one-time donation originally chosen.
We ran this experiment on instant donation pages and the donation pages of their one-time donor-focused July Campaign. Traffic was split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The popup inviting one-time donors to convert their donation to a recurring gift significantly increased recurring donations by 129.4% for all traffic with a high level of statistical confidence (99.6%). This indicates that emphasizing the value proposition of recurring donations can effectively incentivize donors to choose ongoing giving.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring ways to highlight the benefits of recurring donations and nudge donors toward choosing this option. Additionally, leveraging popups strategically to interrupt the donation process and present compelling offers can be a powerful tool in increasing donor engagement and conversion rates.

Most importantly, we found that the popup had no statistically significant impact on overall donor conversion, so we were not sacrificing one-time donations by running the popup. Because of the significantly higher retention rate and lifetime value of recurring donors, this increase in recurring giving could have significant impacts on donor revenue for this organization.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar on instant donation pages impacted gift conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a sticky bar for donation pages following acquisition offers will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is added need in that such a small amount of people that receive free resources donate and that there is appeal because of the match.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2023 - 12/20/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
For calendar year end, we decided to test adding a sticky bar to donation pages for acquisition offers to see if it would increase conversions and revenue. The copy for the sticky bar mentioned that only 1% of people who request resources donate to PTV. We also mentioned the CYE match that is in place.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar to donation pages following acquisition offers significantly increased the conversion rate and revenue. The treatment group saw a 50.0% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, indicating that the sticky bar was successful in encouraging more visitors to donate.

Furthermore, the statistical levels of confidence were high, with a 98.0% confidence level for the increase in Transactions for All Traffic, a 97% confidence level for the increase in Revenue, a 97% confidence level for the increase in Transactions, and a 100% confidence level for the increase in Revenue for All Traffic.

Based on these results, it is evident that the sticky bar effectively conveyed the message that only 1% of people who request resources donate to PTV, creating a sense of urgency and the desire to contribute. Additionally, the mention of the CYE match likely provided an added incentive for visitors to donate.

In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue using sticky bars on donation pages following acquisition offers, as it has proven to be a successful strategy in increasing conversions and revenue. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other ways to communicate the low donation rate and the impact of the CYE match to further motivate visitors to take action.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of targeted messaging and the potential impact of utilizing persuasive elements in fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity in copy for a sticky bar impacts click through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the messaging and call-to-action in the sticky bar from a generic request for feedback to a more specific and personalized invitation to take a survey about the user’s learning experience will result in a higher click through rate because it is a more tangible and manageable ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2023 - 11/26/2023
Dallas Theological Seminary had a survey that they wanted to advertise on their website to encourage people to provide feedback on DTS free online courses. Previously, they had tested the angle of acknowledging that the person reading the sticky bar was a friend of DTS that became the control.
For this test, we wanted to see if that angle was more impactful or if providing clarity on the length of the survey and extending a specific invitation to the person reading the sticky bar would lead to more clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing a specific and personalized invitation to take a survey resulted in a significantly higher click-through rate compared to a generic request for feedback. The treatment version, which used more specific and tangible language, saw a 343.2% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% level of confidence.

Based on these results, it is recommended to use the learnings from this experiment in future experimentation. When designing sticky bars or any other call-to-action messaging, it would be beneficial to use specific and personalized language that clearly communicates the purpose of the action and the value it provides to the user. This approach can increase engagement and participation rates. Additionally, it is important to continue testing and iterating on messaging to optimize click-through rates and other desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How a Sticky Follow Button Impacts Site Follows
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky follow button that “pins” to the top of the page for users on a site profile will achieve an increase in site follows because it draws more attention to the button visually, but also highlights this as a primary call-to-action.
experiments.summary:

On CaringBridge site profiles, we wanted to re-test a sticky follow button that stayed pinned to the page when a user scrolled down. This had previously been tested in Caring Bridge 1.o, but not yet in Caring Bridge 2.0.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a sticky follow button that stays pinned to the top of the page for users on a site profile led to a significant increase in site follows. The treatment version, which included the sticky follow button resulted in a 14.7% increase in Site Follows for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

A takeaway from this experiment is more than just making an element more visually apparent, but what that communicates to the reader. Of all the actions that can be taken on a site profile, we're highlighting this is one that's important.

This strategy can be applied to other features or buttons on the site to capture users' attention and encourage them to take the desired actions. Additionally, it is important to continue testing and iterating on design elements, language choices, and user experience to optimize engagement and achieve the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How a slide out for a disaster appeal impacts donor conversion for website visitors compared with a sticky bar appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky donate button on the website with a slide-out disaster appeal CTA will increase donor conversion compared with a sticky bar appeal by reducing donor friction and increasing the urgency of making a gift during a natural disaster
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2023 - 09/23/2023
CBN’s Operation Blessing provides critical disaster relief supplies to communities devastated by natural disasters. Typically, when a disaster strikes, they place an urgent donation appeal sticky bar message on their website to promote giving. They team wanted to see if this was the most effective tactic for increasing donor conversion during disasters.
We hypothesized that the sticky bar could easily be missed due to “banner blindness” on the site, especially with how busy their pages were with many CTAs, images, etc. We decided to test a sticky donate button that would be on the right side of the screen and slide out a disaster relief appeal message with “yes” “no” buttons. We believed this would reduce friction and increase the urgency of the disaster appeal, leading to higher donor conversion.
The control message read, “Give today to help provide disaster relief for those affected by Hurricane Idalia.”
The treatment message read, “Hi there, can you take 30-seconds to provide clean water, hot food, and critical supplies to those impacted by Hurricane Idalia? 100% of your gift goes to meeting their needs.” It would slide out automatically after the user was on the page for 7 seconds. We ran the experiment for 5 days with traffic split evenly between both experiences.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a sticky donate button with a slide-out disaster appeal message significantly increases donor conversion compared to a sticky bar appeal. The treatment message, which included a more personalized and urgent appeal, resulted in a 39.4% increase in donations with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

When digging into results by segment, every single segment showed significant donor preference for the sticky button vs control.

Here are results with level of confidence (LOC) by segment:
Mobile: 56% increased donor conversion at a 100% LOC (145 vs 93 donations)
 	Desktop: 33% increased donor conversion at a 99% LOC (162 vs 123 donations)
 	New visitors: 20% increased donor conversion at a 94% LOC (146 vs 122 donations)
 	Returning visitors: 60% increased donor conversion at a 100% LOC (172 vs 107 donations)
