experiments.title: How a donation interrupter impacts recurring giving during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the recurring interrupter for Eagle Lake Donation Page donors will achieve an increase in recurring giving because the donor is asked to give less upfront but have a larger impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2024 - 05/17/2024
Every year, The Navigators run two high-urgency campaigns focused on raising money for their Christ-centered summer camp called Eagle Lake. We have been actively testing ways to activate their donor file to monthly sustainers, and we wanted to bring these efforts into their Eagle Lake Campaign.
This interrupter only showed to half of the people who selected a one-time gift under $100 on one of their Eagle Lake donation pages.
The copy asked the visitor to convert their gift to a $10 monthly gift in support of Eagle Lake efforts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that the addition of the interrupter may have had a positive impact on converting The Navigators audience to become monthly sustainers.
A total of 120 donors were shown the interrupter and out of those 8 donors clicked "Yes" to convert their gift to a $10 monthly gift.
That is 6.67% conversion rate!
While we are unable to validate these results, it is encouraging to see almost 7% of donors willing to commit to a monthly gift and become a Sustainer.
We plan to continue testing the presence of the interrupter in an effort to create a larger Sustainers file for The Navigators.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different approaches to increase recurring giving, such as optimizing the messaging and design of the interrupter, as well as testing different donation amounts and frequencies. Additionally, it is beneficial to analyze the donor file to identify specific segments that are more likely to convert to monthly sustainers and tailor the interrupter messaging accordingly. By continuing to test and optimize these strategies, nonprofits like The Navigators can effectively increase their recurring giving and generate more sustainable revenue for their programs such as the Eagle Lake summer camp.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and impact to the homepage slide impacts user engagement and donor conversion metrics

rows.name: Treatment - Praying the Names and Attibutes

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that utilizing a new content offer for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in visitor engagement on the homepage as well as donor conversion metrics because the additional clarity and impact of the content offer will garner more attention from visitors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2024 - 07/17/2024
The Navigators homepage gets a lot of organic traffic. Therefore, we wanted to use this as leverage for the acquisition of new emails and donors to the organization.
The control homepage offered little to no detail about what resource was being offered in the header image. So we wanted to test how adding clarity around what the offer is and the impact the offer will have in an individual’s life would affect click-through rate and other conversion metrics.
Additionally, we wanted to unlock which offer The Navigators audience would engage with best.
We ran an A/B/C split test with the variates based on content offers:
Version A – Control – Discipleship 101
Version B – Praying the Names & Attributes of God eBook
Version C – How to Memorize Scripture eBook
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B/C test conducted on The Navigators' homepage with three different content offers - Discipleship 101, Praying the Names & Attributes of God, and How to Memorize Scripture - resulted in an impactful outcome.

Treatment 1, which featured the content offer "Praying the Names & Attributes of God," showed a significant increase in visitor engagement and donor conversion metrics. Specifically, there was a 124.8% increase in Hero CTA button clicks for All Traffic at a 99.9% level of confidence. As well as a 254.1% lift in Hero Button Clicks on Treatment 2 which featured "How to Memorize Scripture: with a level of confidence of 99.9%. 

These results indicate that both treatment versions outperformed the control in terms of Hero Button Clicks 99.9% of the time. 

We also wanted to see what the downstream revenue looked like for these offers. We used new appeal codes for this test so that we could accurately pull these results.

experiments.title: How a sticky slideout on content pages affects overall donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional opportunity to give for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donations because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2023 - 11/15/2023
The Navigators wanted to take advantage of the traffic generated through their resource and blog pages. As people are being equipped/informed, we wanted to run a test where people would see a sticky donate button on the side that would prompt people to give. The treatment would slide out when someone scrolled halfway through the page. If they click “yes” they would be taken to a donation page to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The addition of a sticky donate button on the side of the resource and blog pages resulted in a significant increase in donations. The treatment group experienced a 36.5% increase in transactions compared to the control group, with a 99.7% level of confidence. This suggests that the prompt to give, along with the value proposition language, was effective in persuading visitors to make a donation.
2. The Sticky Donate Click rate increased by 10,3203% with a 100% level of confidence. This indicated that the presence of the sticky donate button garnered more attention and engagement from site visitors.
3. The Sticky Donate-Yes Button Click rate saw a 17% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that visitors were receptive to the call-to-action and were willing to proceed to the donation page.
4. Surprisingly, the Sticky Donate-No Button Click rate experienced a significant increase of 74,8344% with a 100% level of confidence. This may indicate that even if visitors chose not to donate, they were more engaged and actively responding to the prompt.
Based on these findings, it is evident that the addition of a sticky donate button can be an effective strategy to increase donations. The value proposition language used in the prompt seems to have resonated with site visitors and motivated them to support The Navigators.

experiments.title: How an “Abandoned Cart” Pop-Up onsite Impacts Monthly Giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an abandoned cart pop-up for users who had begun making a gift on the main donation page but abandoned the process will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it will help recapture traffic that would have otherwise left their gift incomplete.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2024 - 05/20/2024
To increase conversion on NPH’s main donation page, we hypothesized that an abandoned cart pop-up would help recapture donors who had arrived on the main donate, started making a gift, but abandoned the process for one reason or another. If the user navigated to a different portion of the site, or simply X’d out and returned at a later time, they would receive a prompt that asked them to finish making their donation. The pop-up was set to dynamically pull the gift amount the user had previously selected as well as the frequency, allowing them to resume where they had left off.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing an abandoned cart pop-up for users who had started making a gift on the main donation page but abandoned the process resulted in an increase in Recurring Donation for All Traffic. This increase was statistically significant at a 97.5% confidence level, indicating that the treatment was effective in recapturing potential donors and encouraging them to complete their donation.

Although the control and treatment had a similar donor conversion rate when considering transactions overall, this experiment reveals that it was most impactful at recapturing recurring donations that would've otherwise been lost. There are many things to consider when making a monthly commitment. A user may have to reflect on what their budget allows them to commit to each month or the best day of the month for it to come out. It's likely that any of these could be why users were originally abandoning the monthly donation before committing. Have that reinforced with a pop-up and made simple by carrying over the original amount and frequency was the prompt they needed to complete the transaction.

experiments.title: How yes/no “autonomy” language on a pop-up impacts conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2024 - 04/27/2024
For The Navigators April Eagle Lake High Urgency campaign, we wanted to test using two buttons, both a yes and no option, on their homepage overlay/pop-up.
We hypothesized that providing the visitor with a micro-decision would lead to an increase in the donor conversion rate.
The control features the standard single call-to-action. Whereas, the treatment offers the call-to-action to donate as well as a “No, I’m not interested option.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing visitors with a choice between a yes and no option on the homepage overlay significantly increased the donor conversion rate for The Navigators April Eagle Lake High Urgency campaign. The treatment, which featured both options, resulted in a 193.8% increase in donations compared to the control, with a statistical level of confidence of 97.2%. This demonstrates that giving visitors the autonomy to make a micro-decision before being asked for a donation can positively impact conversion rates.
In future experimentation, The Navigators should continue to test and implement strategies that offer visitors a choice or a sense of control in the donation process. This can include using two buttons with different options, adjusting language to frame the call-to-action as a choice rather than a directive, or incorporating interactive elements that engage visitors in decision-making. By leveraging the learnings from this experiment, The Navigators can optimize their donation process and ultimately drive higher conversion rates and engagement with their campaigns.

experiments.title: How a micro yes on a sticky slide out on article pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to the article pages sticky donate button for visitors reading the article pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the copy has incentive for somebody to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/30/2023
Focus on the Family is a well-known Christian ministry that receives tons of traffic to their article pages to guide people on a variety of different topics. For this test we wanted to see if we added value proposition to the sticky donate on the side of the page would urge people to give.
The copy we used was specifically to increase the urgency and get a website visitor to agree to a ‘micro-yes’.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value proposition to the sticky donate button on the article pages had a negative impact on donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which saw the value proposition copy, had a 35.9% decrease in donations compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant with a confidence level of 98.3%.

This specific ask was for a visitor to join Friends of the Family, which is their monthly sustainer program. It's important to note that for recurring gifts the treatment had 9 donors, and the control had 10. While it was still the losing variant, it did perform very closely to the control, whereas one-time gifts were significantly less (63 to 38).

Based on these findings, it is clear that the copy used in the value proposition did not effectively motivate visitors to make a donation. It is important to reassess the messaging and value proposition used in future experiments to ensure that it resonates with the target audience and encourages them to take the desired action.

Future experimentation should focus on identifying and testing different value propositions that can effectively incentivize visitors to donate. This could involve conducting user research to better understand the motivations and needs of the target audience, and then crafting messaging that speaks directly to those motivations. It would also be beneficial to test different placements or formats for the donate button to optimize its visibility and impact. By continually testing and iterating on the value proposition and donation messaging, Focus on the Family can work towards achieving a higher donor conversion rate on their article pages.

experiments.title: How making the Follow Site button “sticky” so that it stays onscreen impacts site follows
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing visibility of the Follow button by making ti sticky for users on a CaringBridge profile will achieve an increase in site follows because it not only means they’re more likely to see it, but it highlights this feature as important.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2024 - 02/13/2024
For CaringBridge, site follows on a user profile is an important metric. It not only keeps friends and family better connected, it means they’re more likely to see the user’s posts and encounter key asks to support the site. In a previous iteration, we believed that highlighting this Follow button in the top right-hand corner with a bright orange color would make it stand out and more. This resulted in a huge increase in follows. For this iteration of the test, we hypothesized that taking this a step further and keeping the Follow Site button “sticky” so that when a user scrolls down the profile it stays in view on the right side-bar, would also increase visibility and site follows as a result.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

Making the Follow button sticky on a user's profile led to a significant increase in site followers. The treatment group experienced a 42.8% increase in Site Follows for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating a clear win for the treatment.

From this experiment, we learned the importance of increasing the visibility of key features such as the Follow button to drive user engagement. This should prompt future ideas about key metrics and how if we want a user to take them, we should make it visible and easy to do so. Exploring other potential sticky elements on the site that could enhance user experience and drive key metrics.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar affects donor conversion rates on a CYE donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a humanized sticky bar on a CYE donation page for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it provides more urgency and a special message from a well-known figure at the organization. because  it provides more urgency and a special message from a well-known figure at the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2023 - 12/31/2023
ACI ran their annual calendar year-end campaign. In the final four emails of the campaign, they wanted to test how an urgent, personalized sticky bar signed by Dr. Ben Carson would perform. Typically, they see higher conversion through funnels and campaigns when messaging is directly from Dr. Carson, so we expected an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a personalized sticky bar signed by Dr. Ben Carson did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version of the email had a 19.3% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control version, with a 95.1% level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment version experienced a significant 237% decrease in One-Time Donations with a 98% level of confidence.
These results suggest that the personalized sticky bar did not create the urgency and special message that was expected. Instead, it may have had a negative impact on donor conversion.
To use this learning in future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and language used in fundraising campaigns. While using a well-known figure like Dr. Ben Carson may normally result in higher conversion rates, it is crucial to ensure that the messaging aligns with the organization's goals and resonates with potential donors. Additionally, it is important to conduct thorough testing and analysis before implementing new strategies to minimize potential negative impacts on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How an exit intent pop up affects email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup offering a free ebook for users exiting a page that advertises a for-purchase item will achieve an increased acquisition of email because we believe that targeting the same motivation (scripture memory) at a free price point will acquire those who were exiting because of the price.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/21/2023 - 03/30/2023
The Navigators’ Topical Memory System is a for-purchase book that helps the reader memorize scripture. The landing page for this offer briefly outlines what the TMS is, important points, and an ask at the end to purchase the book. We hypothesized that by adding an exit intent pop-up on the page for a free eBook on a similar topic (How to Memorize Scripture) would not interfere with those who are purchasing the TMS book, but would acquire the emails of those who were planning to exit the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering a free ebook with an exit-intent popup can significantly increase email acquisition. The treatment group saw a 147.9% increase in email acquisition for all traffic, and new visitors, organic search visitors, desktop visitors, social visitors, male visitors, and visitors aged 18-24 saw substantial increases in email acquisition with a high level of confidence.
What does this tell us about users exiting the page? The eBook popup is about the same topic as the TMS page—but where the TMS page is urging users to make a purchase, the popup entices them with a similar offer free of cost. Because this popup is only setup to appear when the user goes to exit the page, this wouldn't detract from those who were going to purchase the book. What it does accomplish is allow The Navigators to acquire the email of someone who would've exited the page and been lost.

experiments.title: How Yes/No “Autonomy” Language Impacts Clicks on a CYE Exit Intent
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that yes/no autonomy language for users on a CaringBridge visit profile will achieve increase in click-thru because users are more likely to interact with a call-to-action that presents an easy “micro-yes.”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/11/2024
For CaringBridge’s CYE campaign, we hypothesized that Yes/No language on the call-to-action buttons would improve click-thru rate and send more traffic to the donation page. In the control, the call-to-action was “Donate Now” whereas in the treatment users were given a choice: “yes, I would like to give,” or “no, I’m not interested.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the hypothesis that yes/no autonomy language on the call-to-action buttons would improve click-thru rate and send more traffic to the donation page was proved to be incorrect. The treatment, which implemented the yes/no autonomy language, actually resulted in a 24.2% decrease in clicks for all traffic compared to the control.
This suggests that users were not more likely to interact with language that presents an easy "micro-yes" before being taken to the main donation page. In fact, it seems that the yes/no autonomy language may have confused or deterred users from clicking on the call-to-action buttons.
It should be noted that donor conversion rate wasn't captured for this experiment. It would be interesting to see the impacts downstream, whether less people making the commitment to "Yes, I would like to give" would still result in more conversions at the donation due to those users being more committed/motivated to give due to the micro-yes.

experiments.title: How a Humanized Sticky Bar on a CYE Donation Page Impacts Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar for prospective donors on the CYE donation page will achieve an increase in conversion rate because donors are more likely identify with and give to a person.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/04/2024
For RTB’s CYE Donation Page, we hypothesized that a humanized sticky bar treatment featuring an image of RTB’s President Fazale Rana would increase conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the humanized sticky bar treatment featuring an image of RTB's President Fazale Rana did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing conversion rate. Instead, it resulted in a significant decrease in donations.
This result was unexpected since RTB has used Humanized Sticky Bar of Fazale on the donation page to great success in the past. One change for consideration is in the language between the two sticky bars. In addition to having an image of Fuz, the treatment does change the statement from being one of "we" to "I."
It is possible that this ultimately led the mission to sound more indvidual/Fazale focused rather than broadening to greater impact.

experiments.title: How a sticky slideout on content pages affects overall donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional opportunity to give for site visitors will achieve an increase in donations because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language. because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language. because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language. because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2023 - 11/15/2023
The Navigators wanted to take advantage of the traffic generated through their resource and blog pages. As people are being equipped/informed, we wanted to run a test where people would see a sticky donate button on the side that would prompt people to give. The treatment would slide out when someone scrolled halfway through the page. If they click “yes” they would be taken to a donation page to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The addition of a sticky donate button on the side of the resource and blog pages resulted in a significant increase in donations. The treatment group experienced a 36.5% increase in transactions compared to the control group, with a 99.7% level of confidence. This suggests that the prompt to give, along with the value proposition language, was effective in persuading visitors to make a donation.

2. The Sticky Donate Click rate increased by 10,3203% with a 100% level of confidence. This indicated that the presence of the sticky donate button garnered more attention and engagement from site visitors.

3. The Sticky Donate-Yes Button Click rate saw a 17% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that visitors were receptive to the call-to-action and were willing to proceed to the donation page.

4. Surprisingly, the Sticky Donate-No Button Click rate experienced a significant increase of 74,8344% with a 100% level of confidence. This may indicate that even if visitors chose not to donate, they were more engaged and actively responding to the prompt.

Based on these findings, it is evident that the addition of a sticky donate button can be an effective strategy to increase donations. The value proposition language used in the prompt seems to have resonated with site visitors and motivated them to support The Navigators.

experiments.title: How an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar with a small donation CTA and opt-in for an email reminder impacts donor conversion during a Calendar Year-End Campaign

rows.name: Dear reader urgency with Scrolling Sticky Bar

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar reminding visitors of the value they’ve received from this organization’s articles will increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and exclusivity of the offer
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2022 - 12/25/2022
Historically, this organization used a combination of sticky bars and slide-outs with messaging around the urgent need of helping youth experiencing homelessness or housing insecurity  and have tied this urgency back to either a donor goal or a revenue goal. During their calendar year-end campaign, they often use a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown to convert visitors to their article pages into donors.
They wanted to test this against a design inspired by one of Wikipedia’s tests where there is an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar when scrolling. Instead of emphasizing the matching challenge in effect and the donor goal, the in-line appeal would ask the reader to consider the value they’ve received during the past year from their articles and tell them that less than 1% of readers donate to make these articles possible. Once the reader scrolled to continue reading the article, they would be prompted with the following, “If we have given you $5 worth of truth about housing insecurity this year, take a minute to donate.” They would then be asked for a gift of as little as $5 to “keep these exclusive articles possible to provide guidance to transform lives.”
If the visitor clicked the button to give, they would go to the donation page with the amount they selected. They could also click, “maybe later” which loaded an embedded HubSpot form that allowed them to opt-in for an email reminder to give. Those who opted in would receive an email reminder before the end of the campaign to make their gift.
Our hypothesis was that the conversational and value-driven copy on the in-line design and sticky bar, coupled with the option to opt-in for an email reminder would increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and exclusivity of the offer. We would also be tapping into the donor’s desire to not lose access to these articles by highlighting how few people give to make them possible.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new messaging and design crushed the control sticky bar with a daily donor goal. What is remarkable is that the avg gift was $29 despite the fact that the CTA was to make a $5 gift. This showed that readers felt that they were receiving much more value from the articles and content and wanted to make sure that it would still be available to them in the new year.

It's also worth noting that the dear reader/slide-up treatment brought in a $10,000 gift. We've pulled this out in the recording of this experiment so as not to skew the average gift or the revenue to give a more accurate picture of the overall results.

111 people opted in to receive follow-up emails to remind them about giving opportunities to this organization. We will be monitoring this segment to see downstream conversion as well as sending specific appeals to them reminding them that they asked to know more about ways to give at a later date.

We will continue to refine the messaging of this bar for future urgent campaigns as well as test into leveraging statistics of how few visitors/pledge signers/readers, etc donate to see if this messaging increases donor conversion on acquisition offer donation pages as well.

We also want to test the opening line, "we humbly ask you, don't scroll away" in other places to see if that conversational approach can improve conversion by keeping people on the page longer. For example, on an instant donation page after someone has requested an eBook or signed a pledge. Will that headline keep someone on the page long enough to read the donation appeal?

experiments.title: How a popup inviting donors to convert their onetime donation to a monthly gift at 60% of the value impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup inviting donors to convert their onetime donation to a monthly gift at 60% of the value of their donation will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2022 - 12/16/2022
An organization was looking for ways to increase their monthly donor acquisition on their website and wanted to test a popup that would fire the moment someone clicked the button to complete their one-time gift and offer them the opportunity to save even more lives by giving a monthly donation of 60% of the value of their one-time gift selection.
We hypothesized that this would attract donors because the gift amount would be less initially than what they’d already decided to donate, and this would offer this conversion to a monthly gift when donor motivation was the highest.
The traffic was evenly split, and this ran for approximately 10 days.
We believe that pop-ups inviting donors to convert their one-time donation to a monthly gift at 60% of the value of their donation will increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Contrary to our expectations, the popup decreased one-time donations and revenue (43.7% revenue reduction). It also failed to increase recurring giving which was relatively flat between variations. In reviewing the experiment, additional value proposition for why donors should become monthly donors was needed.

So, an additional 1-2 sentences in the popup explaining the benefit to the donor for making that switch. Also, in the experience, selecting the option to switch to a monthly gift would update the donor form with the new gift amount and select the checkbox for recurring, but the donor would still need to resubmit the donation. Having a checkbox may have added additional friction to the process.

 

Since we were testing this during year-end, when donors have high motivation, that friction alone could have stalled response on the page. We want to retest this popup in the new year outside of a high-urgency campaign to see if we can increase donor conversion by adding a stronger value proposition for converting a one-time gift to a recurring one.

experiments.title: How stating a specific goal in the headline of a pop-up for CYE fundraising affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the goal in the pop-up headline for website visitors will achieve a greater conversion rate of visitors to donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2022 - 12/23/2022
Our CYE fundraising strategy included a pop-up on the Libertas homepage with the goal of delivering the Tuttle Twins series to 200 public schools by raising $50,000. Most visitors to the site want to ensure that we are teaching children the value of liberty so we appealed to this desire with the headline “Help teach Kids the Value of Liberty by Making a Year-End Donation” which is clear and direct. However, we also know that a tangible goal appeals to donors, so we wanted to see if we stated the goal in the headline if it would lift response.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment pop-up saw a 4.3% lift in donor conversion rate with a 98.7% level of confidence. This experiment is only based on 6 total gifts, but due to this campaign being very time sensitive and the control pop-up receiving 0 gifts, we decided to roll out the treatment headline to 100% of traffic.

We believe the treatment headline won in this experiment because the goal gave donors something tangible to give to instead of a more broad topic like "teaching kids about liberty".

experiments.title: How an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar with a small donation CTA impacts donor conversion during a Fiscal Year End Campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar notifying visitors of the threat of the federal abortion hotline for Care Net’s Pregnancy Decision Line will increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and fear of loss.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2023 - 06/30/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
Historically, they’ve used a dear reader at the bottom of article pages with messaging around the urgent need in the pro-life space and have tied this urgency back to either a donor goal or a revenue goal. During their fiscal year-end campaign, they often use a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown to convert visitors to their article pages into donors.
They wanted to test this against a design inspired by one of Wikipedia’s tests where there is an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar when scrolling. The control and the treatment both had the same copy, but the placements were different: the control being at the end of the article and the treatment being at the beginning. On the treatment, once the reader scrolled to continue reading the article, the dear reader would then turn into a sticky bar with the same copy.
Our hypothesis was that the conversational and value-driven copy on the in-line design and sticky bar would increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and exclusivity of the offer. We would also be tapping into the donor’s desire to not have a federal, taxpayer-funded hotline.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The treatment, which consisted of an in-line appeal that became a sticky bar with the same copy when scrolling, performed significantly worse than the control. It resulted in a 70.3% decrease in donations for all traffic, indicating that this design approach did not effectively convert visitors into donors.
2. Different visitor segments showed even larger decreases in donations. Returning visitors had an 828% decrease. These significant drops in donation rates suggest that the in-line appeal with the fear of loss did not resonate well with these specific segments.
Based on these findings, it is clear that the Wikipedia "before article appeal" approach used in the treatment did not effectively appeal to visitors to become donors. The results suggest that a different messaging strategy should be tested in future experimentation.
Instead of relying solely on the placement, other emotional appeals or value-driven messaging could be considered. For example, highlighting how many more people can be reached through the Care Net Pregnancy Decision Line might be a more compelling appeal for potential donors, or showcasing how this new federal hotline could impact people being able to find life-affirming care.
Additionally, it may be worth exploring different placements for the appeal. While the treatment placed the messaging at the beginning of the article, future experiments could test different placements such as the end of the article, in a sidebar, or in a pop-up overlay.

experiments.title: How a Pop-Up on the Journal Page Increased Follows
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup with value copy for readers on a user’s page, but who do not follow them will achieve an increase in follows because the copy helps impart the importance of staying updating and how they can offer support by following.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2023 - 04/07/2023
CaringBridge is a platform where users can update friends and family about their health journey. In the journal view of a user’s page, CaringBridge wanted to capture users who were browsing the user’s entries but did not follow them.  We hypothesized that a simple popup with value copy about staying updating and informed on the user’s health journey would increase the number of follows.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a pop-up with value copy about staying updated and informed on the user's health journey can significantly increase the number of follows on a user's page. The treatment group saw a 138.8% increase in Follow Site (Journal Pages) for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This improvement was seen across all visitor types, including desktop, mobile, tablet, new, returning, direct, email, organic search, and paid search visitors. However, visitors aged 45-54 had a 221% decrease in follow site (journal pages) with a 97% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the age group that did not respond positively to the pop-up and adjust the messaging accordingly. Additionally, testing different variations of the pop-up, such as other types of value copy or presenting it at various points during the user's journey on the platform, could reveal even more effective ways to increase follows. Overall, the results of this experiment demonstrate the value of reminding users, not only that they weren't already following, but also the important of how their follow supports the user.

experiments.title: How a popup impacted email newsletter sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a email sign up slide out for website visitors will achieve a higher conversion of email sign ups because the opportunity is presented in a clearer fashion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/05/2023
WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio Station.  To convert web traffic to email subscribers, we tested a slide out with fields to sign up for the weekly What’s Trending Newsletter.
The existing, only email newsletter sign-up location is on the homepage after scrolling over half way down the page. No offers are available on other landing pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of an email sign up slide out can significantly increase the conversion of email sign ups. The treatment group had a 395.5% increase in emails acquired for all traffic compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant with 100.0% confidence.
For future experiments we will test into further clarity on the slide out, explaining what the subscriber is signing up both driving clarity and incentive.

experiments.title: How showing a recurring pop-up to users who are about to make a one-time donation affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a recurring pop-up to the instant donation page for petition signers will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because the donor will be able to make more of an impact with a monthly gift at potentially a lower cost.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/28/2023 - 05/21/2023
MSLF wanted to increase its amount of recurring donors. To test into increasing recurring donors coming in from petitions, they decided to add a recurring pop-up on the donation page once someone hits “donate now” at the bottom of the giving form.
This recurring pop-up calculated the new recurring ask based on how much their one-time gift was:
It will check if the one-time amount they give is less than $1000 then take 60% of the original amount rounded to the nearest $5.
If that is less than $20 then it will set it to $20.
If it’s more than $100, then it will set it to $100.
After they ran this experiment for one month, we found that the pop-up increased recurring giving by .13% with a 84.1% level of confidence. But we also found that it decreased one-time giving by 37% with an 87% level of confidence.
Due to the average gift in that experiment being $26.89, we believed the calculations of the pop-up caused the recurring ask to not be much less or the same as their original one-time gift which deterred them from giving at all.
We decided to retest this recurring pop-up with different calculations to see if the results would change:
Anybody who gave $100 or less, ask for a recurring gift. If they gave more than $100, do not show the pop-up.
$20 – $100 = 60% of what their gift was
$20 or less = Just ask for a recurring gift of whatever their gift amount was
We believe these calculations will better reflect what a recurring gift would look like in comparison to what a donor would give one-time.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The recurring pop-up caused one one-time donor to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift. There were no recurring donors from the control donation page.

Unfortunately, the recurring pop-up caused a 43.8% decrease in the overall conversion rate with a 96% level of confidence.

We believe this means that most people who were about to make a one-time donation left the page when presented with the option to turn their one-time gift into a recurring gift. The recurring pop-up added friction to the donation process.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/03/2022
MAF was running a high-urgency campaign for Giving Tuesday. Due to the previous testing of this concept in other high-urgency campaigns, we hypothesized that adding a sticky bar on the donation page itself reiterates the goal and would increase donor conversion. However, we also wanted to humanize this sticky bar by using Aaron Bear’s (CAO and Vice President) image and name so that it seems like it’s a message from Aaron himself. The added layer of using an authority figure within the organization is expected to increase our conversion rate as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, we saw a 49.8% lift in donor conversion rate with a 99% level of confidence when adding the humanized sticky bar to the campaign donation page. As hypothesized, we believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page reiterates the goal of the campaign and personalizes the ask since we are using the face of someone that works at MAF.

Moving forward, we will rollout a humanized sticky bar to all campaign donation pages.

Southern California Public Radio is a public radio station based out of Las Angeles. A popular newsletter that SCPR offers is “How to LA” which provides a daily morning email with current events, upcoming events, things to do, and anything someone may want to know about LA.
We have seen success with other organizations in testing personal-human invites for newsletter sign-ups or donations.

This experiment ran for 5 days and we saw a valid lift with a 35.8% increase in email acquisition with a 95.9% level of confidence.

Our next phases of testing will be in testing clarity around what the newsletter provides, length in copy, and CTA. Stay tuned!

experiments.title: How a pop up to prompt site follows performs against a full page interruption
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the full site interrupter for site visitors will achieve a decrease in both site creations and donations because the full purple curtain seems much more disruptive to the person’s site experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2023 - 04/29/2023
CaringBridge had previously implemented a full page, purple interrupter that prompts the user to follow the author’s site. We noticed a decrease in donations at the time and hypothesized that this new feature was the culprit. While the call to action on this interrupter is for site follows, it shows up on a page that generates a lot of gifts. To confirm our hypothesis we ran this as an A/B test where the control was the regular pop up for site follows and the treatment was the “purple curtain” pop up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted by Caringbridge revealed that the full page site interrupter for site visitors decreased the site follows by 16.5%. The statistical level of confidence was 100%, indicating that the control won. Moreover, the experiment resulted in a directional 64% increase in donations with a level of confidence of 87%. However, when drilling down to where these gifts are coming from we actually found that the donations had a split code that was completely unrelated to this test experience.
The key learning from this experiment is that full-site interrupters could be disruptive to user experience, leading to a reduction in site creation and potentially donations.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar for donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2022 - 12/16/2022
RTB was running a calendar year end campaign and in our efforts to increase donor conversion rate on the donation page, we decided to run a test where we would would humanize the experience for the prospective donor. So, we added a humanized sticky bar with their president’s picture and message so as to remind the prospective donor that they are giving to people and remind them of the impact of their gift.

Additionally, the reminder of the impact of their gift motivated more people to take action.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky bar to a year-end fundraising campaign donation page affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to the donation page of a year-end fundraising campaign will increase donation rates by humanizing and further conveying the value of the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2022 - 01/04/2023
During a year-end fundraising campaign for a faith-based education organization, we wanted to test how adding a humanized sticky bar to the donation page would affect donation rates.
We set this experiment up as a redirect test with ~50% of traffic seeing the standard donation page, sans sticky bar, and the other half seeing a humanized sticky bar from one of the organization’s staff members (including a photo) making a personal appeal and expressing the value of the prospect’s donation.
We ran this test for 10 days, during the last stretch of the fundraising campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 10 days, the treatment featuring a humanized sticky bar achieved a 14.5% increase in donation rates with an 87.1% statistical level of confidence when compared to the control.
In addition, the treatment including the sticky bar increased revenue per visitor by 151% with a 99% level of confidence, indicating that not only did the sticky bar encourage more people to give, it inspired them to give more generously.
We believe that including a sticky bar featuring a staff member of the organization was able to increase donations among page visitors by humanizing and putting a face to the appeal, reminding people that this organization is comprised of real people, just like them, who are trying to make a difference.
And even though we did not receive a large enough sample size to statistically validate these results, we did observe a directional lift that indicates there is low risk associated with implementing this treatment and we therefore feel confident in recommending the inclusion of a humanized sticky bar to 100% of the traffic during their next high-urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How adding impact language to a direct-ask pop up banner during a high urgency campaign affects revenue – marriages saved V1
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language on the gift array of a direct ask pop-up to show the impact of the gift will result in greater revenue
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/23/2022 - 12/02/2022
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They provide help and resources for couples to build healthy marriages that reflect God’s design and for parents to raise their children according to morals and values grounded in biblical principles. Every year during the calendar year-end high-urgency campaign, FOTF places a top-of-page pop-up banner that contains a direct ask to support their mission. This banner (known internally as the “wiki-banner”) has been tested into over many years, with experiments to improve donation metrics. Since many people visit FOTF.com during this season to make a year-end gift, the “wiki-banner” serves as a low-friction tactic to get people to the donation page quickly. It is important to note that the visiting audience to FOTF’s website is served different content / experiences based on pages they have consumed in the past…specifically a “marriage audience” and an “in favor of pro-life audience – Santicity of Human Life” (also known as SOHL).
For this specific experiment targeting the “marriage audience”, we were focused on changing the language on the gift array buttons to reflect the impact that a gift would make on the amount of marriages saved. The theory was that if we could directly show how many marriages could be saved with a specific gift amount, the visitor would be moved to give a bigger gift to make a greater impact. As screen real-estate is at a premium for this “wiki-banner”, we designed an experience that showed the number of marriages saved on a button on the wiki-banner, and in a “hover-state” it would flip to the equivalent dollar amount of the gift. We even added a free-form field that would allow you to add how many marriages you wanted to save, and it would calculate that amount in real-time.  After making a selection, the user is directed to the donation form where the amount they chose is auto-populated on the giving form, to reduce friction. It is also important to note that we were testing for revenue lift on desktop only (not mobile due to screen size limitations).
experiments.keyLearnings:
If you notice, this experiment is labeled V1. We launched this pre-Giving Tuesday (11/23/22)....and were seeing very positive results...that is until the experiment was "over-ridden" inadvertently by FOTF due to some internal development directives. The over-ride happened right before Giving Tuesday, so we stopped getting results and ended up manually stopping the experiment on Dec 2, 2022. Prior to that stoppage, we recorded 236% lift in revenue for the treatment version with a 99.2% Level of Confidence. By statistical measures, this was a valid lift. Even when we removed outlier donations over $2k from both the control and the treatment, we still saw a significant lift from the treatment.
Due to the technical over-ride, we did not feel comfortable relying on these results to make any future recommendations other than to re-build the experiment as it was, and quickly re-launch to see if the results continued to show a positive lift in revenue through the remainder of the year-end campaign. Results from V2 will be shown in a future write-up and linked here.

experiments.title: How adding impact language to a direct-ask pop up banner during a high urgency campaign affects revenue – babies saved – V3 relaunch
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language on the gift array of a direct ask pop-up to show the impact of the gift will result in greater revenue
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2022 - 12/31/2022
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They provide help and resources for couples to build healthy marriages that reflect God’s design and for parents to raise their children according to morals and values grounded in biblical principles. Every year during the calendar year-end high-urgency campaign, FOTF places a top-of-page pop-up banner that contains a direct ask to support their mission. This banner (known internally as the “wiki-banner”) has been tested into over many years, with experiments to improve donation metrics. Since many people visit FOTF.com during this season to make a year-end gift, the “wiki-banner” serves as a low-friction tactic to get people to the donation page quickly. It is important to note that the visiting audience to FOTF’s website is served different content / experiences based on pages they have consumed in the past…specifically a “marriage audience” and an “in favor of pro-life audience – Santicity of Human Life” (also known as SOHL).
For this specific experiment targeting the “SOHL“, we were focused on changing the language on the gift array buttons to reflect the impact that a gift would make on the amount of babies saved. The theory was that if we could directly show how many babies could be saved with a specific gift amount, the visitor would be moved to give a bigger gift to make a greater impact. As screen real-estate is at a premium for this “wiki-banner”, we designed an experience that showed the number of babies saved on a button on the wiki-banner, and in a “hover-state” it would flip to the equivalent dollar amount of the gift. We even added a free-form field that would allow you to add how many babies you wanted to save, and it would calculate that amount in real-time.  After making a selection, the user is directed to the donation form where the amount they chose is auto-populated on the giving form, to reduce friction. It is also important to note that we were testing for revenue lift on desktop only (not mobile due to screen size limitations).
NOTE: This is the third version of the experiment (V3 relaunch) due to technical challenges of the V1 + V2 version not properly returning data.
experiments.keyLearnings:
If you notice, this experiment is labeled V3 relaunch. We launched this one AFTER V1 + V2 did not return any data. As it turns out, the previous versions of this experiment did not include a pivotal piece of code that would identify this target audience as having consumed "SOHL" content in the past. As such, the previous experiments returned no data.
Once we corrected the errors, we re-launched this experiment in parallel with the experiment that was targeting "marriage audiences". Like it's "sister-experiment", this version ran from 12/11 to 12/31. Unlike the "sister-version" of this experiment, the results showed an increase in the revenue by around 82% with a 98.6% Level of Confidence....and thus statistically validating for the treatment.
Like its' sister experiment....This led us to even more questions like...
What would results have looked like earlier in the campaign if the code had been correct?Why does this audience respond more positively to impact language than the marriage audience? Does the timing of the experiment change the results?
There were several key take-aways that came from this series of experiments.
Timing/motivation may play a big role in the effectiveness of impact language, but we cannot be sure due to the technical challenges experienced early in the campaign.Make sure you are always monitoring your experiments for data anomalies, and don't be afraid to end an experiment early and re-launch if the data does not "look right"We cannot say definitively that the impact language led to great revenue during the majority of the high-urgency campaign...but we can say that there is enough data to show a positive trend and learnings that we will take into our next high-urgency campaign experiment

experiments.title: How adding a dollar goal to a sticky bar impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the monetary goal in a sticky bar for site visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2022 - 12/31/2022
During a high urgency campaign, RTB ran a sitewide sticky bar that prompted people to give towards their campaign. Mid-campaign the team noted that the stickybar did not state the dollar goal amount and adding this goal may motivate more people to contribute. In order to test this hypothesis we created an experiment where the control (the original sticky bar) did not have the goal amount and the treatment stated the goal amount for the site visitor.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 32% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 96%. This means that people were less likely to give when given the dollar amount in the sticky bar. This could be because this makes the experience more transactional for the site visitor. Additionally, it could be that the dollar amount was too large for the prospective donor and it made them feel like it was unattainable (and that their donation would not have an impact).

rows.name: Sticky Bar - Humanized with Dan's Photo

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar for website visitors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2022 - 01/04/2023
During Avila Institute’s year-end campaign, we wanted to capitalize on traffic to one of their most popular websites, SpiritualDirection.com, and run a 3-way experiment to see whether a sticky bar made an impact on donations.
For this experiment, our control was no sticky bar; variant one had a sticky bar with just copy; variant 2 had a sticky bar with a picture of Dan Burke, the founder of Avila. Both variant 1 and 2 had the exact same copy. We ran this sticky bar on all pages on the website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we expected a sticky bar to outperform no sticky bar, the results of adding in Dan's image were really interesting. The Dan image version of the stick bar had a 92% increase in conversion rates compared to both the control and variant 1. We believe that this is due to Dan's notoriety within the Avila community; everyone knows who he is, what he looks like, and by adding an image we humanized the message a little more, giving donors the perception that it was really Dan asking for urgent help during the campaign.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 89.4% increase with a 94% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 129.3% increase in donations with an 90% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 57.2% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 423.5% increase in donations with a 90% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 227.5% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.35-44 visitors had a 1.3% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 54.1% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 123.0% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 209.2% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a yes/no pop up impacts donor conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking  a value-prop yes/no question  for site visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2022 - 12/11/2022
Navigators was running a high urgency campaign at the end of the calendar year. In order to capture more donations from web visitors we ran a test on the CYE popup/overlay. We wanted to see if we could more people to click through and donate on a pop up that was more value prop-centric with a yes/no question. The control for this experiment used campaign-specific language and the treatment asks the site visitor a yes/no question. If they select yes, they’re taking to the donation page. If they select no, the pop up exits out.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 44% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 95.9%. These results negated our initial hypothesis. However, based on previous experiments we've ran with the Navigators, these results are consistent with their audience's behavior. While the second pop up has the site visitor align with a certain value claim, it's not direct in what we're asking the person to do: donate. Previous experiment results show us that this organization's audience responds to more directs communications.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to an instant donation page affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a humanized sticky bar featuring a well-known public figure on an instant donation page will increase donation rates by adding value and credibility to the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2022 - 12/06/2022
For a content offer for a public policy client’s pledge, we wanted to know how adding a humanized sticky bar including the image of a well-known public figure on a donation page would affect donation rates.
This experiment ran as an A/B test with roughly 50% of traffic being shown the treatment including the sticky bar and roughly 50% of traffic shown the control, sans the sticky bar. No other elements were changed.
Because of the large volume of traffic the donation page received, we were able to validate the results of this experiment after only 2 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 48 hours, we observed a 38.6% INCREASE in donation rates, with a 96.9% level of confidence, among traffic who were shown the treatment including the humanized sticky bar, validating our hypothesis that a humanized sticky bar would increase donation rates.
We believe that the increase in donations occurred because the large and colorful sticky bar, featuring a well-known and well-respected public figure among this organization's prospective donors, increased the visibility of the perceived impact for potential donors.
The sticky bar also invites the user to "join" this public figure in achieving the outcome they want to achieve, while additional creating a message match from the pledge page seen before the donation page, as the pledge referred directly to the four values being presented in the sticky bar, creating momentum toward the conversion and leveraging the persuasion principle of "consistency" to inspire the user to follow up on their pledge by taking action.
It is also worth noting that the treatment including the sticky bar produced a ~15% higher average gift, perhaps because the additional value statement shown in the sticky bar increased the perceived value of a larger donation in the minds of donors.
Because of the positive results of this experiment, we will look for other opportunities to include a sticky bar on instant donation pages in future acquisition efforts.

experiments.title: How does using an exit intent pop-up impact donations for Pathway to Victory?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an exit intent for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2022 - 11/04/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that primarily generated revenue through monthly premium offers. Typically premium offers will have multiple giving levels that include bundles of various or multiple premiums.
For the month of October 2022, the premium was a brand-new book written by Dr. Jeffress titled, “18 Minutes with Jesus.” Dr. Jeffress traveled the country and did multiple interviews for this premium so the extra traffic provided an opportunity for us to run multiple experiments on their website.
One of our homepage experiments focused on whether an exit intent pop-up helped drive a higher donor conversion rate. This pop-up was launched when a user, on the landing page for the offer, then clicked on the ‘back button. Our hypothesis was that it would help drive additional revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the overall results of this experiment show a slight decline in conversion rates of 2.5% with a 100% level of confidence, this does not tell the whole story. Since the pop-up was only triggered when a potential donor did not complete their donation and clicked on the back button in their browser, we can look at the results as this exit-intent helped drive additional donations and revenue towards the campaign, instead of whether it performed 'better' than the control version. Since the donor's intent was to not complete the donation by hitting the back button, using this exit intent pop-up led to a 63% conversion rate for those that were served the exit intent.

experiments.title: How communal-based communication affects clickthrough rate on a matching gift popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing communal language in the donation popup for website visitors will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/10/2020
CaringBridge was running a two-day match in the month of December as part of their year end campaign. They had previously tested into a highly effective popup during matches that used urgency and an hourly donor countdown to increase response during these time-sensitive campaigns. However, they wondered if more “communal” messaging that affirmed the donor in their relationship to the CaringBridge author and made a less direct ask would be more effective at getting people to enter the donation funnel.
They drafted a treatment that made some key shifts. First, it began by talking about the donor themselves—”You’ve been such a great friend by staying engaged through CaringBridge.” Then, it softened the ask by starting with “If it’s not too much to ask”, which gives the donor a sort of “release valve” from the pressure of “donate now”. Finally, the matching gift from the Board of Directors was positioned as an opportunity or service offering provided to the donor rather than something they were expected to respond to.
They split the traffic between the two popups and monitored downstream traffic separately to see how it affected both clickthrough rate and conversion, as they realized that the two do not always correlate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the communal language produced a 45.3% increase in clickthrough rate. There are a few reasons behind what might have contributed to the increase.

First, the intro of "You've been such a great friend by staying connected through CaringBridge" not only affirms the donor, it piques their interest. It's a compliment, rather than the presumptive "your donation will be matched" language on the control. This additional engagement could have contributed to more people reading the next sentence—"If it's not too much to ask, would you consider giving today so that our Board of Directors can match your gift?".

This next sentence presents the ask in a completely different way. First, the donor is given an initial choice: "is this too much to ask, or not?", before they read about the incentive. Second, the incentive is offered in a radically different way than the control. Where the control actually thanks the Board of Directors for matching, the treatment gives the donor the chance to be the hero by making the gift, and places the match in a position that is responsive to the donor's action.

This test illuminated the possibilities of "communal" language for CaringBridge—which is a very "communal" organization in the first place. This opens up the opportunity do to more testing on the donation page downstream from this ask.

experiments.title: How matching gift copy affects clickthrough rate on a modal popup
experiments.researchQuestion: How does matching gift language affect clickthrough rate on a modal popup window?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2018 - 12/28/2017
FamilyLife was in the final week of their year-end campaign, and launched a modal popup to point web traffic to the donation page. The popup contained a countdown clock as well as urgency-focused message. The default version of the popup had language that echoed the headline, but they wondered if including matching gift language would increase clickthrough rate.
They created a treatment that kept urgent language, but added in the fact that the gift would be matched dollar-for-dollar. They launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment (with matching gift language) produced a 56% increase in clickthrough rate. This test revealed that while urgency is key to any message with a deadline (especially at the end of the year), incentive can provide additional lift for action oriented copy.
In the control, the potential donor is presented with four elements to inspire them to click through and give: an urgent headline, a countdown clock, additional copy above the button, and the button copy. But in the treatment, the matching gift language provides a fifth element, without leaving out any of the previous four—incentive.
Many times, calls-to-action have one of these elements, but not the other. This test shows the power of combining the two.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to an acquisition page affected conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a digital acquisition donation page for prospective donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2022 - 08/25/2022
Luther Seminary is offering Enter the Bible: Revelation Study Guide as an acquisition offer through paid Facebook ads and to their housefile through email. We wanted to increase ICR on the instant donation page, so we decided to retest the humanized sticky bar on this page.
We hypothesized that this would increase donor conversion rate by humanizing the donation page with a message (and picture) from Katie Langston who is well known in the Enter the Bible community asking the user for a donation to be able to continue to provide resources like this one in the future.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding in a humanized sticky bar actually led to a decrease in conversion rates of 56.4%.
This informs us that this humanized message actually has a negative effect on donor conversion rate and decreases the motivation to give a gift. We believe this is due to the lack of motivation to give throughout the full funnel therefore the presence of the sticky bar does not "convince" people to make a donation.
Further testing on messaging and subjects will be done in the future to see if we can get a lift. This experiment should be run again on an instant donation page that receives a high volume of traffic to the page.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 53.7% decrease with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar on an instant donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2022 - 05/29/2022
Luther Seminary is offering an Enter the Bible: Proverbs Study Guide as an acquisition offer through paid Facebook ads. While watching the data over a period of time, we noticed the offer’s GCPN was averaging around $3 – $4 and the % signed up was averaging around 45% – 50%. While both of these metrics were good, we noticed the ICR was very low, averaging around 1.6%. This caused us to reevaluate the donation page and brainstorm an experiment.
While we have previously tested adding a humanized sticky bar to high urgency campaign donation pages, we have never tested adding this type of sticky bar to an instant donation page following an acquisition offer.
We hypothesized that this would increase donor conversion rate by humanizing the donation page with a message (and picture) from Katie Langston who is well known in the Enter the Bible community asking the user for a donation to be able to continue to provide resources like this one.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The addition of the humanized sticky bar resulted in a valid 839.5% lift in donor conversion rate. This informs us that Luther's audience responds to seeing a familiar face in their donation experience. When they are spoken to by a member of an organization they are more likely to respond. This also informs us that the Luther audience cares about the organization's resources and wants Luther Seminary to be able to continue providing free resources to teach people all over the world about Jesus.
It is recommended that this is tested on other acquisition offer donation pages.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 780.8% increase with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding an exit intent pop up to blog pages impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an exit intent pop up with an ebook offer for blog readers will achieve a higher email acquisition rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2022 - 07/01/2022
Reasons to Believe’s main site traffic is generated by their blogs. They’re a content-heavy organization and their blogs are a prime opportunity to invite people to take the next step. While we already had dear readers on their blogs, we hypothesized that not many people scrolled to the bottom of their blog pages – especially given the length of many of their articles. In order to introduce another opportunity for name capture, we decided to test adding an exit intent pop up with an ebook offer. We also embedded the name acquisition form to this pop up in order to reduce any friction for the site visitor. We hypothesized that adding this exit intent pop up would increase email acquisition rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 44% lift in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 98%. Our hypothesis was proven correct that giving people another opportunity to receive an offer by giving their email would increase email acquisition rate. By making it an exit intent pop up, we were not interrupting their reading experience and did not wait for them to scroll to the bottom of the article. In turn, this also drastically impacted Google Grant results that generate traffic to these pages. We saw the most names acquired in any month through this experiment.

experiments.title: How an inline offer compares to an exit intent for pop-up email acquisition on a website
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: An inline offer will acquire more emails than an exit intent pop-up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2020 - 09/25/2020
The Oblates were using an exit intent pop-up to acquire emails with an offer to get daily prayers sent via email. The effort was highly successful in acquiring emails, but presented a few drawbacks. First, exit intents can only be see on desktop computers. Second, the user experience is not ideal because people may feel pestered as they try to leave the page. So we wanted to test an inline offer for daily prayer instead of an exit intent. We needed to do it in a way that ensured that it did not diminish the success of having an exit intent pop-up.
experiments.keyLearnings: Overall, the inline offer increase email acquisition by 70%. And when we looked at just desktop traffic, which is the device that a third of their constituents use, it had no affect on email conversion rates.

experiments.title: How using direct language in pop-up copy for a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that directly asking if Working Preacher is a helpful resource for people viewing the Working Preacher website will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2022 - 05/26/2022
Luther Seminary runs a high urgency campaign for Working Preacher every year during the fall and spring. Luther Seminary ran an A/B pop-up test during the fall campaign between copy about helping pastors receive the resources they need and copy about supporting the Working Preacher campaign. The copy around supporting the Working Preacher campaign and website saw a 7.9% lift with a 41.9% LoC.
For the spring campaign, they wanted to use copy around supporting the campaign and website, but they tested more detailed copy around what Working Preacher provides for users against shorter copy that more directly asked the user if Working Preacher helped them as an individual.
They crafted an alternate popup and split the traffic to see if it produced a conversion increase.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we observed was a 49.5% increase in donor conversion rate on the treatment with a 97.9% level of confidence. The results indicated that the Working Preacher audience responds more to a direct and specific ask about whether or not Working Preacher was valuable to them as an individual. Being direct on what results from their gift by helping them specifically grow closer to God and lead others vs being abstract by saying Working Preacher helps people and pastors all over the world will inspire more gifts leading to a higher donor conversion rate.
Another takeaway from this experiment is that mobile visitors had a 229.8% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence. We believe this to be caused by the treatment having shorter pop-up copy therefore the user was more inclined to click on the "Make your donation" button because the shorter copy caused less friction.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 74.8% increase with a 100% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 229.8% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence.Returning visitors had a 56.1% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
