experiments.title: How a sticky donate button with a campaign specific CTA effects donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a CTA on a sticky donate button related to an ongoing high urgency campaign will result in a higher number of donations, than a generic donate button.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2022 - 06/22/2022
Focus on the Family runs a high urgency campaign every year centered around pro-life issues. This year, they wanted to test if adding a sticky donate button to article pages with a call to action mirroring the ongoing high urgency campaign would result in higher donor conversions than a similar button with a generic call to action. It is worth noting that this experiment was limited to a segmented audience that used user data to identify if a user had prior pro-life interests. For the control, a generic donate button (with a pulsing heart icon) was placed on article pages that matched the data segmentation. For the treatment, a sticky button with the words ” Save a Baby” (with a pulsing baby icon) was placed on article pages that matched the data segmentation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
During this experiment that had 91,000 samples over a two week period, we saw a 55% increase for the treatment with a 95.5% Level of Confidence. In this case, this experiment validated for the hypothesis. Key takeaways from this experiment are that matching the CTA of the button to the segmented audience during a high urgency campaign can result in a greater conversion.
Next steps for the experiment would be to test his same hypothesis using the same data segments but do so during a non-high urgency campaign to see if properly segmented users are still converted at a higher frequency with a specific CTA.

experiments.title: How a delayed pop-up affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that triggering a pop-up in a shorter duration for site visitors will achieve a better survey completion/email capture rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2022 - 05/22/2022
We built a National Scouting survey pop-up modal to live on the BSA homepage to drive survey completions. Internal teams were concerned with site UX and asked that we set pop-up to fire after the user was on the page for 15 seconds. We believed a shorter duration of 5 seconds would produce a better result and so we created a split test for half of the users to see a pop-up at 15 seconds and the other half to receive it after 5 seconds had elapsed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The 5 second pop-up clearly outperformed waiting 15 seconds to trigger the modal. The 5 second variant acquired 53 emails versus the 23 acquired by the 15 second version. The 15 second version underperformed the 5 second version by 56% with a 99.9% confidence rate, proving that the shorted trigger duration was clearly the stronger performing variant.
Hypothesis for this performance has more to do with time on page and were users even on the page for 15 seconds to see the longer triggered variant on the page or had they already navigated elsewhere within the BSA site.
Follow up test will be to shorten the pop-up trigger from 5 seconds to 2 seconds. Test to follow this one will test trigger time versus triggering pop-up upon page scroll.

experiments.title: How adding a donor countdown on a sticky bar affects donor conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro-goal and donor countdown on a sticky bar  for site visitors  will achieve a high donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/26/2022 - 05/08/2022
RTB was running a recurring giving (Monthly Partner) campaign. Their goal was 100 new recurring donors by the end of the campaign. In order to bring awareness to the campaign, the used a sticky bar on their website to drive traffic to the Monthly Partner donation page. While they’ve been using “100 monthly donors” in their messaging, we hypothesized that breaking down the goal into a smaller, more achievable goal would drive more donations. We stated that “10 monthly donors” are needed today in order to provide a micro-goal. In addition to that change, we had the button have an active countdown on how many monthly donors were left in the daily goal.
Traffic to this page was primarily driven organically.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 100% increase in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 95%. However through most of the duration, the experiment was targeted to only the homepage (not site-wide), which gave us a smaller sample size than expected. Nonetheless, the experiment did validate and informs us that RTB's audiences are more likely to respond to a smaller, achievable goal in a recurring donor campaign. The larger goal of 100 donors can potentially feel like a daunting goal that the donor cannot realistically contribute to.
After this experiment ran, we launched a site-wide pop up with both the micro goal and the donor countdown. This is worth retesting for a different high urgency campaign to see if we get the same type of response.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with a “special message” on a donation page impacted gifts from returning visitors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personalized sticky bar for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2022 - 03/21/2022
In an effort to improve donor conversion, we wanted to further “humanize” the instant donation page for Focus on the Family’s video series subscribers. We developed a treatment that included a special message sticky bar from a Vice President encouraging visitors to remain on the page and take the time read the donation page offer. The sticky bar included a photo of the VP and the following note: “Thank you for signing up for this video series! Before you go, please take a moment to read the important message below to learn how you can help more families get access to free video series like this one.” We split traffic 50/50 and launched this treatment on several active donation pages to observe the impact of the sticky bar on donation conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment slightly over one week, we saw a 33% lift in donor conversion rate with 95% level of confidence. We were especially interested in how returning visitors would respond to this message since it was a new highlight to previous donation pages they would have experienced. For that segment specifically we saw an 83% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence. We will continue to run the sticky bar to 100% of traffic based on these results but we will also move forward with testing special messages from other staff members at Focus to see if we can continue to improve and optimize the treatment by continually rotating how the page appears for repeat visitors to hold their attention.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:

experiments.title: How adding communal language on a sticky bar impacted website clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding communal language for online course takers will achieve an increase in website visitors.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/01/2021
When launching a new course, DTS adds a sticky bar to their website to allow visitors to access the course. The standard language is about getting access to the newest free online course, but we wanted to test how addressing website visitors conversationally like friends would affect the clickthrough rate on the sticky bar.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that making the language more communal and less transactional provided a 29.2% increase in traffic to the course page. Letting people know that they were invited to take the course because of their existing relationship with the organization was more effective than simply giving them a highly visible point of access. Establishing these findings will be helpful for building language to incorporate in future course offerings. Since we validated the experiment for website traffic, we can do further testing to see if this language is similarly effective in other channels like Facebook ads and email copy.

experiments.title: How adding communal language to a sticky bar impacted free trial sign-ups

rows.name: Sticky Bar Free Trial & Email Disclaimer - w/ Testimonial

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing communal language for a sticky bar on the homepage will achieve an increase in free trial sign-ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2021 - 08/02/2021
As we continue to identify testing opportunities to increase free trial sign-ups on the OA Club homepage, we wanted to test replacing a long-standing testimonial quote in the sticky bar with communal language to see how site visitors would respond. The communal language treatment was more personal in tone and indicated an existing relationship with the visitor. The treatment version was presented as “To say thank you for being a friend to Adventures in Odyssey and to encourage you as you grow in your faith, we’d like to offer your family a 14-day free trial. Begin your free trial now—no credit card required!”
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that free trial signups on the treatment experienced an 8.5% decrease with a 100% level of confidence. But what's interesting when reviewing the behavior as individual segments the conversion rate for returning visitors was a 5.7% while new visitors had a 3.5% conversion rate. It makes sense that addressing new visitors as "friends" would not resonate as strongly as it would for someone who was repeatedly interacting with the site. For future communal language tests, we would want to limit the experience to only returning visitors or design a new communal language treatment that is more introductory and welcoming and acknowledges the majority of the traffic volume to the page is new visitors.

experiments.title: How communal language affects donor conversion on website sticky bars
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Talking to constituents like friends and partners will increase their engagement and donations
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2020 - 12/28/2020
During their calendar year end campaign, the Missionary Oblates were running a sticky bar on their website promoting giving during year end. This sticky bar was placed across their website. Due to some other successes in talking to their constituents like partners in their mission, we wanted to test similar language on the year end sticky bar. So we tested the typical year-end language with elements of urgency against a more communal language with a more conversational tone that also pointed to the upcoming deadline to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment increased donor conversion from the sticky bar by 89%! There are a couple of factors behind this success:
The treatment spoke to website visitors as "friends". By recognizing them as such and even recognizing their prayers and support, we were able to draw people in and increase initial engagement. We see this by the fact that the click-through rate for the treatment increased by 53% with a 99.9% level of confidence. The treatment's call-to-action was softer and more conversational as well by saying, "If it's not too much to ask...". This approach aligned with the initial communal style and continued to reinforce this "friend" and "partnership" approach.
This experiment reinforces that communal language resonates well with this audience. Donors have even told the organization in the past that they see themselves as part of the mission. This presents more opportunities for testing this similar approach in other channels and deeper in the funnel.

experiments.title: How presenting a micro ask before the macro, impacts email conversion from a pop-up on the website
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a micro ask before the macro ask increase email acquisition from the pop-up on the website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2021 - 05/27/2021
For the past five months, Save the Children has been testing into the email acquisition pop-up on their website. The majority of these experiments have been focused around messaging and value proposition. But all of these experiments have resulted in no positive or negative impact on conversion. As we continue to test into this feature on the website and look for ways to optimize the experience, we hypothesized that we might be able to engage more website users and ultimately increase name conversion by presenting an easier engagement experience on the pop-up before we ask them to give us their email address.
To test this, we presented a “yes”/”no” question that a user would answer before the email acquisition form field would show. The “yes”/”no” question was the same sentiment as the control – “Would you like to stay informed?”. The “Yes” button was highlighted red. When a person would click on the “Yes” button, an email form would slide out and the copy read, “Great! Please provide your email address to sign up.” If a person clicked “No”, the pop-up would simply disappear.
The control presented the same kind of email acquisition opportunity, but in the form of a statement – “Sign up to stay informed”.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for 10 days, we found that the "Yes"/"No" micro ask increased email acquisition by 35%! This valid lift happened on both desktop and mobile devices. In fact, mobile devices had the most significant valid increase in name conversion of 42% (99% LoC). This experiment shows us the importance of engaging people through an easy entrance point results in more people likely to convert. This micro ask increases a persons likelihood and motivation to complete the macro ask.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a testimonial that aligns with the value proposition for website visitors to the homepage will achieve an increase in free trial signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/09/2020 - 11/26/2020
We were looking for ways to increase clicks on a sticky bar on the AIO homepage so we developed two treatments to test. The first treatment used the control copy for the testimonial and we added a pulsing star icon to draw attention to the button. For the second treatment, we swapped out the testimonial copy for a new quote that aligned with the value proposition that the audio episodes introduced listeners to a biblical perspective.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment, we found that the second treatment actually decreased free trial signups through the sticky bar and the control copy was still the most effective at getting clicks. The first treatment was also showing a directional decrease comparatively but did not reach statistical significance. However, when looking at donations and revenue on those who did convert, we did validate an increase in donations for each of the treatments so even though we had less traffic to the landing page, the "quality" of the traffic that was coming through ended up being higher.

experiments.title: How communal language impacted donation page visitors at year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using relationship-based language for site visitors will achieve greater donation page traffic.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/28/2021
DTS had a sticky bar running on their site at year-end with language about a deadline to reach a $500,000 Challenge Gift. We wanted to test a hypothesis that adding softer, more relationship-based language in place of the transactional, deadline focused language would drive additional traffic to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the experiment was the treatment copy decreased conversions by 60%. For future experiments, we would want to match the value proposition more closely on the treatment in order to truly isolate the deadline and transactional Challenge Gift language from the communal language. For example, a gift to "reach lives with the truth of the Bible" could have been perceived as more motivating than "reach the world with God's Word" so the results are too convoluted to draw a true conclusion. We would also like to run a similar test during a different season to see if this alters results at all since we know that donors are typically highly motivated to give during the month of December.

experiments.title: How communal language affects clickthrough rate on a recurring giving popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing communal language for repeat visitors will achieve an increase in monthly donors.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/09/2021
CaringBridge had run a popup asking visitors on their 2nd and 8th visits to become a recurring donor for more than a year, which had built a strong base of monthly donors. The control asked donors to “consider something” before they go, and then talked about the need for recurring donors. They decided to try to include some “communal language”, that first thanked the donor for staying connected to the CaringBridge community and then solicited them for a monthly gift based on their firsthand experience of the value of the CaringBridge community.
They decided to test this new language through an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The communal language on the popup increased clickthrough rate by 18.7%. However, upon reviewing the conversion rate on the donation page from the two popup treatments, it was clear that the communal language attracted more clicks but decreased the motivation of the donors, as conversion rate dropped 75.2% from the increase in clicks. This is a reminder that all clicks are not created equal—more traffic does not necessarily result in more donors if those donors are not properly motivated and incentivized by the existing copy.

CaringBridge had a secondary hypothesis that the language on the donation page was not robust enough, and, with some improvement to the page copy, might be able to convert more of these "communal language clickers". They decided to run a monthly giving donation page test first and then re-test this with the winner.

experiments.title: How communal language affects donor conversion on website sticky bars
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Talking to constituents like friends and partners will increase their engagement and donations
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2020 - 12/28/2020
During their calendar year end campaign, the Missionary Oblates were running a sticky bar on their website promoting giving during year end. This sticky bar was placed across their website. Due to some other successes in talking to their constituents like partners in their mission, we wanted to test similar language on the year end sticky bar. So we tested the typical year-end language with elements of urgency against a more communal language with a more conversational tone that also pointed to the upcoming deadline to give.
experiments.keyLearnings: Speaking to the donors in a more informal, less urgent tone increased donations by 89%. It also increased the click through rate by 53% with a 99.9% level of confidence. Our hypothesis is that the increase in donor conversion is due to combining the deadline of December 31 with a tone that draws them in and talks to them like a friend.

experiments.title: How an email acquisition opportunity is presented on the website
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a slide in email acquisition offer impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2021 - 02/23/2021
Save the Children uses a forced pop-up on their website to acquire email addresses. The copy and and trigger timing of the pop-up has been tested in the past with little to no impact on conversion, so we hypothesized that the placement of the feature may impact conversion. To test this, we developed a treatment that functioned more as a slide in feature on the bottom of the page than a forced pop-up. No copy or design of the feature was changed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a significant decrease in donor conversion from the slide in feature compared to the forced pop-up. With an almost 41% decrease in emails acquired, we can learn that the placement of the offer does have an impact on the number of people signing up to stay connected with Save the Children. The pop-up is almost much more disruptive - drawing attention to the feature and capturing more names than the slide in.

Continued testing is needed to really understand the motivation of this audience and what elements of friction exist in the conversion process.

experiments.title: How communal language impacted donation page visitors at year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using relationship-based language for site visitors will achieve greater donation page traffic.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/28/2021
DTS had a sticky bar running on their site at year-end with language about a deadline to reach a $500,000 Challenge Gift. We wanted to test a hypothesis that adding softer, more relationship-based language in place of the transactional, deadline focused language would drive additional traffic to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the experiment was the treatment copy decreased conversions by 60%. For future experiments, we would want to match the value proposition more closely on the treatment in order to truly isolate the deadline and transactional Challenge Gift language from the communal language. For example, a gift to "reach lives with the truth of the Bible" could have been perceived as more motivating than "reach the world with God's Word" so the results are too convoluted to draw a true conclusion. We would also like to run a similar test during a different season to see if this alters results at all since we know that donors are typically highly motivated to give during the month of December.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial to a sticky bar impacted conversions

rows.name: Sticky Bar Free Trial & Email Disclaimer
Sticky Bar Free Trial & Email Disclaimer - with Testimonial

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a testimonial from a free trial member for website visitors will achieve increased conversions on the sticky bar.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/22/2020 - 07/24/2020
AIO was offering a special extended 28 day free trial during the summer months and we wanted to see if introducing a testimonial from a free trial user would lift conversions on the sticky bar. We pulled a quote from one of their survey responses that said “The best audio stories ever! The whole family loves them and we have kids from 5 to 45!” and put this up against the control copy that simply asked the question “Looking for ways to entertain and encourage your family at home?”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The testimonial treatment saw an 11.9% increase with a 98% level of confidence in those who clicked to begin the free trial from the sticky bar. Using this learning, we'll continue to run experiments and additional testing on the body copy to see if there is an optimal testimonial that drives conversions.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 13.8% increase with a 94% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 16.5% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence.Tablet visitors had a 77.7% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding seasonal relevance impacted sticky bar clicks

rows.name: Sticky Bar Free Trial & Email Disclaimer - w/ Testimonial

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing relevancy of an offer for website visitors around the holidays will achieve increased free trial signups through the sticky bar.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/23/2020 - 12/10/2020
Surrounding the Thanksgiving and Christmas holidays, we wanted to increase the appeal and relevancy of a free trial to families by indicating its usefulness for road trips and increased time at home. We pulled a testimonial to use as the treatment copy – “We have so enjoyed this free trial…long rides in the car have been great and we have included AIO in our bedtime routine. My boys love it.” and we ran it against the current testimonial with the strongest leading performance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we discovered was the treatment—while seasonally relevant—decreased conversion rate by 17.3% with a 97.6% level of confidence. Our hypothesis for this decrease in results was due to the limiting factors that were introduced by the new copy. The control copy appeals to a wider audience as it mentions it is for the "whole family" and "kids 5 to 45" while the treatment copy only specifies that "My boys love it" and it may appear that the episodes are only valuable to listen to during travel or at bedtime instead of helpful on a daily basis regardless of the activity at hand. For future experiments, we will look to use testimonials that are seasonally relevant but that would still appeal to a wider audience rather than a limited segment.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Organic Search visitors had a 30.6% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 100% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 21.7% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 96% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 28.2% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How increasing the size of a sticky bar (while asking for more information) affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will increasing the size of a sticky bar (while asking for more information) affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/05/2020
Hillsdale College ran an acquisition sticky bar on Imprimis to encourage new visitors to sign up for a subscription. They noticed in their database that they had a large amount of email addresses with no first name or last name, which lowered downstream value because they were not able to address the prospects by name. They decided to run a test to see if asking for first and last name would affect email acquisition rate. To accommodate these fields, they had to increase the size of the sticky bar, which was an additional factor that could affect conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment, with its larger size and higher ask for information, increased conversion rate by 83.5%. This shows that users are not afraid to give more information—in fact, it might actually increase the commitment to opting in. However, this must be backtested against a sticky bar of the same size that asks just for email to understand what percentage of this increase is due to the size of the bar.

experiments.title: How sticky bars affect conversion in non-match periods
experiments.researchQuestion: How will sticky bars affect donor conversion in non-match periods?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2019 - 07/19/2019
CaringBridge had run sticky bars on their donation pages to promote matching grants during their campaign months. While this tactic had generally lifted response by reinforcing the match, they wanted to see if it could be effective in non-match times as well. They created two different treatments for the sticky bar—one that reminded them that CaringBridge is donor-supported, and one that thanked them for their generosity and included a photo and name of a CaringBridge staff member, for increased personalization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran for a little more than a month, with little happening in the way of increased conversion. The team was about to shut it off, but they noticed that it was nearing a valid lift in conversion on mobile devices. After another ten days, it had achieved validity.
The second treatment, with the thank-you messaging, increased donor conversion by 13.1%—but only on mobile devices. Because the sticky bar has much more visibility at the top of a smaller screen, it is much more impactful to that view. In fact, the sticky bar was producing negative results on desktop (-5%) and tablet (-11%) devices, which were holding back the aggregate increases. As mobile constitutes most of the traffic, this was a big lift in conversion, and an important reminder that device traffic must be split apart to see the true impact of tests. CaringBridge was able to roll this out to mobile visitors only using Google Optimize's personalization feature.

experiments.title: How presenting an email acquisition opportunity as a forced pop-up impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase name acquisition on the Answers pages through a forced and exit-intent pop-up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2019 - 06/01/2019
Billy Graham Evangelical Association gets significant traffic to their Answers pages. At the bottom of these pages there is an opportunity for people to sign up to receive the BGEA newsletter. We hypothesized that presenting that acquisition opportunity in a different way might increase conversion and name acquisition for the organization. To test this, we developed a pop-up/exit-intent pop-up that appeared on desktop (and a mobile slide-up). Knowing that a lot of the people coming to the Answers pages were returning visitors, and that a forced pop-up on a page can cause a poor user experience, we delayed the pop-up from appearing until after a person had been on the page for 30 seconds. If they exited prior to that time and seeing the pop-up, we would then trigger an exit-intent pop-up. In addition to this, we also put a rule on the pop-ups to only show every third visit.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new method for presenting the acquisition opportunity increased conversion by 93.3%! The newsletter sign-up on the bottom of the page was getting lost with all of the other content on the page. By pulling that offer up from the bottom and presenting it when people were more highly engaged with the site, we were able to increase the motivation of people on the page to get the offer. In addition, the trigger parameters that were put on the pop-up created a more positive user experience and will allow the pop-up to be effective longer than just using a forced pop-up with no rules in place.
In our next phase of testing, we would like to test the kind of offer in the pop-up to see if we can increase the relevancy and appeal of what is being offered to people on these high-trafficked, highly-engaged pages.

Billy Graham Evangelical Association gets significant traffic to their Daily Devotional pages. experiments.title: How the minimum gift amount asked on a recurring pop-up impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will changing the minimum donation amount of the recurring gift prompt from $15 to $19 increase overall revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/05/2019 - 10/29/2019
Focus on the Family had seen significant success acquiring new recurring donors through the popup recurring gift ask. The popup was a window that would appear, prior to a gift being processed, that asked the donor to convert their one-time gift into a recurring gift. We had done several iterations of experiments on the pop-up around value proposition, percentage of donation asked, and even the minimum gift amount. We learned that if the minimum gift amount was $25 and not $15, we decreased overall revenue by 49%. After reading research around the average gift for a recurring gift, we found that $19 was what the majority of people would give. Learning this, we decided to test this gift amount as a new minimum amount against our control minimum amount of $15.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for over three weeks, we found that the treatment version ($19 minimum recurring gift version) received 12 less recurring gifts than the control. And even though this minimum recurring gift amount ($19) was more than the control ($15), it would decrease annualized revenue by 43% if we put this in place.
Even though research has shown that $19 is the average gift amount for a recurring gift, we have found through this experiment that when converting a one-time gift into a recurring gift, it can have a negative effect on overall revenue and cause fewer people to convert their gift into a recurring donation.

We decided to see if we could promote The Heritage Guide to the Constitution offer for a premium gift with a sticky bar on the digital guide website. What we didn’t know is whether the sticky bar placement at the top or the bottom of the page would generate more traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings: With a 95% level of confidence, we were able to prove that the sticky bar placed at the top of the page generated 108% more clicks than when the sticky bar was placed at the bottom of the page.

experiments.title: How communicating the impact of a specific gift amount impacts conversion on a recurring gift pop-up
experiments.researchQuestion: Will communicating how many babies can be saved through a specific gift amount impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/27/2019 - 03/12/2019
Focus on the Family has found great success with their recurring gift pop-up feature on donation pages. During a pro-life campaign, they also found that using a value proposition specific to the particular campaign (pro-life), increased recurring gift conversion as well. To continue to build on those learnings, they hypothesized that communicating the number of babies a person could save with their one-time gift versus an ongoing monthly gift might increase recurring conversion even more.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When the motivating factor of the value proposition went from how a person could save babies from abortion by making a donation to how many babies a person could save with their donation, donor conversion significantly increased by 27%. The treatment copy was dynamic based on a person's gift amount and showed people that by converting their one-time gift into a recurring gift, they would have an even greater impact.
Based on these learnings, can we increase donor conversion even more by visually showing people the impact they can have with their recurring gift using images? We propose testing this approach next.

experiments.title: How a two-step form on a pop-up offer affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using a two-step form instead of a “Learn More” link lead to more conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2019 - 03/05/2019
In this experiment, we had launched popups on key pages on our website in order to try and get a few more people to enroll in the online fundraising benchmark study. To enroll in the study, you needed to provide 3 key metrics – monthly web traffic, monthly donations, and average gift size.
The original pop-up had a brief description of the report, and then had a button link to the landing page. But we wondered if we could increase conversion by asking someone right away what their monthly web traffic was. Upon entering their web traffic, they’d advance to the landing page to finish provide their other 2 metrics and their contact info. (The landing page pre-filled their web traffic info so it didn’t have to be answered again)
experiments.keyLearnings: While the volume of people converting on this offer is small (this was expected), the treatment saw an infinite increase in conversions. Although it required more work to get to the landing page, having someone complete an easy first step made them more committed and likely to convert on the following page. Clicking "Learn More" is a much easier action, but it doesn't result in as much motivation and commitment to the final offer.

experiments.title: How the kind of offer presented on a homepage pop-up impacts donor conversion

rows.name: Grandparent Content Offer with Donation Ask

experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer would result in a higher donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2018 - 11/18/2018
Boys Town offers parents and grandparents content to help them in their parenting journey. This content is offered to people on their website and through paid media. They have offered this content on the homepage of their website as well. While this method has been great to acquire new email addresses for the organization, it hasn’t been great to acquire new donors. In an effort to increase this metric, they hypothesized testing a direct donation ask as a pop-up on their homepage against one of their better performing offers, a grandparent email series.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we were able to acquire email addresses with the grandparent offer, we weren't able to convert any of those names into donors instantly. In fact, the grandparent offer received zero donations while the direct ask received 18 in a two week time period. The traffic coming to the homepage of the Boys Town site is relatively motivated to give and help families in need. This is a bit surprising knowing that the majority of the traffic coming to the site are new and not necessarily returning visitors. The direct ask concept is something we will want to continue to test by adding value proposition, having a donation page that matches the ask of the pop-up, segmenting the homepage traffic with specific content based on their engagement, etc.
In the month's time that the direct ask pop-up was up, it brought in 110 donations and over $6,200 in revenue.

experiments.title: What sticky bar message drives the highest click activity when promoting a survey?

rows.name: Help us align our priorities based upon what is important to YOU!
Can you spend 5-minutes telling us what is important to you in 2019?
Is American headed in the right direction? How did Trump do? Answer these and more...

experiments.researchQuestion: What sticky bar message drives the highest click activity when promoting a survey?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/20/2018
During year end fundraising in 2018, we launched a strategy that started by engaging web visitors, prospects and donors through the National Concerned Citizen Survey, which was followed by an ask. As a part of the traffic generation tactics that were used was adding sticky bars on https://americansforprosperity.org and http://afpscorecard.org websites.
We wondered which type of sticky bar promotional message would drive the highest clickthrough rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: With a 126.7% lift over the standard message of "helping us align our priorities to what is important to YOU", we saw that promoting the "ease" of the survey ("Will you take 5-minutes to help us align our priorities?") seems to drive the highest click activity.

experiments.title: How placement of a sticky bar affects email acquisition on mobile devices
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a sticky bar anchored to the top of the mobile experience perform against one anchored to the bottom?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/15/2018
Leadership Institute sought to acquire as many email addresses as possible from their flagship news site, CampusReform.org. They had put email acquisition offers on the site through “sticky bars” and sought to optimize both through testing and optimization on both desktop and mobile. They had tested to a sticky bar that appeared when someone scrolled through 25% of the page at the bottom of the page. They hypothesized that this might be interrupting the user experience too much on mobile, as the content removed their ability to scroll down. They decided to test placement—from the current “bottom anchored” bar to the “top anchored” bar.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The top-anchored sticky bar produced a 110% lift in name acquisition than the bottom-anchored sticky bar—which showed that where you place the sticky bar in terms of user experience has a tremendous impact on the results it produces. 

Ultimately, this test established a new standard—future testing should focus on design and value proposition, but this provides a helpful framework for understanding user experience.

experiments.title: How the value proposition on a website pop-up can impact clicks to a donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition would get more people to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2017 - 10/23/2017
The month of October is Breast Cancer Awareness Month. This is a critical time of year for National Breast Cancer Foundation to raise money for their organization around awareness and education of breast cancer. They get a significant amount of traffic to their homepage during this month. They have a pop-up on the homepage that they display in an effort to convert the site traffic into donors. They tested the value proposition of this pop-up from something tangible (provide 1,000 more women with screen services) to a more general value proposition (help women facing breast cancer).
experiments.keyLearnings: The more general value proposition increased clicks to the donation page by 14%! The general value proposition had a higher appeal. Providing help to women facing breast cancer is something that people can understand, there is no explanation needed and they want to give towards that. The more specific value proposition was a great approach, but it lacked clarity about what “screening services” were, creating friction in the mind of the end user.

experiments.title: General vs Specific: Does a Tangible Ask Increase Sticky Button Conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the sticky button ask from “Donate” to “Give Shoes” for website visitors will achieve more gifts and revenue because a specific, tangible ask makes the impact clearer and more motivating than a generic donation ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2026 - 03/11/2026
Buckner is a humanitarian ministry organization with many varying initiatives and campaigns. We’ve recently been evaluating how to convert visitors to their websites into donors, and decided on a sticky donate button design. However, we wanted to test if a specific campaign call-to-action would outperform a general donation ask in driving donation conversions through the sticky button.
We tested two versions of a persistent sticky button:
Control: A heart icon with the text: “Donate”, leading to a general donation page supporting children and families
Treatment: A shoe icon with the text: “Give Shoes”, leading to the Shoes for Orphan Souls donation page (one of Buckner’s most recognizable giving opportunities)
Shoes for Orphan Souls is an evergreen campaign that is also currently promoted with Meta Advertising. It has a strong correlation to Buckner’s brand and a clear cost-per-impact, at $15 for a pair of shoes for one child.
Our hypothesis centered on whether specificity, especially tied to this well-known and emotionally resonant program, would make the giving opportunity feel more compelling.
This test ran for 30 days with a 50/50 traffic split on their general homepage and blog pages, excluding any sensitive blog topics or resources that usually avoid donation asks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran this test for 1 month, but the site traffic and sticky button conversion rates were too low for us to acquire enough samples for a valid result. The data we do have shows the specific treatment ask to Give Shoes was likely preferred over the control ask to Donate, but further testing is needed to see if this result is truly valid.
Key Learnings:
1.) Tangible framing may also influence gift amount - The treatment saw a higher average gift ($70 vs $50), suggesting that the specific impact framing anchored donors to give more generously, not just convert at higher rates. This suggests that reducing ambiguity at the first touchpoint  may increase both conversion and gift size.
2.) Familiar, campaign based asks can outperform generic ones - Shoes for Orphan Souls is a well-known and emotionally resonant program. This test suggests that leading with a recognizable and mission-specific initiative can outperform general appeals, especially on high traffic entry points like a homepage, where they may be motivated to give to that campaign in the first place.
3.) The Sample size limits confidence, but the direction is strong enough to warrant future testing - With only 8 donations, this test lacks the volume needed for a firm conclusion. However, the consistency of the lift indicates that this is a promising direction worth retesting or scaling in a new way.
In future testing, we would need to increase the sample size by running the element on more pages, and also track clicks on each CTA button to see if the landing pages had any effect on the conversion rate.
We should also test alternate elements such as a slide-up element to see if that would increase visitor engagement and get more samples for a test like this.

experiments.title: Does Calling Out the Giving Tuesday Donor Goal in the Sticky Bar Increase Engagement?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the Giving Tuesday donor goal for IMB homepage visitors will increase engagement with the web asset, as making the goal visible taps into goal-oriented behavior. Visitors are more likely to click, explore, and give when they understand exactly what their participation contributes to.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/09/2025
During IMB’s 2025 Giving Tuesday campaign, we tested the addition of a donor goal to the homepage sticky bar. Our hypothesis was that clearly displaying the goal would increase engagement with the sticky bar—measured by higher click-through rates—and drive more motivated traffic further down the giving path.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding a donor goal to the homepage sticky bar resulted in a notable increase in engagement, yielding a 29.9% rise in clicks across all traffic. This suggests that prominently displaying a clear Giving Tuesday goal can attract more visitors to the giving pathway. However, because the confidence level fell below the standard 95% threshold, these engagement results should be interpreted as directional rather than definitive for future decision-making. When examining downstream giving behavior, both the control and treatment produced 9 total gifts.
Transactions per session: Treatment showed a 1.3% lift (2.1% LOC)
 	Transactions per click: Treatment showed a 22% decrease (43.5% LOC)
In other words, while the treatment succeeded in motivating more users to click into the donation experience, it did not increase their likelihood of completing a gift once they arrived at the donation page. The sticky bar drove more traffic to the page, but not more donors. Revenue: To prevent outliers from skewing insights, gifts over $500 were removed. Following this adjustment, the treatment resulted in a 93.7% decrease in revenue (94.1% LOC) compared to the control. This suggests that although engagement was higher, the additional traffic did not translate into higher-value giving behavior. In future experiments, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations of the donor goal messaging to see if different phrasing or formats may have a more significant impact on user engagement. It is also important to continue monitoring and analyzing results from experiments to continually improve strategies for engaging with website visitors and driving them further down the giving path.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with donor centric language affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language on the stickybar for website visitors will achieve an increase in clicks because having language where the donor is first and focusing on what their donation does would encourage users to click more.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/27/2025
For CaringBridge, during their 2 day match in June, we wanted to test changing the language on the stickybar. This was an appeal stickybar. The language change was to put the donor “first”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the language on the stickybar to put the donor first and focus on what their donation does resulted in a 12.2% increase in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that the revised language resonated with website visitors and motivated them to engage with the call to action.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of the language to further optimize for donor engagement. Additionally, conducting more experiments with larger sample sizes to achieve a higher statistical level of confidence would help ensure the validity of the results and the effectiveness of the changes implemented. It is important to continue testing and refining strategies based on data-driven insights to maximize impact and drive successful outcomes for Caringbridge.

experiments.title: Can Introducing a Slide-Up Prompt Donations from Prospective Donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a slide-up donation prompt to the high-traffic “Ways to Listen” page will increase conversions by reaching engaged users at a moment of intent without disrupting their experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2025 - 06/09/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, an NPR member station serving listeners across North Carolina. The goal was to explore whether introducing a slide-up donation prompt on the station’s “Ways to Listen” page could increase conversions.
To do this, the team developed a new web asset specifically for this high-traffic page. The slide-up was designed to engage users with a subtle, timely prompt that encourages giving—without interrupting their browsing experience. By targeting a page that typically serves an informational (rather than fundraising) purpose, the test aims to uncover a new opportunity to engage potential donors in a low-friction, high-visibility context.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Conclusion & Next Steps

The slide-up treatment increased conversions from 0.07% to 0.27%, a 297% relative lift. While the confidence level of 81.9% does not meet the threshold for statistical significance, the notable increase suggests that the slide-up is an effective way to engage visitors on the “Ways to Listen” page—a section typically focused on information rather than fundraising.

Key Learnings:
The “Ways to Listen” page presents an untapped opportunity to introduce donation asks in a non-intrusive way.
 	Visitors on this page are open to engaging with donation prompts when presented subtly through slide-ups.
 	Even a simple slide-up design can significantly improve conversion rates over no prompt.
Recommendations for Next Steps:
Design Test: Introduce a more humanized slide-up featuring a photo of a popular WUNC host to build trust and emotional connection.
 	Messaging Test: Experiment with different copy that emphasizes the listener’s impact or the value of WUNC’s programming to increase motivation.
 	CTA Test: Try varying calls to action (e.g., “Support WUNC,” “Join Our Community,” “Help Keep Us On Air”) to find the most compelling phrasing.

experiments.title: Does a pop-up with future framing copy generate more donations than a contrast effect copy?
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believed that emphasizing urgency and the challenges ahead would better motivate donations than the paywall-focused message, as it could create a stronger emotional incentive and sense of immediacy for users.
Psychological Framing
This test compared two psychological tactics:
Future Framing (Test): Highlights what lies ahead—upcoming threats, opportunities, or needs. This approach encourages users to act now to influence future outcomes.
Contrast Effect (Control): Draws attention to what users currently have (free access) and implies a risk of losing it. This technique relies on juxtaposing the current benefit with the potential consequences of inaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2025 - 04/01/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate the impact of homepage pop-up messaging on donor acquisition. The goal was to determine whether leaning into urgency and future challenges would outperform messaging focused on WUNC’s lack of a paywall.

experiments.title: How an impact pop-up Dear Reader message affects donor conversion on article pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that displaying the “Dear Reader” message as an impact pop-up rather than at the end of the article will increase donor conversion rates by capturing attention earlier and making the donation ask more immediate and visible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2025 - 03/26/2025
FamilyLife is a Christian organization focused on strengthening marriages and families by providing resources, events, and guidance rooted in biblical principles, helping people navigate the challenges of family life and fostering lasting, healthy relationships.​
In previous campaigns, FamilyLife has utilized “Dear Reader” messages at the end of article pages—a tactic that has proven successful in increasing donation rates. To build upon this success, we are testing an alternative approach: implementing an impact pop-up “Dear Reader” message that appears shortly after the page loads, eliminating the need for users to scroll to the end of the article. Our hypothesis is that the immediacy and visibility of the pop-up will capture users’ attention more effectively, thereby increasing the likelihood of securing donations.​
For this test, we are promoting the devotional “You’re Going to Make It” by Lysa TerKeurst, a resource that has demonstrated strong performance in FamilyLife’s Facebook advertising campaigns. The experiment is structured as a 50/50 split: half of the site traffic encounters the traditional end-of-article “Dear Reader” message (Control), while the other half experiences the impact pop-up “Dear Reader” message (Treatment).
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 30 days, from February 26 to March 26. While the results did not reach statistical validity, the impact pop-up "Dear Reader" message (Treatment) showed a positive trend, leading to a 49.5% increase in donor conversion rate with an 85.9% level of confidence compared to the end-of-article "Dear Reader" message (Control).

Although the overall conversion rate remained low, the pop-up format demonstrated a higher engagement potential, suggesting that making the donation ask more immediate and visible may help drive conversions.

Next Steps: Since the results were not statistically valid, retesting this approach in different contexts—such as other article pages, adjusting pop-up timing, or refining the message—could help validate the learning. Further optimizations to the design or placement of the pop-up may also enhance its effectiveness in future tests.

experiments.title: Should asks for the content pages be premium focused?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having the ask on the content pages be similar to the homepage for the Spring Share campaign will achieve more transactions because there will be continuity between the asks on the site, which can lead to more trust and credibility.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/31/2025
Moody Radio is running a Spring Share campaign that drives a ton of traffic to their website from on-air, paid traffic, and social.
Earlier in the campaign, we tested how an ask on the content pages should look with a premium.
For this test, we wanted to see if presenting the ask with the premium being the focus is better at converting donors, or if presenting the ask similar to how we are on the homepage would be better.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that presenting the ask on content pages differently from the homepage for the Spring Share campaign resulted in a 20.6% decrease in donations. Although the results did not reach a statistical level of confidence of 95%, the trend indicates that the control (current homepage format) performed better than the treatment (focus on premium).

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the potential impact of changing the format of the ask on different parts of the website. It is clear that continuity and consistency in messaging across different pages can play a crucial role in building trust and credibility with donors. Therefore, it may be beneficial to maintain a similar approach to the ask throughout the website to ensure a unified donor experience.

Further testing is recommended to validate these findings and to explore other factors that may influence donation conversions on content pages. Adjusting the format of the ask, testing different premiums, or even considering the timing of the ask in the donor journey could all be valuable areas for future experimentation to optimize donation conversions for the Spring Share campaign on Moody Radio's website.

experiments.title: Does adding a video slide-out to a landing page increase instant donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that by adding a small slide-out video to the landing page—mirroring the ad experience that likely brought users there—we could increase donor conversion rates by creating a more consistent, engaging, and compelling user journey.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/24/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The goal was to assess whether a video slide-out on the landing page of a popular content offer would improve donor conversion rates. The feature mimicked the ad experience and was only shown to desktop users.

experiments.title: Does a specific ask perform better than a general for an offer?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a suggested gift amoutn on the button for the premium slide in will achieve a higher conversion rate because the donor will have less to think about if we already present them with a donation amount.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2025
Moody Radio runs a Spring Share each year that drives a ton of traffic to their website.
With the campaign launch, we promoted one of their premiums “Killing Sin”. We put the offer on a slide in for the content pages on the website. We’ve tested into this element before and we know that it works really well for the Moody audiences. For this test, we really wanted to see what would happen if we added a suggested gift amount on the button to go to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a suggested gift amount on the button for the premium slide in resulted in a 30.1% increase in donations for all traffic. This indicates that providing a specific donation amount can help simplify the decision-making process for donors and prompt them to take action.

In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the impact of suggesting gift amounts on donations. It would be beneficial to conduct further tests to validate the initial findings and determine if this strategy consistently leads to increased conversion rates. Additionally, we can experiment with different suggested gift amounts to see if certain amounts are more effective than others in driving donations.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that providing a suggested gift amount can be a successful tactic for increasing donations on the website. This insight can inform future strategies for optimizing donation processes and enhancing the overall fundraising efforts of Moody Radio.

experiments.title: How adding a donor goal sticky bar to the donation page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar to the sohl donation page for the CYE campagin will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the sticky bar will reinforce the need as well as add exclusivity to the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2024 - 12/28/2024
For this test we wanted to see if adding a sticky bar that contains a donor goal would increase the appeal to give as well as convey the message that we’re wanting to send to the users. By adding a sticky bar on the family donation page we found that it will increase conversions. Below is what we found for the SOHL audience
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a sticky bar containing a donor goal to the sohl donation page for the CYE campaign resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, which means the results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on the results. It may be beneficial to conduct further tests or experiments to confirm the effectiveness of adding a sticky bar to the donation page. Additionally, it could be helpful to explore other strategies or elements that could potentially increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Will adding a sticky bar to the top of a donation page reinforce the need for users to give?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in a sticky bar that has specific donor goal language for the monthly family campaign giving page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the sticky bar will reinforce the need as well as add exclusivity to the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2024 - 12/28/2024
For this test we wanted to see if adding a sticky bar to the campaign donation page that contains donor goal language would increase the donor conversion rate on the page. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a sticky bar with specific donor goal language to the monthly family campaign donation page resulted in a significant increase in Revenue for All Traffic by 397.1% with a 91.0% level of confidence.

2. There was also a 48% increase in Transactions - One-Time with a 96% level of confidence.

3. While the results were positive, the statistical confidence level of 91.0% for Revenue for All Traffic falls slightly below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity.

4. Further testing is recommended to confirm the effectiveness of the sticky bar in increasing donor conversion rates.

5. Future experimentation should focus on refining the donor goal language in the sticky bar, testing different designs or placements, and running the experiment for a longer duration to ensure reliable results.

6. It is important to continue testing and refining strategies to optimize donor conversion rates and maximize fundraising efforts for the nonprofit "Focus on the Family".

experiments.title: What message resonates more “give hope” or “deliver hope”
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy from “giving hope” to “deliver hope” for the homepage wiki header will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because while the language is slightly different the tangibility between deliver and give is significant.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/04/2024 - 11/21/2024
Each high-urgency campaign FOTF pushes a wiki header on their homepage. When the campaign first started we wanted to make sure that the language that we used in it was the most optimized that it could be going into our biggest testing season. For this we wanted to see if “give hope” or “deliver hope” resonates more with their audience. While the actual message itself is very similar, we were able to see a massive difference when it comes to making a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy from "giving hope" to "deliver hope" for the homepage wiki header resulted in a significant increase in Revenue for All Traffic, specifically a 134.6% increase. However, the statistical confidence level of 87.8% falls slightly below the typical threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it would be important to continue testing different language variations to validate the initial positive outcome. Additionally, considering the notable increase in revenue seen with the change from "giving hope" to "deliver hope," it might be beneficial to explore further tests that focus on emphasizing the tangible impact and action-oriented language to drive donor conversion rates. Ultimately, this experiment highlights the importance of continuously testing and refining messaging to maximize effectiveness in engaging with the audience and achieving campaign goals.

experiments.title: Will Adding a Lower Third Ad vs a Sticky Bar Perform Better on the Today in the Word Homepage During Calendar Year End?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in a lower third ad for calendar year end on the homepage will achieve greater donor conversion because we’re introducing a novel web element that contains more gift options to interact with during critical giving days of the year.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2024 - 01/01/2025
During campaigns for Today in the Word, we often use a sticky bar message on the homepage to iterate a need and a campaign goal. After several campaigns, the sticky bar can get a little stale after several uses. We want to know what would happen if we introduced a lower third gift banner that scrolls up the page (vs sitting at the top) is helpful as a NEW element to interact with during a season of highly motivated giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a lower third gift banner on the homepage during critical giving days can lead to a significant increase in donations. The treatment group experienced a 21.6% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control group. This indicates that introducing a new web element, such as a lower third ad, can capture the attention of donors and provide more gift options for interaction, ultimately boosting conversions.

Moving forward, it is important to consider the success of this experiment and use it as a basis for future experimentation. The results suggest that innovative web elements can have a positive impact on donor conversion rates, especially during crucial giving periods. Therefore, incorporating new and interactive features on the website, such as lower third banners, could potentially drive more donations in the future.

However, since the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, further testing is recommended to confirm the validity of these results. Conducting additional experiments with different variations of web elements and analyzing their impact on donor behavior could provide more insight and help fine-tune strategies for maximizing donations during campaigns.

experiments.title: How exclusivity language on a high-urgency pop-up impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including exclusivity language for users on the RTB website will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it highlights the donor’s identification with a group, beyond just being a donor..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
For RTB’s first-ever Giving Day, we hypothesized that the inclusion of exclusivity language — highlighting that the donor is joining a unique or exclusive group of donors to the first RTB Giving Day — will improve conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including exclusivity language on the RTB website did lead to a significant increase in donor conversion rate. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 68.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for valid results.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. Additionally, it would be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results.

While the initial findings are promising and suggest that exclusivity language may indeed impact donor conversion rate positively, we should not make any definitive conclusions or implement permanent changes based on these results alone. Further testing with a larger sample size and longer duration is needed to validate the impact of exclusivity language on donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How using a pop-up impacts donations for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a pop-up for a specific offer for general website visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2022 - 11/04/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that primarily generated revenue through monthly premium offers. Typically premium offers will have multiple giving levels that include bundles of various or multiple premiums.
For the month of October 2022, the premium was a brand-new book written by Dr. Jeffress titled, “18 Minutes with Jesus.” Dr. Jeffress traveled the country and did multiple interviews for this premium so the extra traffic provided an opportunity for us to run an A/B/C test.
One of our homepage experiments focused on whether using a pop-up promoting the new book would make an impact on donor conversion rates. Our hypothesis was that with all the traffic being driven to the website by TV and radio promos, having the offer be front and center would increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While using a pop-up helped increase donation conversion rates across the entire site, when drilling down into donations for the specific 18 Minutes with Jesus offer, the results were different. Overall, using the pop-up resulted in a 12% decrease in donor conversion rates with a 100% level of confidence, but it also had a 8.9% increase in revenue with a 83% level of confidence.

Since the number of sessions was not equally weighted, this experiment requires further refinement in the next iteration.

experiments.title: How humanizing an Anti-Paywall pop-up effects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We anticipate that integrating an anti-paywall pop-up onto the WUNC homepage will inspire prospects to consider donating. By presenting them with a clear value proposition by an actual person and reminding them of WUNC’s commitment to providing free content, we aim to instill a sense of reciprocity and encourage support for the station.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2024 - 06/24/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, aimed to assess the impact of implementing an anti-paywall pop-up featuring a personalized message and photo of an employee on the homepage for new website visitors. The primary goal was to determine if this approach could effectively boost donation rates by immediately engaging visitors upon entry and potentially fostering greater support for WUNC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the implementation of an anti-paywall pop-up featuring a personalized message and photo of an employee on the WUNC homepage did not lead to an increase in donation rates. In fact, the treatment group actually saw a 21.3% decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence for these results is 84.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%.
Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. It is important to continue experimenting with different strategies and approaches to engage website visitors and encourage donations. Future experiments should focus on testing a variety of tactics, such as different messaging, timing of pop-ups, and targeting specific segments of website visitors, to determine what truly resonates and drives donation conversions. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure a high level of statistical confidence in the results before drawing conclusions and making decisions based on the findings of any experiment.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency in a sticky bar impacted conversion rates during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a visible countdown deadline for the homepage sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it will increase the motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2023 - 12/31/2023
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could increase donor conversion rate by adding a sense of urgency. To do this we added a countdown timer to a sticky bar to give a deadline.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of a visible countdown deadline to the homepage sticky bar resulted in a 21.8% increase in donations for all traffic.

experiments.title: How yes/no “autonomy” language on a recurring ask pop-up impacts recurring conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in recurring conversion rate because  the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for the gift. because  the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/05/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we hypothesized that “yes/no” autonomy language would provide the user with a micro-decision therefore increasing the donor conversion rate.
The control features the standard single call-to-action. Whereas, the treatment offers the call-to-action to donate as well as a “No, I’m not interest option.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors led to a significant increase in recurring donation conversions. The treatment group saw a 126.1% increase in donations for all traffic at an 87.8% level of confidence.
Although this experiment did not validate, the results suggest that The Navigators audience appreciates the autonomy provided by both a "yes" and "no" option, and in turn, they are more likely to give a gift.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of autonomy language to see if the trend of increased conversion rates holds true. Additionally, exploring other ways to empower visitors and make them feel in control of their actions could further enhance donation outcomes for The Navigator's audience.

experiments.title: How a more generalized ask in a sticky bar for Giving Tuesday impacted click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing the message in terms of a broader impact will resonate with more people and lead to a higher click-through rate because it is more emotionally engaging.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 11/28/2023
On Giving Tuesday, Dallas Theological Seminary made a concerted effort to acquire more monthly donors, called Cornerstone Partners. They’ve typically taken an approach that outlines the number of new Cornerstone Partners needed before the midnight deadline.
This year, they wanted to see if a more general ask would lead to higher click-through rates because the goal number could be perceived as overwhelming for someone reading.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment version of the message, which framed the ask in terms of a broader impact, did not lead to a higher click-through rate compared to the control version. In fact, the treatment version saw a significant decrease in clicks by 61.1% with a 93.1% level of confidence.
These results suggest that the hypothesis was incorrect and that framing the message in terms of a broader impact may not resonate with more people or be more emotionally engaging. Therefore, it may not be an effective strategy for increasing click-through rates and acquiring more monthly donors.
In future experimentation, it would be necessary to further test different messaging strategies to determine what resonates most with the target audience. It is important to take into account the specific characteristics and motivations of the donors to understand what messaging would be most compelling to them.
Additionally, it would be advisable to conduct experiments with larger sample sizes and run the experiment for a longer period to increase the statistical validity of the results. This would help ensure that the findings are more reliable and generalizable.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing different messaging approaches and continuously refining strategies to optimize donor engagement and acquisition.

experiments.title: How unique value propositions impacted clicks for a direct ask sticky bar
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the giving motivation of equipping the next generation of Christian leaders for website visitors will achieve an increase in click conversions because that value will resonate the strongest with our audience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 06/30/2023
DTS is amid their fiscal year end fundraising campaign. With 15 days remaining, we want to add a website sticky bar as an additional revenue opportunity. We’ve built two different sticky bars unique in value proposition. The control uses the value proposition of “reaching lives across the globe with the truth of the Bible” and the control used the value proposition of “equipping the next generation of Christian leaders.” We want to see which value resonates the strongest with our website audience and generates the most clicks to the FYE donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment sticky bar resulted in a 33.3% decrease in clicks with a nearly valid 90.6% level of confidence. The treatment produced 22 clicks compared to 52 to the control.
We noticed the treatment heading in the wrong direction so we ended the experiment after 11 days in order to send 100% of traffic to the control variant.
It seems the control value proposition of "reaching lives across the globe with the truth of the Bible" resonates the most with our online audience. There are other factors to consider with the experiment, however.
The treatment led with "SPECIAL OPPORTUNITY" while the control led with "DEADLINE APPROACHING". Perhaps the control was more effective in this case as it notified users of an important deadline immediately. The treatment lead-in is much more broad which could lead users to overlook it or move casually past it. Also worth noting is the treatment mentioned the Challenge Gift amount of $600,000 while the control only referenced the Challenge Gift without stating the amount.
It's possible that including the $600k amount turned visitors off as they may have felt as if their modest gift would not make a significant difference in the overall goal.
These are all isolated elements that would be worth testing into to get more refined results. Though, in this high urgency scenario, we wanted to act fast and get our best two sticky bars copy versions out to the public.

experiments.title: How personalizing a sticky bar with an on-air personality impacted donations during a high-urgency campaign
