experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing a sticky bar with an on-air personality for pledge donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/04/2023
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. Since a lot of web traffic during pledge drives is driven by offline sources (mainly radio) we wanted to see whether personalizing a sticky bar with a quote/image from a specific on-air personality, who would be on the air during a specific time and day, would make an impact on overall online donations.
We ran this as a 3-way test, with a control, sticky bar with quote but no image, and sticky bar with quote and image of on-air personality.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to test whether personalizing a sticky bar with a specific on-air personality would impact overall online donations for WUNC, a nonprofit NPR station. The treatment showed a 13.4% increase in donations for all traffic, with an 87.6% statistical level of confidence, indicating that personalizing the sticky bar had a positive effect on donor conversion rates. However, the results are not entirely valid and require further testing to ensure the intended impact.
While results varied from day-to-day, the overall lift that adding a sticky bar to the homepage during the pledge drive was impactful, with an 87% level of confidence that adding one would help increase overall donations.
Interestingly enough, while the image treatment version had a slightly lower conversion rate vis-a-vis the impact on overall online donations, when digging into the directly-attributed results, the treatment version had 6 gifts compared to the no image version of 3 gifts.
Further testing is definitely warranted, as is trying to quantify the connection between offline (radio) and online traffic.

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button on article pages with a slide-out donor appeal impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky donate button with a slide-out CTA featuring additional value proposition copy will increase donor conversion by strengthening the value proposition of the sticky donate button.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/23/2023 - 03/31/2023
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. They had previously tested a sticky donate button on article pages that floated on the right-hand side of the page with a pulsing heart and linked to the main donation page. This sticky button improved donor conversion. They wanted to test a new version of that button with an expanded value proposition that would appear in a slide-out when the button was clicked. This they posited would enable visitors to receive a reason to donate before visiting the donation page itself. Visitors could then choose to give or dismiss the message altogether.
For the experiment, they wanted to test two different value propositions. One would focus on FamilyLife’s standard appeal message about making a gift to support families in crisis. The other would focus on the perceived value the visitor received from FamilyLife this year and ask them to make a gift to keep FamilyLife here for them and their family.
Originally these two variations were going to be tested against the prior winning sticky donate button that lacked a slide-out CTA message. Due to a setup error, the control experience lacked a sticky donate button altogether, so it became an experiment between these two competing value proposition slide-outs and no sticky donate button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that inviting visitors to reflect on the value FamilyLife provided them and then asking them to donate to keep FamilyLife there for them and their family was far more motivational than asking them to make a gift to help other families. We hypothesize this is because it is harder for a potential donor to think about the needs of unidentified "families in critical need" than it is to remember their needs and how FamilyLife has been their for them.
We will continue testing this value proposition to further refine it and identify key motivational elements for donor conversion. Our next test will be this slide-out CTA sticky button against the former control experience of just a sticky donate button without a slide-out message.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a high urgency campaign website sticky bar impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to sticky bar copy promoting a key fundraising opportunity for website visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the value proposition will appeal more to potential donors and motivate them to learn more and make a gift through the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2023 - 03/31/2023
The Illinois Policy Institute was running a high urgency campaign through the end of March. To alert website visitors and get more people to the campaign donation page, we’re testing two sticky bars: One that is very brief and simply alerts users of an urgent matching gift and campaign deadline, and the other includes more value proposition copy, appealing to issues that the user likely cares about and telling them how they can learn more about a way to help – while also pointing out the urgency of a deadline.
Which angle will produce more donations?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value proposition to the sticky bar copy can lead to a significant increase in donations. The treatment version that included more value proposition copy saw a 304.6% increase in donations, with a statistical level of confidence of 94.4%.

All donations completed in this experiment were completed by desktop visitors and returning visitors. It is clear that the treatment sticky bar (with value prop) spoke more effectively to visitors through appeal and motivation. A "matching gift" does not mean as much to our audience as improving certain issues that they are passionate about.

Future sticky bars run for high urgency campaigns should include value proposition language, as that's what our audience wants to hear. It may also be worth testing variations of the treatment version to further optimize the messaging and achieve an even greater increase in donations.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar impacted donations after a course sign up
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar encouraging support for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the personalized message from a prominent and relatable figure will inspire them to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2023 - 03/12/2023
Dallas Theological Seminary’s Galatians course reroutes users to a donation page after they sign up for the course. This donation page is experiencing a lower than expected donation conversion rate, so we want to test adding a humanized sticky bar to the top of the donation page in hopes that it will effectively nudge more people to make a donation.
The sticky bar messaging includes a thank you for signing up and kindly asks the page visitor to stick around to view a special message about how they can help more people get access to free bible study courses like the one they just signed up for. It also includes a picture and a signature of the organization president and course instructor implying that the message is being stated by that person.
We’ve seen encouraging results for the humanized sticky bar for various organizations so we want to see if it is successful in this instance in increasing donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment resulted in a 95.5% lift in donations at an 84.1% level of confidence. The results confirm our hypothesis that the personalized message from a prominent and relatable figure would inspire visitors to donate and increase donor conversion rate. It is also important to consider the person speaking through the humanized sticky bar. In this case, users likely appreciate that the message is coming not only from the president of the organization but also the course instructor - the person they will be engaging with as they learn more about Galatians.
The key learning from this experiment is that creating a personalized and humanized experience for potential donors can significantly increase donor conversion rates. The sticky bar separates the page from standard pages - the sticky bar stands out as a unique feature that catches the users attention right away and amplifies the donation value proposition. This treatment should be tested with other course donation pages.

experiments.title: How humanizing an appeal by including a specific child’s name, age, and image on a slider affects conversion rates

rows.name: Slider with specific child details (name, age, photo)

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding specific child details to the micro goal popup on the sponsorship list page for potential donors will achieve an increase in sponsorship conversion rates because we are mentioning an individual child by name instead of a certain number of children.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/05/2022 - 05/16/2022
When a user enters the child list sponsorship page experience on all devices they will either see a slider with a certain number of kids in need of sponsorship or a specific child’s details that they could sponsor. We wanted to see if this personalized approach would increase potential donors desire to sponsor a child.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped after two weeks due to large declines in donor conversion.
• Donor conversion was lower for the variant (-19%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (86%).
• Revenue per visitor was lower for the variant (-25%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (89%).
• We recommend not changing the sponsorship slider to include specific child details as they may not match the particular child a donor would choose to sponsor.

experiments.title: How adding impact language to a direct-ask pop up banner during a high urgency campaign affects revenue – marriages saved V2 relaunch
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language on the gift array of a direct ask pop-up to show the impact of the gift will result in greater revenue
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2022 - 12/11/2022
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They provide help and resources for couples to build healthy marriages that reflect God’s design and for parents to raise their children according to morals and values grounded in biblical principles. Every year during the calendar year-end high-urgency campaign, FOTF places a top-of-page pop-up banner that contains a direct ask to support their mission. This banner (known internally as the “wiki-banner”) has been tested into over many years, with experiments to improve donation metrics. Since many people visit FOTF.com during this season to make a year-end gift, the “wiki-banner” serves as a low-friction tactic to get people to the donation page quickly. It is important to note that the visiting audience to FOTF’s website is served different content / experiences based on pages they have consumed in the past…specifically a “marriage audience” and an “in favor of pro-life audience – Santicity of Human Life” (also known as SOHL).
For this specific experiment targeting the “marriage audience”, we were focused on changing the language on the gift array buttons to reflect the impact that a gift would make on the amount of marriages saved. The theory was that if we could directly show how many marriages could be saved with a specific gift amount, the visitor would be moved to give a bigger gift to make a greater impact. As screen real-estate is at a premium for this “wiki-banner”, we designed an experience that showed the number of marriages saved on a button on the wiki-banner, and in a “hover-state” it would flip to the equivalent dollar amount of the gift. We even added a free-form field that would allow you to add how many marriages you wanted to save, and it would calculate that amount in real-time. After making a selection, the user is directed to the donation form where the amount they chose is auto-populated on the giving form, to reduce friction. It is also important to note that we were testing for revenue lift on desktop only (not mobile due to screen size limitations).
NOTE: This is the second version of the experiment (V2 relaunch) due to technical challenges of the V1 version. That write-up can be found here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
If you notice, this experiment is labeled V2 relaunch. We launched this one AFTER the V1 version that we ended on 12/2 due to internal FOTF technical challenges. This version ran from 12/2 to 12/11. While this experiment was set up correctly, it was also running in parallel with a similar experiment that was targeted to only the SOHL audience. Unfortunately, challenges with the data capture for that experiment (linked here) were resulting in no returns. The design of the experiment was to run in parallel with the SOHL version, so that we could see the effect on both audiences seperately. We made the decision to end this experiment early on 12/11, so that we could determine what needed to be fixed on the SOHL version, so that we could relaunch both experiments at the same time, and accurately record data on both at the same time.

All of that said, we were seeing similar results (although not as drastic) to the V1 experiment before we ended this experiment. Revunue lift for V2 was around 55% with an 86% Level of Confidence. Even when we removed outlier donations over $2k from both the control and the treatment, we still saw a significant lift from the treatment.

As with the V1 version of this experiment, we did not feel comfortable relying on these results to make any future recommendations other than to re-build the experiment as it was, and quickly re-launch (a second time) to see if the results continued to show a positive lift in revenue through the remainder of the year-end campaign. Results from V3 will be shown in a future write-up and linked here.

experiments.title: How adding impact language to a direct-ask pop up banner during a high urgency campaign affects revenue – marriages saved V3 second relaunch
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language on the gift array of a direct ask pop-up to show the impact of the gift will result in greater revenue
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2022 - 12/31/2022
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They provide help and resources for couples to build healthy marriages that reflect God’s design and for parents to raise their children according to morals and values grounded in biblical principles. Every year during the calendar year-end high-urgency campaign, FOTF places a top-of-page pop-up banner that contains a direct ask to support their mission. This banner (known internally as the “wiki-banner”) has been tested into over many years, with experiments to improve donation metrics. Since many people visit FOTF.com during this season to make a year-end gift, the “wiki-banner” serves as a low-friction tactic to get people to the donation page quickly. It is important to note that the visiting audience to FOTF’s website is served different content / experiences based on pages they have consumed in the past…specifically a “marriage audience” and an “in favor of pro-life audience – Santicity of Human Life” (also known as SOHL).
For this specific experiment targeting the “marriage audience”, we were focused on changing the language on the gift array buttons to reflect the impact that a gift would make on the amount of marriages saved. The theory was that if we could directly show how many marriages could be saved with a specific gift amount, the visitor would be moved to give a bigger gift to make a greater impact. As screen real-estate is at a premium for this “wiki-banner”, we designed an experience that showed the number of marriages saved on a button on the wiki-banner, and in a “hover-state” it would flip to the equivalent dollar amount of the gift. We even added a free-form field that would allow you to add how many marriages you wanted to save, and it would calculate that amount in real-time.  After making a selection, the user is directed to the donation form where the amount they chose is auto-populated on the giving form, to reduce friction. It is also important to note that we were testing for revenue lift on desktop only (not mobile due to screen size limitations).
NOTE: This is the third version of the experiment (V3 relaunch) due to technical challenges of the V2 version. That write-up can be found here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
If you notice, this experiment is labeled V3 relaunch. We launched this one AFTER the V2 version that we ended on 12/11 due to internal FOTF technical challenges. This version ran from 12/11 to 12/31. This experiment was running in parallel with a similar experiment that was targeted to only the SOHL audience. Unlike the previous 2 versions of this experiment, the results showed a decrease in the revenue by around 9% with an 82% Level of Confidence.
While previous versions of this experiment showed a positive lift (even validating in the V1 version), this one showed a decrease, but not until the final days of the experiment. As we experienced so many technical challenges with launching this experiment, we kept a watchful eye on the results on a daily basis. From 12/11 through 12/29, the treatment was actually leading in revenue….but on the last 2 days of the year, the opposite actually happened.
This led us to even more questions like...
Does the timing of the experiment change the results? Did donors in “non-peak” times get inspired to give more based on messaging?Were the donors in the last 2 days motivated to give regardless of messaging?Was the change in messaging more of a friction point in the last 2 days of the year?Would it be better to change messaging in the wiki based on the urgency and timing of the campaign? (Ex., show more impactful language earlier in the campaign, then make the most urgent times as simple and frictionless as possible)
There were several key take-aways that came from this series of experiments.
Timing/motivation may play a big role in the effectiveness of impact languageMake sure you are always monitoring your experiments for data anomalies, and don't be afraid to end an experiment early and re-launch if the data does not "look right"We cannot say definitively that the impact language led to great revenue during the majority of the high-urgency campaign...but we can say that there is enough data to show a positive trend and learnings that we will take into our next high-urgency campaign experiment

experiments.title: How showing a reminder pop up to users that abandoned the donation process affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing a pop up reminder to complete a donation for people that have closed out a donation page, without making a donation will achieve a higher amount of revenue than allowing users to remember on their own.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2022 - 09/30/2022
Focus on the Family wanted to see if showing a pop-up with an opportunity to complete a donation (from a previously clicked on amount), would cause users to complete their transaction…instead of abandoning their donation all-together. This experiment was based on abandoned cart functionality often seen in the e-commerce space, but seldom used in the NPO space. Their current donation platform does not natively allow for this functionality, so we built a test using the user’s last click on a donation amount (on an abandoned or closed-out donation page) to launch a pop up with that amount, asking the user to complete their transaction with a simple nudge.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment ran for 41 days. While it did not reach statistical significance (only an 87% Level of Confidence), we did have some very interesting learnings. Since we were testing for revenue lift, we wanted to be sure the data was not skewed by abnormally large donation amounts from either the control or the treatment. FOTF often gets large donations that can skew a revenue experiment if we do not normalize the data. After we ran the experiment, we created a custom Google Analytics segment that only counted donations that were less than $1k in the sample size. After doing so, we noticed that the revenue for the treatment was significantly higher - an 85.2% lift in revenue. This took the average gift of this new sample group from $75.54 to $131.86. It is to be noted that prior to removing the gifts over $1k, the control had a sample size much closer to the treatment. Did this weigh into the final results? Are the motivations different for people that come to the main site vs coming from an article page or elsewhere? WHile this test provided some exciting potential lifts, it is still unclear if motivations based on entry point could skew this data. The next step in the process will be to run the same experiment, but split into two different ones, and test if the revenue lift is better or worse when directly visiting via the main page or visiting instead through a secondary page.

experiments.title: How a “complete your gift” popup during a Fiscal Year End Campaign affects donor revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a “complete your gift” popup targeting visitors who choose a gift amount but abandon the donation page will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2022 - 09/04/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. For their august Fiscal Year-End campaign, they were seeing a decrease in donor revenue and we wanted to see if we could turn that around in the final days of the campaign. We decided to test a popup what would show if a visitor visited any donation page on their site and chose a gift amount but then abandoned the page before competing their donation. This popup would then fire when they went anywhere else on the site and offer them the prompt to complete their X gift or indicate they weren’t ready to do so. If they chose to, it would reload the donation page so they could complete the transaction.
We split the abandoned donation page traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for about 11 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We were only able to record an extremely marginal impact on donor conversion for the popup experience, however the impact on revenue was found in all primary channels to the website. While we were just under a 95% LOC for all traffic, we found a similar increase on all other channels including a 41% increase for direct traffic at a 97% LOC.
The popup reduced friction for these donors, causing them to give a higher amount when they revisited the donation page. Since this ran during the Fiscal Year End campaign, we want to relaunch it in September to measure impact without a high urgency campaign skewing results one way or another.
Other revenue impact measured included:
Social visitors had a 77.0% increase in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 20.7% increase in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 13.8% increase in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 16.6% increase in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 17.9% increase in revenue with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar impacted donor conversions on an acquisition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a digital acquisition donation page for prospective donors will achieve a change in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2022 - 08/01/2022
Luther Seminary was offering a free online course called Cosmic Adventure that explained the relationship between science and faith including the history behind this relationship. They offered this free course through paid Facebook ads and to their housefile through email marketing. This offer was running for almost a year and the conversion rate on the donation page was on a steep decline.
For this experiment, we wanted to see whether adding a humanized sticky bar, featuring a message and image of Andy Root, Professor at Luther Seminary and Chair of Youth and Family Ministry, would have an impact on donor conversion rates from prospective donors coming from this digital acquisition offer.
The message tried to focus the prospective donor’s attention on why they should make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding in a humanized sticky bar actually led to a decrease in conversion rates of 61%, but with only an 86.7% level of confidence. We hypothesize that this is due to the nature of who we included in the messaging, as well as the messaging itself. While Andy Root is well known in his community at Luther Seminary, he might not be that well known to those prospective donors who come onto the file through digital acquisition efforts--they are downloading the content because it appeals to them, and not necessarily because of who it's coming from.
Further testing on messaging and subjects will be done in the future to see if we can get a lift.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 59.9% decrease with a 91% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How humanizing a sticky bar impacts donor conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing a sticky bar for site visitors  will achieve a high donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2022 - 05/21/2022
The Navigators were running a high urgency campaign for Eagle Lake camps. In order to drive more traffic to the campaign’s donation page, we wanted to implement a site-wide sticky bar. Their team member, Abby Anderson, would be the sender of these emails so we wanted to user her likeness (image and signature) to humanize the sticky bar message. People would be hearing from her from both emails and the site which would create consistency, familiarity, and would would make site visitors feel like they’re hearing from an actual person. Our hypothesis is that the humanized sticky bar would result in an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 50% directional decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 89%. Though this experiment did not validate, we have sufficient data to hypothesize that The Navigators' audience are not motivated to give by seeing a humanized sticky bar. It's also difficult to discern what percentage of site visitors were actually receiving the campaign emails. That means that particular group of people would not have been with familiar with Abby as an email sender. For future tests, it would be beneficial to retest this concept but use the picture of a student who needs to be sponsored for Eagle Lake Camps. Giving the prospective donor a visual of who they would actually be helping (or someone like this student), would humanize the content further without needing to know a specific Navigators staff member.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to sticky bar messaging impacted donations for a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and seasonally relevant messaging for website visitors will achieve more calendar year-end donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2021 - 01/04/2022
During a calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test various messaging on a sticky bar to see what would most resonate with donors to get them to the donation page to make a gift. We had a general control message that prompted a gift to ‘share resources with believers’ and mentioned the upcoming deadline. The first treatment added clarity around what the donor’s gift would accomplish – highlighting that each dollar would impact 10 people. The second treatment posed a question to introduce donor autonomy – will you help us? The third treatment was the same message, but appeared at the bottom of the screen rather than the top of the page. We split traffic to all 4 versions for site visitors during the last week of the year.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not reach statistical significance since the experiment was time-bound and only ran for a short period of time, what we did find is that the language that clearly outlined the impact of each dollar given seemed to be moving more donors to conversion. This will need to be tested again outside of a high urgency campaign window for more clear results, but we can find additional ways to incorporate this language into the value proposition for future donor communications.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 4.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 5.1% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 163.7% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 78.0% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 5.8% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How prequalifying stickybar audiences to a campaign impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that buyer language used on-site to promote a campaign for web visitors will achieve increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/25/2021
The Fund for American Studies was running on-site stickybar and home page popup promotions for their Founders’ Day Campaign and wanted to experiment with whether “selling the click” or “selling the donation” would be most effective for their audience visiting the website.
We decided to call these treatments “shopper” language (to sell the click) and “buyer” language (to sell the post-click donation).
experiments.keyLearnings:
With the deadline approaching for the campaign, we decided to take the version of the stickybar/popups that was showing a lead for the remaining few days.

We did observe some other statistics that are worth noting:
Ad clickthrough rate on the "buyer" version of the ad copy did see valid decrease during the valuation period — showing 39.1% less clicks (LoC: 99%).
 	However, because the fewer people clicking on the "buyer" version of the ad did convert (as shown above) at a higher rate, we observed an increase in donor conversion rate after they clicked the on-site ads.
 	Combining more donors (as noted previously), the "buyer" version of the ad produced a more generous donor to the campaign — showing an increase of +18.4% increase in the size of the average gift.
 	Because of that higher donor total and a higher average gift, the revenue for the "buyer" ads actually was a lift of +183% (LoC: 93.6%).

experiments.title: How adding a pulsing star icon to a sticky bar impacted clicks

rows.name: Sticky Bar Free Trial w/ Testimonial
Sticky Bar Free Trial w/ Star& Pulsing Button

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a pulsing icon for website visitors will achieve increased clicks on the sticky bar.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2020 - 10/12/2020
As we looked to increase clicks on a sticky bar to begin a free trial, we developed a treatment that added a pulsing star icon to the sticky bar. All other elements remained the same. We wanted to emphasize this as the primary call to action for website visitors since there are multiple buttons on the page with additional calls to action. We hypothesized that adding an animated element to this specific button would help it stand out more on the page as the preferred call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking across all traffic, we did not validate results on the experiment. However, we did see that email visitors had a 272.3% increase in begin free trial signup with a 97% level of confidence, while all other segments indicated a directional decrease. This could be a result of the consistency between the messaging communicated via email and the corresponding offer on the page. We'll continue to do additional testing to introduce other elements to drive free trial signups for additional segments.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Paid Search visitors had a 16.4% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 91% level of confidence.
 	All Traffic visitors had a 9.9% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Desktop visitors had a 14.7% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 24.4% decrease in begin free trial signup with an 88% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 13.1% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How communal language on a high-urgency campaign sticky bar affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language will increase revenue during a high urgency campaigns.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2021 - 05/30/2021
FamilyLife was running a matching gift campaign with a focus on recurring giving. Since communal language—copy that shows the reader that they are united with the organization in the most important beliefs and causes that they care for, all spoken with a familiarity of friends—works well for other faith-based organizations. We’d seen lifts of donations and revenue during high-urgency campaign and wanted to test if this would benefit FamilyLife as well. We tested a 50/50 split in traffic on their website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, there was a directional lift in revenue. This did not fully validate because of the 23% decrease in revenue for new visitors that pulled down the full validation of a 59% increase in revenue for returning visitors.
Our hypothesis is that communal language, invoking the tone of friendship and care, works very well with returning visitors because they are more closely acquainted with the organization. Whereas a intimate tone with a new acquaintance will not resonate.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Organic Search visitors had a 21.5% increase in revenue with an 86% level of confidence.Paid Search visitors had a 57.7% decrease in revenue with a 98% level of confidence.Referral visitors had an 89.5% increase in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a personalized sticky bar on a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalizing the sticky bar on the donation page increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2019 - 06/08/2019
CaringBridge had run experiments to show that inserting a “sticky bar” at the top of donation pages could increase conversion rate during one of their matching gift appeal. However, they had recently updated their site code to be able to personalize the messages on the sticky bar to match the person’s donation page that the bar was running on. They wanted to test the impact of this during a match appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The personalized version of the sticky bar increased conversion by 10.1%, though it did not reach full statistical validity because it only ran for two days of the match period. This is a good directional learning, though it is muddied by another result—when this test was re-run later in the second match, it decreased conversion by 9.7%, also not statistically valid.
Ultimately, this might be too small of a change to validate a consistent result. However, the sticky bar has continued to generate increases in conversions across the board, especially on mobile.

experiments.title: How removing an exit-intent pop-up on a donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the exit-intent pop-up impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/02/2018 - 01/26/2018
On Focus on the Family’s primary donation page, they use an exit-intent pop-up that appears on the page as you are about to leave. During their year-end giving campaign, we tested removing this feature. We found that by removing the exit-intent pop-up, we were able to increase donor conversion because the audience was highly motivated to give. Understanding these learnings, we also wanted to test this approach outside of year-end when motivations were more neutral. During the month of January, we tested removing the exit-intent pop-up from the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After four weeks of running this experiment and analyzing the data, we found that having an exit-intent pop-up on the primary donation page doesn't significantly impact conversion - positively or negatively outside of high-urgency campaigns. The conversion rate throughout the month fluctuated week by week telling us that there is no real difference in conversion with an exit-intent pop-up.

experiments.title: Does having a notable figure’s presence in Spring Share content drive more donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that featuring Karl Clauson for Moody Radio Slide out visitors during Spring Share will have no impact on engagement or downstream donor metrics.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2026 - 03/02/2026
To measure the impact of featuring Moody Bible Institute President Dr. Mark Jobe’s transition from leadership, we tested whether highlighting a different staff member would affect user engagement and downstream metrics.
The test ran for approximately 2 weeks, with traffic split evenly between the 2 variants. We tracked clicks and gifts to evaluate the overall performance and impact of each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Karl Clauson's feature on Moody Radio's Spring Share slide-out yielded encouraging early signals:
Clicks: +11.1% (59.8% confidence)
 	Transactions (all traffic): +35.3% (69.7% confidence)
Both results are positive, but do not meet the 95% significance needed for firm conclusions.
Looking Ahead:
Early signs suggest Karl Clauson's presence may boost engagement and giving. More testing is needed to confirm this.
Next steps:
Running the experiment longer or expanding the sample size to build confidence
 	Review for biases or other factors that may affect the results

experiments.title: How an exit intent with a thermometer and an exit intent with social proof affects donor conversion rate during Calendar Year End
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an exit intent with a donor goal thermometer image and social proof image for site profile visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because for the donor goal thermometer version, the image would highlight what the user could do to fill the gap, and the social proof image would show how the user could make an impact along with the other people that have already made an impact, encouraging the user to help CB meet the match.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/09/2026
During their 2-day matches, CaringBridge usually has an exit intent that appears on-site profiles, featuring language that reflects the urgency of the match and how the user can help CaringBridge reach its goal.
During this particular match at the end of the calendar year, we wanted to try an A/B/C test with different exit intents that used varying visuals on the exit intent.
The first treatment, which had an exit intent, featured a thermometer image, showing how the user could help fill the gap.
The second treatment, exit intent, featured an image of people, highlighting how they could help reach the goal among the community of people who had already contributed.
This was tested on both mobile and desktop devices, as well as on-site profiles.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an exit intent with a donor goal thermometer image and social proof image for site profile visitors can potentially increase donor conversion rate. The treatment showed an 8.2% increase in donations for all traffic, indicating that the visuals used in the exit intent may have had a positive impact on user engagement.

There was an increase in revenue for both mobile and desktop, which indicated that the social proof image variant was a clear winner. For desktop, there was a 19.37% increase in revenue. For mobile, there was a 24% increase in revenue.

Control (desktop) - $7,208

Treatment 1(desktop) - $7,515

Treatment 2 (desktop) - $8,770

Control (mobile) - $9,686

Treatment 1 (mobile) - $10,665

Treatment 2 (mobile) - $12,200

So the conclusion for this test is that while both variants produced an almost similar amount of donations, the variant with the social proof image encouraged users to give higher gifts, resulting in an overall increase in revenue.

experiments.title: Can a slide-down donation prompt outperform WUNC’s standard slide-up during CYE?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing a slide-down donation prompt against our standard slide-up for all homepage visitors would help identify whether a fresh, less familiar user experience could improve engagement and conversions during CYE because repeated exposure to the same slide-up format may be leading to creative fatigue, while a new slide-down interaction could draw renewed attention without major structural or messaging changes.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/25/2025 - 12/08/2025
WUNC is a public media organization serving central North Carolina, funded primarily through listener and reader support. As part of its 2025 year-end growth goals, WUNC focused on optimizing high-traffic homepage fundraising elements while minimizing risk during its most important revenue window.
This experiment, conducted during the CYE period, tested a modest UX variation to assess whether a refreshed interaction pattern could improve performance without altering the messaging or offer.
The team compared WUNC’s standard slide-up donation prompt, used across multiple drives, to a new slide-down version using the same gift array and copy. The test was prioritized to evaluate potential creative fatigue in the existing unit and to explore low-risk ways to diversify on-site fundraising experiences.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Big takeaway:

The existing slide-up outperformed the slide-down on donation conversion rate, winning directionally with 78% confidence. Results did not reach conventional statistical significance, and the experiment was ended early to prioritize year-end revenue.
📊 Performance Metrics (Primary KPI)
Donation conversion rate (donations / impressions):
Slide up: 26 / 4,370 = 0.60%
 	Slide down: 18 / 4,384 = 0.41%
 	Result: Slide-up leads at 78% confidence (two-proportion z-test; not statistically significant)
✂️ Donor Type Breakdowns (Directional only)
One-time donor conversion rate:
Slide up: 17 / 4,370 = 0.39%
 	Slide down: 12 / 4,384 = 0.27%
 	Confidence: <70% (not statistically valid)
Sustainer conversion rate:
Slide up: 9 / 4,370 = 0.21%
 	Slide down: 6 / 4,384 = 0.14%
 	Confidence: <70% (not statistically valid)
First-time donor conversion rate:
Slide up: 15 / 4,370 = 0.34%
 	Slide down: 11 / 4,384 = 0.25%
 	Confidence: <70% (not statistically valid)
📊 Secondary Metrics
Average gift:
Slide up: $94
 	Slide down: $84
 	Confidence: Not statistically evaluated (insufficient data on variance)
Revenue:
Slide up: $2,450
 	Slide down: $1,520
 	Revenue differences reflect overall conversion volume rather than gift size effects.
Next Steps

experiments.title: How Using Heritage Member Survey Insights to Shape Year-End Messaging Affects Donor Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using Heritage member survey insights to shape more urgent, action-oriented year-end messaging in the sitewide sticky bar will increase donor conversion rates because it reflects supporters’ strongest motivations—fear of wasting this moment and a desire to lock in permanent wins through accountability—making the appeal feel more relevant, concrete, and compelling at the point of decision.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2025 - 01/05/2026
This experiment took place during The Heritage Foundation’s calendar year-end fundraising campaign, when urgency, traffic volume, and donor intent were at their highest. It coincided with the release of new qualitative research from Heritage’s own audience, which showed deep anxiety about conservatives “blowing it” by failing to lock in recent political gains. Year-end provided a natural deadline, but the research made clear that what truly motivated this audience was not just time pressure—it was fear of losing momentum and allowing courts, bureaucrats, and future elections to reverse progress.
The sticky bar was tested sitewide on Heritage.org, ensuring persistent visibility across the full donor journey rather than limiting the message to a single landing page.
We wanted to run this experiment because the control message leaned heavily on a standard year-end frame—“give before midnight on December 31”—without fully addressing the deeper emotional drivers revealed in the research. Survey respondents repeatedly expressed fear that conservative leadership would waste a once-in-a-generation window and fail to codify wins. The treatment was designed to test whether explicitly framing a gift as a way to lock in victories, dismantle the deep state, and hold policymakers accountable would better align with those anxieties.
Finally, the sticky bar format itself made this a low-risk, high-impact test. It allowed us to introduce sharper, more militant language drawn directly from donor sentiment without rebuilding core pages mid-campaign. In essence, this experiment tested whether shifting from abstract urgency to permanence, accountability, and action would increase engagement during the most critical fundraising moment of the year.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The main takeaway from this experiment is that there was no meaningful difference between the control and treatment in overall transactions or sticky bar–driven clicks. Conversion rate remained flat at 2.4% across both variants, and while the treatment showed slight directional movement in sticky bar clicks, the difference was not statistically meaningful and did not translate into increased giving. Due to the small number of sticky bar–initiated conversions and low confidence levels, the results are not considered valid for drawing performance conclusions.
Moving forward, this test suggests that changing sticky bar messaging alone is unlikely to materially impact donor behavior during year-end, when most conversions are driven by higher-intent paths like email and direct visits. We will use this learning to deprioritize sticky bars as a primary lever for conversion optimization and instead apply survey-informed messaging insights to higher-impact surfaces—such as homepage heroes, landing pages, and email—where message framing has a greater opportunity to influence donor decisions and reach statistical validity.

experiments.title: How a slide-up versus a slide-down performs during a Giving Tuesday high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a different slide-up/down tactic for potential donors will lead to an improvement in donation conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2025 - 11/30/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of implementing a slide‑down banner instead of a slide‑up banner during a peak giving moment. We hypothesized that the slide‑down banner would be more visually prominent, capture user attention more effectively, and increase conversion. The slide-up banner was the control in this instance, as we had previously tested it during other time periods, and it proved to be the winner.
In this experiment, the slide-up appeared after the user started scrolling down the page, and the slide-down appeared once the visitor went to the homepage, remaining at the top of the page as the user scrolled. Users could minimize the slide-down banner if they wished; even when minimized, a donation array continued to display at the top of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results show a directional decrease in overall conversion and RPV. This suggests that the slide‑down banner may have been perceived as distracting and less visually appealing, ultimately leading to reduced conversion.

Key Results:

-       -10% non-statistically significant decrease (71%) in overall conversion rate.

-       -2% non-statistically significant decrease (54%) in overall RPV.

Overall analysis:

As show in the statistics above, the slide down treatment version had a 10% decrease in overall transaction conversion rates, with a 70% level of confidence. The treatment also had a slightly lower revenue per visitor, showing a 1.8% decrease with a 54% level of confidence.

 

Desktop versus mobile analysis:

Desktop:

Control had a 6.4% conversion rate

Treatment had a 6.1% conversion rate, which is only a 4.6% decrease with a 27% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor increased slightly on the treatment version, with a 10% increase and a 67% level of confidence in that result.

Mobile:

Control had a 4.6% conversion rate

Treatment had a 3.7% conversion rate, which is a 19% decrease with an 80% level of confidence. Moreover, revenue per visitor also declined on the treatment version by 39%, with a 97% level of confidence.

 

Recurring versus one-time analysis

Recurring:

Control: 0.69% conversion rate

Treatment: 0.71% conversion rate, which is a 3% increase with only an 8% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also was marginally impacted, with a 1% increase but only a 50% level of confidence.

 

One-time

Control: 4.8% conversion rate

Treatment: 4.2% conversion rate, which is a 12% decrease with a 75% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also marginally decreased by 1% with a 51% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How clarity about donor dollars on the homepage impacts engagement and downstream donor metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that transparent “100% of your gift” language for IMB homepage visitors will achieve an increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics because it establishes immediate trust in how donor dollars are used and creating a seamless message connection between the homepage and the giving page. because  it establishes immediate trust in how donor dollars are used and creating a seamless message connection between the homepage and the giving page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/14/2025
To strengthen IMB’s homepage value proposition and increase clicks to the donation page, we tested adding a statement to the homepage graphic that clearly explains where donor dollars are used.
This message about how gifts are applied was also featured on the main giving page, creating stronger congruency between the microdecision to click “Donate” and the experience donors encounter once they arrive on the giving page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using transparent language like "100% of your gift" did not yield the expected increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics. Despite the treatment version emphasizing how donor dollars are used, it actually resulted in a 13.2% decrease in clicks for all traffic compared to the control version.
Since the statistical level of confidence is only 68.3%, these results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making. Therefore, it is important not to draw conclusions or make changes based on these results alone.
However, was the treatment more effective at motivating donors?
The short answer: No.
The treatment produced a 23.1% decrease in transactions at a 47% LOC including all transactions made.
However, in order to see the most accurate results for this test, we removed all gifts over $500, and saw the treatment actually has a 60% decrease in overall transactions at a valid 95.1% LOC.
In future experimentation, it is essential to consider factors such as the language used, the placement of the message, and the overall messaging strategy. It may be beneficial to further test different variations of the message or to explore other elements of the homepage that could impact engagement and donor behavior. Additionally, ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence in the results before making significant changes is crucial to making informed decisions.

experiments.title: How does a sticky bar on the donation page, calling out the premium, impact donor conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky bar on the donation page, calling out that donors will receive a gifted copy of the latest resources with their donation, for will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2025 - 05/27/2025
During Time of Grace’s May Challenge Grant, we hypothesized that a sticky bar calling out the book premium available to every donor would increase the conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a sticky bar on the donation page emphasizing the book premium available to donors may have a positive impact on the conversion rate. However, the results of the experiment only showed a 3.2% increase in Transactions for All Traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 20.1%, which is below the 95% threshold necessary for validity.

Originally, the motivation behind this sticky bar was to call out the premium for the reader, in a way that didn't require them to fully read the donation page. However, it's likely that donors arriving on this page from Facebook ads or the Homepage banner may have already been aware of this thank-you gift—making it a non-unique piece of information to have pinned.

experiments.title: How impact callouts on a high urgency pop-up impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding impact callouts to the April Eagle Lake overlay for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it offers the tangible difference each donation amount can make.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/05/2025
For The Navigators’ April Eagle Lake campaign, we tested adding impact callouts to each gift array amount on the overlay. When a donor selected a gift amount, that amount was automatically pre-selected on the donation page, where the corresponding impact message was reinforced in the copy—creating a more seamless and compelling giving experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding impact callouts to the April Eagle Lake overlay for homepage visitors resulted in a small increase of 0.4% in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only at 1.1%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

However, after further investigation, the treatment had a negative impact on revenue. The treatment saw a 35.6% decrease in revenue at a 61.8% level of confidence. While these results did not validate they do suggest that the impact callouts were negatively trending.

In future experimentation, we should ensure that we have a sufficiently large sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. Additionally, we should consider refining the impact messaging in the callouts to potentially make a larger impact on donor conversion rates. It is also important to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to optimize the donation experience for our visitors.

experiments.title: How impact callouts on a high urgency sticky bar impacts click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including impact callouts on the April Eagle Lake pushdown for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in click rate and engagement with the pushdown because it offers the tangible difference each donation amount can make. because it offers the tangible difference each donation amount can make.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2025 - 05/05/2025
For The Navigators’ April Eagle Lake campaign, we tested adding impact callouts to each gift array amount on the pushdown. When a donor selected a gift amount, that amount was automatically pre-selected on the donation page, where the corresponding impact message was reinforced in the copy—creating a more seamless and compelling giving experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including impact callouts on the April Eagle Lake pushdown for homepage visitors resulted in an 18.4% increase in Clicked CTA button for All Traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 53.1%, which is below the generally accepted level of 95%. These results indicate that the impact callouts did in fact get more people to engage with the pushdown.

Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical confidence level is high enough to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore different ways of including impact callouts or other strategies to improve donor conversion metrics.

experiments.title: How adding a wiki-style donation ask impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donation header for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2023 - 12/13/2023
This test was run to validate whether or not users on engagement pages are more likely to donate when there is a donation/ask box present at the top of the page. The rationale behind the test was based on the research that shows users who are actively engaging with our content are more likely to donate when directly presented with an ask as this reduces the barrier to entry and provides the user with an easy next step to take with the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donation header for potential donors led to a 33.4% increase in donations for all traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence at 75.5% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. As a result, these results should not be considered valid for future decision making.

When digging deeper into the results, we saw:
One-time donor conversion was higher for the variant (19.4% more) but only with a 51% level of confidence.
 	Revenue per visitor was 153% higher (albeit only $0.13 per visitor) for the treatment version, with an 88% level of confidence.
 	No recurring gifts on the control, but 4 on the treatment.
 	On desktop:
 	Control had a 0.19% conversion rate with a $0.07 rev/visitor
 	Treatment had a 0.06% conversion rate, which is a 65% decrease with a 99.4% level of confidence. It also had a 9% lower revenue per visitor, with a 57% level of confidence.
 	On mobile:
 	Control had a 0.09% conversion rate, with a $0.01 revenue per visitor.
 	Treatment had a 601% increase in conversion rate (albeit, 4 gifts compared to 29, so not a valid sample size) with a 99% level of confidence. Mobile also had a 2,512% increase in revenue per visitor (but from $0.01 to $0.34) with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Does a visually disruptive donation ask generate more donations than a contextually integrated one?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that placing the donation ask at the end of the article, with visual contrast and a gift array, would increase donor conversion because users would be more likely to consider donating after consuming the article content and be more inclined to act with a clearer path to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 03/26/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate the effectiveness of in-article versus end-of-article donation asks on article pages. The “Dear Reader” style messages were designed to prompt donations in a low-friction, editorial-style format.

experiments.title: How does the placement of an employer match widget impact donation conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro ask for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/29/2024 - 07/10/2024
The goal of this test was to assess the impact of integrating a micro-ask into the employer match component on donation forms. Our hypothesis was that adding a micro-ask decision to the donation form for the employer match section will increase revenue per visitor and overall matching funds. This is because when users are presented with a Yes/No decision, they are more likely to engage, for the good or bad, rather than to bypass it by scrolling. Incorporating a micro-ask can make users more involved and attentive toward the employer match.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The goal of this test was to assess the impact of integrating a micro-ask into the employer match component on donation forms. Key results from the analysis include:

+4% non-statistically significant lift (68%) in overall conversion rate.

+9% non-statistically significant lift (87%) in overall revenue per visitor.

Directionally, we saw a lift in overall performance, but more importantly, we do not see a negative impact. We will be implementing this feature across all donation forms.

Results in detail:

Desktop:

Control: 14.5% conversion rate. Revenue per visitor of $20.06.

Treatment: 16.2% conversion rate, 96.5% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor of $19.79, a decrease of 1.3% with a 55% level of confidence.

 

Mobile:

Desktop: 7.1% conversion rate, $4.13 revenue per visitor.

Treatment: 6.8% conversion rate, $5.25 revenue per visitor, an increase of 27% with a 98% level of confidence

experiments.title: Is increasing urgency the best way to convert donors on a lower third?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer on the lower third banner for website content pages will achieve more transactions because adding the countdown timer to the end of the campaign will increase the urgency for a user to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/03/2025
Moody Radio just concluded its annual Spring Share campaign.
During this campaign, we tested adding several elements across all parts of their website.
For this specific test, we wanted to see if we could increase the urgency on the lower third banner that shows on the content pages of moodyradio.org
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a countdown timer on the lower third banner for website content pages did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing donations during the Spring Share campaign. The treatment actually resulted in a 24.3% decrease in donations for all traffic, which indicates that the countdown timer may have had a negative impact on user engagement and conversion.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 57.6%, which means that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making. It is crucial to always ensure a high level of confidence (at least 95%) in the results of A/B experiments before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on those results.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully design and implement A/B tests with a focus on achieving a high level of statistical confidence. It may also be beneficial to consider conducting qualitative research or usability testing to gather more insights into user behavior and preferences before implementing changes on the website. This will help ensure that any future experiments are more impactful and effective in achieving the desired goals.

experiments.title: What happens if we add a gift array to a sticky bar?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array for the homepage sticky bar will achieve more transactions because the user will be able to start their process right there after saying yes instead of being directed to the donation page first.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/12/2025
Care Net recently ran a recurring campagin to raise money for their new merger ICU.
In the past, we have tested into adding a sticky bar to the homepage. For this test, we wanted to see if we could increase transactions by adding the gift array to the sticky bar itself.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a gift array to the homepage sticky bar resulted in a 5.5% increase in donations for all traffic. While this is a positive result, the statistical level of confidence was only 16.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

 

It is interesting to note that while we saw no difference in the total number of transactions completed, we did find a 25% lift in total revenue at a 56% LoC and a 45% lift in recurring transactions completed at a 53% LoC. While the gift array could have made this impact. I think the CTA had stake at play too and could be giving us a hint at what the audience prefers. In the control we saw a CTA of "activate my monthly gift" where as the treatment had microgoal "be 1 of 50". I think it is important to look into this microgoal to see if we can test this hypothesis out.

Overall, the experiment provided valuable insights into the potential impact of adding a gift array to the homepage sticky bar, but additional testing is needed to draw conclusive results. This information should be used to guide future experimentation and help refine strategies for increasing donations on the Care Net website.

experiments.title: Will a personalized slide out be more effective at conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a personalized ask on the slidout for the content pages will achieve more thansactions because it feels more human.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/12/2025
Recently, Care net ran a recurring campaign with the goal to acquire 50 new monthly partners.
In an effort to support this campaign we added a slide out that had a direct ask on all content pages (read anything other than the homepage and donation page). In the past we have seen this slideout work really well.
For this test we wanted to see what would happen if we made the generalized ask a little more personal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the personalized ask on the slideout for content pages resulted in a 35.5% decrease in donations compared to the generalized ask. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 64.0%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid.

 

When breaking down the results a little further we saw the control - without the personalized ask have an 80% increase in revenue and a 100% lift in completed recurring transactions. Which is what this campaign was trying to achieve.

Similar to the sticky bar, I think we're seeing these results due to the CTA that is on the buttons. It seems like the audiences responds better to a microgoal "become 1 of 50" vs. "activate your monthly gift".

It may be beneficial to conduct further testing with variations of personalized asks to see if there are specific types of personalization that resonate better with donors. Continuous experimentation and data analysis are essential in order to make informed decisions and optimize donation strategies for better results.

experiments.title: Should we be asking for a $10 gift or a $20 gift on web elements
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a specific ask amount of $20 for the slide in will achieve a higher average gift without losing transactions because the ask is specific and is still less than the overall average gift that is normally made to the Radio site during a CYE campaign.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
In previous campaigns we’ve added direct ask elements onto the site to see if we can increase donations. However, we wanted to see if we could increase the average gift amount without sacrificing overall revenue and/or donations.
For this test, we set up two identical slide ins. One contained a $10 ask and the other contained a $20 ask. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results show that incraseing the ask amount to $20 did in fact make us lose donations compared to having a lower entry gift.

However, we did not only want to look at transactions, we wanted to see if we could increase the overall gift, and thus the revenue.

By having the ask be $20 we saw a decrease in revenue by $8,000.. however, we will need to dig into the individual transactions to see if there are outlier gifts that are skewing the results.

It's also important to note that the average gift for both the control and treatment are way higher than the initial ask. This shows that even if we ask a lower gift of $10 people are giving more than that.

experiments.title: How is the best way to present an ask on the homepage during CYE?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a lower third element for the homepage will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the lower third element is new, whereas the sticky bar we have relied on heavily in the past and could have banner blindness. The lower third also has additioanl gift options that will help increase donor conversion where as the sticky bar only has a give now button. because the lower third element is new, whereas the sticky bar we have relied on heavily in the past and could have banner blindness. The lower third also has additioanl gift options that will help increase donor conversion where as the sticky bar only has a give now button.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
For this test we wanted to see if having a sticky bar is really the best way to present an ask on the homepage during a high urgency campaign.
This test we took inspiration from the Chicago Sun Times and placed what we’re calling a ‘lower third’ element on the page. This test will only show on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a lower third element on the homepage during a high urgency campaign resulted in a 9.1% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the sticky bar. This suggests that the lower third element may be more effective in capturing donor conversions. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 49.7%, which is below the recommended 95% threshold for validity.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the design and placement of donation asks on the homepage. While the lower third element showed promise in this test, further research and testing may be needed to confirm its effectiveness. It is also crucial to ensure that statistical significance is achieved in order to make reliable decisions based on the results.

Overall, the findings from this experiment can be used as a starting point for exploring new ways to optimize donation asks on the website, but further testing with a larger sample size is recommended to draw more conclusive insights for future decision making.

experiments.title: How is the best way to present an ask on the homepage during CYE?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a lower third element for the homepage will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the lower third element is new, whereas the sticky bar we have relied on heavily in the past and could have banner blindness. The lower third also has additioanl gift options that will help increase donor conversion where as the sticky bar only has a give now button.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
For this test we wanted to see if having a sticky bar is really the best way to present an ask on the homepage during a high urgency campaign.
This test we took inspiration from the Chicago Sun Times and placed what we’re calling a ‘lower third’ element on the page. This test will only show on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the lower third element displayed on the homepage did not result in a higher donor conversion rate compared to the sticky bar. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations. However, because the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the possibility of banner blindness with the sticky bar and to continue testing new elements to optimize donation conversions. It may be valuable to explore different designs, messaging, and placements to see what resonates best with users. Additionally, ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence in the results will provide more reliable insights for decision-making.

 

Along with the increase in transactions, there was an increase in revenue by $12,000 by showing the lower third sticky bar.

experiments.title: Will a dynamic sticky bar convert donors better for Giving Tuesday?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a rotating message in the sticky bar for the Giving Tuesday homepage sticky bar element will achieve a higher donor conversion because the rotating message is eyecatching and can attract more attention to the space rather than having a static wiki. It also contains different value proposition that may entice a donor to give. because the rotating message is eyecatching and can attract more attention to the space rather than having a static wiki. It also contains different value proposition that may entice a donor to give. because the rotating message is eyecatching and can attract more attention to the space rather than having a static wiki. It also contains different value proposition that may entice a donor to give. because the rotating message is eyecatching and can attract more attention to the space rather than having a static wiki. It also contains different value proposition that may entice a donor to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
For CYE 2024 we wanted to test a different approach to have on the homepage for Giving Tuesday, which in turn would inform the control that we would need to have for the next test.
For this test, we wanted to see if having a sticky bar with a rotating message would increase donor conversion, rather than having a static sticky bar with a donor countdown. This test would only show on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a rotating message in the sticky bar for the Giving Tuesday homepage did not lead to an increase in donor conversion. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 34.5% decrease in donations for all traffic.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 75.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results based on transactions cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

However, similar to the Radio test we did see a massive increase in revenue from the dynamic sticky bar. In fact, we saw the same $4k lift in revenue. We're still digging into outlier gifts with the moody team but, it is interesting that this test is following suit.

experiments.title: Will a Dynamic sticky bar work better than a wiki for Giving Tuesday?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a rotating message in the sticky bar for the Giving Tuesday homepage sticky bar element will achieve a higher donor conversion because the rotating message is eyecatching and can attract more attention to the space rather than having a static wiki. It also contains different value proposition that may entice a donor to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
For CYE 2024 we wanted to test a different approach to have on the homepage for Giving Tuesday, which in turn would inform the control that we would need to have for the next test.
For this test we wanted to see if having a sticky bar with a rotating message would increase donor conversion rather than having a wiki bar at the top of the page. This test would only show on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment with the rotating message in the sticky bar did not perform better than the control with a static wiki in terms of donor conversion. Despite seeing a 4.9% decrease in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 22.1%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for validity.

However, while this test did not validate on transactions, it has a significant increase in revenue. In fact, the revenue for the treatment was $4,200 more than the control. Based on the revenue being significantly higher we went ahead and rolled out the dynamic sticky bar to be our control for the next test. We are working with the Moody team to see if there was a major gift included in the results to make the jump in revenue that drastic.

experiments.title: Is a specific ask better than an open ended ask?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a specific gift amount for a slide in ask on the internal pages of the website will achieve a higher donor conversion because having a suggested gift amount will decrease decision friction.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
During previous campaigns we have found that putting a slide in ask on the internal pages of the Moody Bible Institute (edu) website increases donations to the campaign.
For this test we wanted to see if we could increase the average gift, while also not sacrificing donations, by asking for a specific gift amount of $60. To get to the $60 ask we looked at their overall average gift (excluding majors). Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having a specific gift amount suggested in a slide in ask on the internal pages of the Moody Bible Institute website led to a significant decrease in donations. While the hypothesis suggested that providing a suggested amount would reduce decision friction and increase donor conversion, the results showed the opposite effect.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the potential impact of suggesting a specific gift amount on donor behavior before implementing any changes. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that statistically significant results are achieved with a high level of confidence before making any decisions based on the outcomes of an experiment.

It is also recommended to consider conducting further tests or adjusting the approach to understand the reasons behind the unexpected outcome and to potentially uncover the most effective strategy for increasing donations without sacrificing donor conversion.

experiments.title: Will adding a sticky bar with a donor countdown on the episodic donation page impact transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar  for the campaign episodic donation page will achieve more transactions because the sticky bar will increase the urgency and motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/06/2025
For this test, we wanted to see if adding additional urgency/ motivation on the campaign episodic donation page would impact the number of transactions made.
