We tested this on the monthly donation page and found that it did increase transactions and revenue. You can see the results of that test here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar to the campaign episodic donation page can increase transactions, potentially due to the increased urgency and motivation it provides to donors. However, the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level of only 53.8%.
Similar to the test on the recurring donation page, we also saw an increase in average gift when the stickybar was present on the page. ($288/avg. gift vs. $388/avg. gift).
This campaign was ran towards the end of December when urgency is at it's highest. It's important to note that the treatment was winning up until the 28th (last day this was scheduled to run). In future testing it might be worth testing this approach earlier in the campaign and not at the end with the urgency for most people to give is already there.

experiments.title: How a homepage banner performed against a sticky bar
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a lower third ad for the homepage will achieve higher donor conversion because the lower third ad will be something new on the site for users to see, where the sticky bar could have banner blindness. It also gives the donor three specific gift amounts to choose from whereas the sticky bar is an open ended ask.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/14/2025
For this test we wanted to see if we could potentially test into a different element on the homepage besides a sticky bar. For this we created a bottom banner that slides up on the page that contains three different options to give.
We ran this test during a high urgency campaign where traffic to the homepage is higher than normal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a lower third ad for donations on the homepage did not result in higher donor conversions compared to the sticky bar. In fact, the treatment actually saw a decrease in donations. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing different elements on the homepage to find what resonates best with donors. It is important to consider the placement, design, and messaging of donation prompts to optimize conversion rates. Additionally, it would be helpful to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size and longer testing period to ensure more reliable results.

experiments.title: How a standard overlay compares to an overlay with a gift array during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an urgency overlay with an interactive gift array for site traffic during the final day of the holiday appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and overall revenue because the array prefills the selected amount onto the donation page, eliminating friction and providing users who are likely coming to the site in order to make their gift with a first step on page load.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/31/2024 - 01/01/2025
During the final day of CaringBridge’s holiday campaign, half the traffic received a standard urgency overlay whereas the other half received an overlay with an interactive gift array. The frequency and amount the user selected would then be prefilled onto the donation page, eliminating friction and helping to encourage a higher gift amount. We hypothesized that the treatment would increase donor conversion rate and overall revenue, providing users with a gift array on page load – assuming that much of the traffic will be coming to CaringBridge.org on the final day of the year in order to make their year-end gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which included an urgency overlay with an interactive gift array, resulted in a 5.3% increase in donations for all traffic on the final day of the holiday campaign. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 48.8%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.

It's important to note that this experiment was conducted during the final day of the year, where urgency is extremely high and uniquely motivated. As seen in the little to no difference between control and treatment, it is possible that users on the site at this time already know that they intend to make their gift – meaning that whether encountering version 1 or version 2 it doesn't significantly sway that decision negatively or positively.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the presence of the humanized sticky bar on the CYE Donation page for email 16 visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the personalized message boosts trust and creates a more personalized experience for the potential donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2024 - 12/30/2024
During IMB’s CYE campaign, we implemented a humanized sticky bar on the donation page as part of the strategy for Email 16.
This sticky bar ran for a full day, with the test limited to users who accessed the donation page via a link embedded in Email 16.
The sticky bar treatment was shown to 50% of the page’s traffic, allowing us to evaluate its effectiveness in driving engagement and conversions in this specific segment of our audience.
The control featured no sticky bar.
The treatment sticky bar featured Chuck Anderson, sender of Email 16, and had the following copy: “There’s only one day remaining to support IMB missionaries through your year-end gift to the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering®. As a reminder, your gift will play a crucial role in sharing the joy and peace of Jesus with those who have yet to hear His name. – Chuck Anderson”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the humanized sticky bar featuring Chuck Anderson on the donation page for Email 16 visitors did not result in an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment saw a 15.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic with a statistical confidence level of 25.6%, indicating that the control group performed better.
It is important to note that the statistical confidence level is below the threshold of 95%, which means that these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of humanized elements like sticky bars on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to further test different variations of humanized elements or personalize them based on donor preferences to see if this can lead to an increase in conversions. Additionally, ensuring a longer experiment duration and a larger sample size can help in obtaining more reliable and valid results for future decision making.

experiments.title: How yes/no “autonomy” language on a pop-up impacts conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors during CYE will achieve an increase in the donor conversion rate because the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitors to feel in control before being asked to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
For IMB’s CYE campaign, we decided to test into donor autonomy on the homepage pop-up. We have had success with this concept with other clients, so we anticipated this concept would also perform well for IMB.
The hypothesis for this test is that having an option to say “yes” or “no” gives the potential donor a micro-decision to feel as if they are in control of the situation.
The control featured a single CTA: “Make my gift now »”
The treatment offered two options: “Yes, I’ll make a gift” and “No, not at this time” (this option closed out the pop-up)
We ran the pop-up from Dec 9 – Dec 27, 2025 with 50% of traffic receiving the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that providing visitors with the autonomy to make a micro-decision on the homepage pop-up can lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment, which included a "Yes, I'll make a gift" and "No, not at this time" option, resulted in a 25.0% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only at 82.7%, falling below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for validity.

Despite the statistical level of confidence falling short, the experiment still provides valuable insights. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the concept of donor autonomy and how it influences visitor behavior. Additionally, it may be helpful to further refine the language and options presented in the pop-up to see if different variations can yield even better results.

Overall, the learnings from this experiment can inform future testing strategies and emphasize the importance of giving visitors a sense of control in their decision-making process on the website. This insight can be applied to optimize donation processes and potentially improve conversion rates in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a Yes/No Slideout Compares to a Two-Step Slideout
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a two-step slideout for users on the homepage will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it provides the reader more autonomy answering the first question, and a clearer understanding of where the CTA will take them in the second.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2024 - 12/29/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we hypothesized that a Yes/No slideout broken into two steps would increase donor conversion rate from homepage traffic. In the control, readers are presented with a Yes/No slideout, in which answering “yes” to the question of value then takes the user to the donation page. We believed that by breaking this question into a two-step process: “Have you been impacted by CaringBridge” and if so, “Will you make a gift?” will afford the reader more autonomy, while also better priming them for the main question of whether or not they will give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the two-step slideout for users on the homepage did not achieve the increased donor conversion rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations for all traffic compared to the control group. It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 18.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Based on these results, it is not advisable to make any significant changes to the donation process on the CaringBridge homepage. Future experimentation should focus on different variables or approaches to improving donor conversion rates. It is essential to carefully analyze the results of each experiment and ensure that the statistical significance is robust before making any decisions based on the findings.

experiments.title: Will having a specific donor goal increase the likelihood to give
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a specific donor goal for the homepage sticky bar on Giving Tuesday will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having a specific goal that is counting down shows the social proof that other people are giving and provides inclusivity.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/15/2025
For Giving Tuesday this year, we wanted to see if having a specific micro goal would increase conversion on the homepage sticky bar.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having a specific donor goal for the homepage sticky bar on Giving Tuesday did not increase donor conversion rates as hypothesized. The treatment actually resulted in a decrease in donations compared to the control group, although the statistical significance was only at 31.1%.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the results and ensure that there is a high level of statistical confidence before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other factors that could influence donor conversion rates, such as the design of the sticky bar, the messaging used, or the timing of the campaign. It is also valuable to test different hypotheses and continue to learn from each experiment to optimize donation strategies in the future.

experiments.title: What’s the best way to present an ask on the homepage during a campagin?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting the homepage ask in a different way than a sticky bar for the CYE campagin will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the popup will draw a users eye directly to it, where the sticky bar could have banner blindness.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/16/2025
In the past, Care Net was tested into using sticky bars on their homepage. Each campaign season we try to test adding a new elements to the sticky bar and messaging to see what is the best way to present it in order to get donors to convert. This year, we wondered if there was an opportunity to try something new to see if the sticky bar was still the best method. For this we created a slide up gift array that will show on the homepage that contains copy, and three specific ask amounts for people to start their donation on the homepage itself. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the popup gift array did not result in a higher donor conversion rate compared to the sticky bar for the CYE campaign. In fact, there was an 18.4% decrease in donations with a 39.8% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control (sticky bar) performed better.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential for banner blindness with the sticky bar but also to acknowledge that new elements may not always outperform existing ones. It is crucial to thoroughly test any changes before fully implementing them, especially when the results do not reach a statistical level of confidence of at least 95%. Additionally, it is essential to continue testing and iterating on different approaches to find the most effective method for increasing donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How the placement of a Dear Listener message vs. an Impact Pop-up on a donation page impacts donor conversion during a year end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the Impact Pop-up for FamilyLife’s calendar year-end campaign will increase donor conversion better than the Dear Listener message because the pop-up creates a more immediate sense of urgency and engagement, encouraging users to take action right away.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2023 - 12/16/2023
FamilyLife, a nonprofit dedicated to strengthening families through biblically-based resources, ran this experiment as part of their high urgency calendar year-end campaign, which began on December 2nd. Historically, FamilyLife has relied on a “Dear Listener” tactic at the end of their content—an approach that appeals directly to their audience and has proven successful in driving donations during previous high urgency campaigns. For this test, however, we sought to explore whether a different tactic could outperform this traditional method in terms of donor conversion.
To determine whether a more interactive and urgent format would yield higher conversions, we tested the Impact Pop-up against the “Dear Listener” message. The “Dear Listener” message was used in the Control group, maintaining the traditional approach where the appeal is made at the end of the content. In the Treatment group, we implemented the Impact Pop-up, which was triggered during the user’s session and emphasized the urgency of supporting the mission. The pop-up also featured an image of a family reading a Bible to reinforce the message and visually engage users.
This experiment was set up as a split A/B test in VWO, with the traffic being split 50/50 between the Control (Dear Listener) and the Treatment (Impact Pop-up). We tested these tactics on their podcast pages. Both versions used similar messaging, but the key differences were the format and the visibility of the appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran from December 2nd to December 16th, a two-week period, and although it did not reach the ideal 30-day threshold for full validation, the Impact Pop-up showed a promising 51.8% increase in conversions with a 67.6% likelihood of conversion (LoC) compared to the Dear Listener message. The Dear Listener generated 9 transactions, while the Impact Pop-up generated 14 transactions. While the result is compelling, it was still a bit too early to validate fully, but given the urgency of the campaign, the decision was made to roll out the Impact Pop-up.

The Impact Pop-up likely performed better because it created a sense of urgency by appearing during the user’s session, prompting immediate action. Unlike the Dear Listener message, which required users to scroll down the page to see it, the Impact Pop-up was triggered after 7 seconds of page engagement, ensuring that most visitors to the page would see it. This guaranteed visibility likely contributed to higher engagement and conversion, as users didn’t need to actively seek out the message. The emotional appeal of the image of a family reading the Bible further reinforced the urgency, making the call-to-action feel more relevant and personal for users.

Given the success of the Impact Pop-up in this initial period, we recommend rolling it out as the primary tactic for future high urgency campaigns. However, since the experiment didn’t run long enough to validate fully, we suggest retesting, continuing to monitor its performance closely, and conducting follow-up testing to further optimize the pop-up’s design and messaging. Additionally, testing with a larger sample size and longer duration could help validate its effectiveness across different user segments.

experiments.title: How gift array options in a high urgency sticky bar impact donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the gift array to IMB’s CYE sticky bar for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the gift array minimizes the number of steps that need to take place on the actual donation page therefore minimizing friction and decision making resulting in more gifts.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
In an effort to boost IMB’s conversion rate from their CYE Sticky Bar, we opted to test into including a gift array on the sticky bar.
The gift array options were fully functional, and when clicked would auto fill the donation page with the applicable amount selected. We had three gift array options available on the sticky bar: $25, $75, and $250.
This test ran from Dec. 11 – Dec. 27th, 2024 with 50% of the traffic seeing the treatment version (gift array).
We hypothesized that adding the gift array would reduce friction on the donation page by minimizing the steps needed to complete a donation, ultimately resulting in more gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the addition of a gift array on the CYE sticky bar for homepage visitors did not result in an increase in donor conversions for IMB. In fact, the treatment version of the sticky bar with the gift array saw a 23.4% decrease in donations compared to the control group.
Despite the decrease in donations, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only at 66.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of this experiment are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In terms of overall revenue, the control produced $15,686 and the treatment produced $10,930. Resulting in a 30.3% decrease in revenue at a 74.8% LOC.
We also looked further into the gift array amounts to see if one greatly outperformed the others.
$25 Button5 gifts$120 in Revenue$75 Button2 gifts$200 in Revenue$250 Button5 gifts$1,500 in Revenue
The remaining gifts came through the "More options" button that did not preselect a gift amount in the form meaning about half of users chose the "more options" button.
While these results are inconclusive, they do suggest that iMB's audience may react more positively to a standard sticky bar rather the gift array options.
In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further testing with a larger sample size and potentially tweak the gift array options or placement on the sticky bar to see if different configurations could yield more positive results. It is also crucial to ensure that statistical significance is reached before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on A/B test results.

experiments.title: How including the gift array in a sticky bar affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a gift array on a sticky bar for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rates because it simplifies the donation process, reduces friction by allowing users to select a donation amount directly from the sticky bar, and leverages psychological principles such as pre-commitment and anchoring..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2024 - 12/27/2024
The American Cornerstone Institute ran a high-urgency campaign for the final month of the year. Within this campaign, they wanted to test the impact of including the gift array on the sticky bar of their website.
They conducted an A/B test, split 50/50, with two versions of the sticky bar: the control version was a standard sticky bar with a button to take them to the donation page and the treatment version included the gift array in the sticky bar which took them to the donation page and pre-selected the amount they chose on the sticky bar.
Their hypothesis was that the gift array would increase donor conversion rate because it simplifies the donation process, reduces friction by allowing users to select a donation amount directly from the sticky bar, and leverages psychological principles such as pre-commitment and anchoring.
By making the options immediately visible and actionable, we anticipate that users will be more engaged and motivated to complete their donation, leading to higher conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Including a gift array on the sticky bar did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In fact, the treatment version saw a 0.3% decrease in donations for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence was only 0.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This indicates that the results of the experiment are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

- Ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. In this case, the experiment ran for 20 days, but it may have been beneficial to run it for a longer period or with a larger sample size.

- Consider other factors that may influence donor conversion rates, such as the placement of the sticky bar on the website, the wording and language used in the sticky bar, and the overall user experience on the donation page.

- Continue to test different strategies and elements on the website to optimize donor conversion rates. While this experiment did not yield the desired results, it is important to learn from it and apply those learnings to future experiments to improve the effectiveness of donation strategies.

experiments.title: How a Slide-Up performs against an Exit-Intent during a Holiday Appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide-up with gift array for users on the CaringBridge site will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because the experience is less intrusive than the pop-up, and the in-line array pre-fills the donor’s selected amount on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/18/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we conducted a re-test of the Slide-Up with gift array vs the standard Exit Intent pop-up. The goal was to determine whether there was a significant impact upon donations and revenue during a high urgency appeal – where motivation is significantly higher.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the slide-up with gift array did not perform as well as the standard exit intent pop-up in terms of donations during the high urgency appeal. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 55.0%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%.

However, this test did help troubleshoot an earlier discrepancy between the slide-up and exit intent where traffic targeting was unfairly impacting results. In reality, when distributed to traffic properly 50/50, the two onsites performed similarly.

experiments.title: How does an impact call out versus a sticky bar impact donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the impact feature on instant donation page for paid media acquisition will achieve an increase in donations because it is a visual representation of their matched impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2024 - 12/30/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Last year for calendar year end we tested a sticky bar on instant donation pages that are within the funnel for new name acquisition content offers: https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/6937
This year we decided to test the sticky bar vs the Anedot donation platform feature of an impact call out. This feature shows the donor the “impact” of their gift, this case the double impact due to the year end match.
While the sticky bar explains the match and impact of the donation, our hypothesis is that the impact features visual will increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which featured an impact call-out visual representation, resulted in a 2.5% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 8.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.

3. Due to the low statistical confidence level, the results of this experiment should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the statistical confidence level is sufficiently high to draw valid conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore different variations of the impact call-out feature or other visual representations to determine their effectiveness in increasing donations. A/B testing can continue to be a valuable tool for Pathway to Victory in evaluating different strategies for optimizing donation pages and improving the overall fundraising process.

experiments.title: Does adding a scroll delay on a sticky bar make an impact on donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that delaying when a sticky bar renders for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/16/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
In the past, we’ve successfully tested using a sticky bar during a year-end campaign to help increase donor conversions on content offer donation pages. This year, we wanted to test whether the timing of when that bar shows up makes an impact. In this test, the control was displayed right when someone clicked onto that page; the treatment version had a 3 second delay and only showed up as people were reading the copy on the form.
Our hypothesis was that by timing the sticky bar, it would have more of an impact on donations if people had already started reading the copy on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. The treatment, which involved delaying when a sticky bar renders for potential donors, resulted in an 8.1% increase in Transactions for All Traffic.
2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 57.3%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%.
3. Due to the lower statistical confidence, the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
4. Future experiments should focus on ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence before drawing conclusions or implementing changes based on the results.
5. It is essential to continue testing different variations to determine the most effective strategies for increasing donor conversions, considering factors such as timing and messaging on donation pages.

experiments.title: How a humanized slideup impacts donor conversion rate during a high-urgency match
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized slideup for users on CaringBridge profiles will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it humanizes the ask, and the letter-style sign-off presents the match opportunity from a person.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/03/2024
For CaringBridge’s Giving Tuesday match, we hypothesized that an iteration of the slide-up where the ask is presented by a person, with a letter-style sign-off would increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that introducing a humanized slideup with a letter-style sign-off on CaringBridge profiles resulted in a 25.0% increase in non-tribute donations. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence is only 51.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for statistical significance.
Ultimately, this experiment was ended early and should be re-tested before these results can be used for decision making

experiments.title: How does a slide out impact recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a slide out for homepage traffic will achieve a higher recruitment of recurring donors because the slide is a more visible method of recruitment.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2024 - 12/03/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV would like to improve its recruitment of recurring donors (known at Pathway Partners) so we decided to test out a slide out on the homepage.
With the amount of traffic to the homepage we decided to make this a 3 way test:
control is no slide out
treatment 1 is slide out to become a Pathway Partner and receive monthly resources
treatment 2 is slide out to become a Pathway Partner by making a mission gifts
Currently to access the Pathway Partner page is a bit buried on the PTV site. Our hope is that the slide out on the homepage will achieve better results due to the homepage visibility
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a slide out for homepage traffic led to a 30.8% increase in Pathway Partner Revenue (Recurring) for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 35.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a high level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the reliability of the results.

Overall, while the initial results showed a positive impact of adding a slide out for homepage traffic, it is important to use caution in drawing conclusions from the data until a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved.

experiments.title: Does Having a Dynamic Text Sticky Bar Increase Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having dynamic text for the homepage sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the dynamic text will decrease banner blindness and present more than one value proposition to become a donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/24/2024
We have previously tested into the use of sticky bars during high-urgency campaigns for Moody.
For this campaign, we wondered if changing the text to be dynamic instead of static we could catch the eye of more users and ultimately lead them to convert on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We observed a 19.7% increase in conversion at a 54% level of confidence with the first treatment of the dynamic texting. This suggests that our approach to incorporating dynamic text can be effective, especially when the copy is tailored to focus on the donor's perspective rather than the organization's. Conversely, the second treatment resulted in a 9% decrease in conversion, reinforcing the importance of donor-centric messaging.

From this, we can infer that dynamic text has the potential to significantly boost conversion rates when crafted thoughtfully. Specifically, emphasizing the donor's role, impact, or benefits appears to engage the audience more effectively than messaging that centers around the organization itself. However, to fully understand the scope and limitations of this tactic, it will be necessary to conduct additional tests across different placements and contexts. This further research will help us identify where and how dynamic text performs optimally, allowing us to refine our strategies and maximize conversions in various scenarios.

experiments.title: Does a dynamic sticky bar convert donors better than a static sticky bar?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a donor countdown for the sticky bar CTA button will achieve a higher donor conversion because the donor countdown shows social proof that others are donating and can create a fear of missing out experience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/01/2024
Earlier in KCBI’s Fall Fundraiser we tested a static sticky bar vs. a rating sticky bar that showed 3 different value propositions to give. From that test we learned that their audience prefers the static sticky bar, however we wondered if we could improve on that version to maximise donations.
For this test we added in a donor countdown in the cta that added an element of social proof of fear of missing out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor countdown to the sticky bar CTA button led to a 10.4% increase in donations for all traffic. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 59.5%, falling below the threshold of 95% needed to consider the results valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the results have a higher level of statistical confidence before making any decisions based on them. While the donor countdown showed promise in increasing donations, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be needed to confirm its effectiveness. Additionally, it is important to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to optimize donation conversions while ensuring the results are statistically significant.

experiments.title: Can a Donor Countdown Be More Effective than a Static Sticky Bar?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor countdown for the sticky bar on the home page will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is something moving in the sticky bar so it’s eye catching and the donor countdown adds credibility and exclusivity.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/24/2024
During Fall Share we have tested into using Dynamic text over static. However, we had more questions on how to really optimize this element. We wondered, if having dynamic text was better than static, does it have to be all  of the text? Or could just one element being dynamic still have the same amount of influence?
For this test, we wanted to see if having a dynamic donor countdown would be more appealing to users than just have a regular static sticky bar.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor countdown to the sticky bar on the home page did not result in a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. This suggests that in this particular case, the dynamic element did not have the intended effect of making the sticky bar more appealing to users.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of adding dynamic elements to key areas of the website. It may be beneficial to conduct further testing with different types of dynamic content or in different placements to see what resonates best with users. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that any findings are statistically significant and have a high level of confidence before making decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: Does Having Dynamic Text Sticky Bar Have the Same Affect Post On-Air?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dynamic text sticky bar for the homepage during post on-air will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the dynamic text is much more eye catching and it has clearer value proposition that is shortened down to one sentence.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/24/2024
During the on-air portion of Fall Share we had a 3 way test to see which sticky bar would be the best performer (you can see those results here.)
In that test, we had two versions of the dynamic text sticky bar against the control and found that donor-centric language increased the likelihood for somebody converting by 19%. For this test, we wanted to see if these same results held true during the post-on-air portion of the campaign. Since we already know donor-centric language is better, we tested only that treatment against the static sticky bar. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that donor-centric language in the form of a dynamic text sticky bar for the homepage during the post-on-air campaign can lead to an 18.2% increase in donations compared to a static sticky bar. This confirms the earlier finding that donor-centric language is more effective in prompting conversions. However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment is below the recommended threshold of 95%, which means the results may not be entirely reliable for future decision making.

In future experimentation, we should continue to focus on using donor-centric language in our messaging and explore different ways to incorporate this into our campaigns. It's also important to ensure that our experiments have a high level of statistical confidence to draw more definitive conclusions. Additionally, we should consider testing other elements of the sticky bar, such as design and placement, to further optimize its effectiveness in driving donations.

experiments.title: Does Adding a Sticky Bar to the Campaign Donation Page Increase Donor Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar for the main campaign donation page will achieve a higher conversion rate because when a user lands on the page there is reinforcement at the top to give from the president of Moody Bible Institute.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/24/2024
Moody Radio just completed its Fall Share drive, one of their main catalytic campaigns each year.
This campaign drives traffic from paid ads, broadcasts, and other places to the radio site and the main campaign donation page.
For this test, we wanted to see if adding a message from the Moody Bible Institue President, Dr. Mark Jobe, would influence more users to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In running this test we found that having the message from Dr. Jobe on the donation page actually causes a decrease in conversion rate by 11% at a 70% LoC. Because the LoC did not reach 95% this is not a valid test, but it is a directional decrease and should be tested more.

While this test did produce a decrease in conversions, we actually saw donors give more generously when the sticky bar is present on the page. We saw a $354 average gift with the treatment and a $293 average gift on the control.

experiments.title: Will adding a sticky bar to a campaign donation page affect donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar for the Fall Share campaign donation page will achieve a greater donor conversion rate because the need is reinforced on the page in a more eye catching way when somebody lands on the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/07/2024
Moody Radio runs a Fall Share campaign each year. This campaign draws in a ton of traffic from ads, organic, and on-air listeners.
For this campaign, we wanted to see if adding a stickybar at the top of the campaign giving page would increase donor conversion
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of a humanized sticky bar to the Fall Share campaign donation page did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group.

However, we did see an in the average gift when the sticky bar was present. The control saw a $293 average gift while the treatment saw a $345 average gift!

Although we might not have increased overall transactions, by having the sticky bar present on the page we did see an increase in generosity. More testing needs to be done with this before it can be rolled out.

experiments.title: Will presenting a specific ask amount on a sticky bar increase donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting users with a specific ask for the recurring donor drive sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there will be less decision friction on the donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/07/2024
Today in the Word recently ran a recurring donor campaign. We had previously tested into different web elements on their site during previous campaigns, but wondered if we could iterate on previous learning to try to find a new control heading into CYE.
For this test we wanted to see if listing a specific gift amount would increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that presenting users with a specific ask for the recurring donor drive sticky bar resulted in a 59.2% increase in donations for all traffic. While this is a promising result, the statistical level of confidence was only 59.1%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

Moving forward, it is important to recognize that although the treatment showed a positive impact on donor conversion rate, the lack of high statistical confidence means that these results should not be used for future decision making at this time. It would be beneficial to conduct additional experiments with larger sample sizes or different variations to further investigate the impact of presenting a specific gift amount.

In future experimentation, it would be wise to continue testing variations of presenting specific gift amounts to donors to see if it consistently leads to increased conversion rates. Additionally, exploring other factors such as messaging, design, or timing of the ask could also be valuable in optimizing donor conversion rates for the recurring donor drive.

experiments.title: Humanized vs. Premium: Which Slide-Out Drives More Transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We anticipate that a slide-out showcasing a premium offer accompanied by a photo will drive more donations. This is based on the belief that prospects will be more inclined to donate when enticed by an attractive offer they can potentially obtain.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/15/2024 - 08/30/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, sought to evaluate whether a slide-out featuring a compelling value proposition and photo or one highlighting a premium offer and photo would generate more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the slide-out showcasing a premium offer accompanied by a photo did not drive more donations compared to the slide-out featuring a compelling value proposition and photo. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 29.4% decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control group outperformed the treatment group.
However, the statistical level of confidence for these results is only 77.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used to increase the statistical power of the results. Additionally, it may be helpful to consider other factors that could have influenced the outcome of the experiment, such as the timing of the experiment or the specific wording and design of the slide-outs.
Overall, while this particular experiment did not yield definitive results, it provides valuable insights for future testing and optimization of donation strategies.

experiments.title: How adding sticky donate button impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a step between clicking a button and giving for the sticky donate button will achieve a higher average gift or conversions because the prospective donor would be primed more to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/05/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding a step between clicking the sticky donate button and going to the donation page could increase the number of transactions or the amount of revenue that a donor would give.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a step between clicking the sticky donate button and going to the donation page actually resulted in a decrease in donations as well as revenue. The yes/no question could have caused more friction in the process instead of being a micro yes.

experiments.title: How adding a slide out impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a step between clicking a button and giving for the sticky donate button will achieve a higher average gift or conversions because the prospective donor would be primed more to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/05/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding a step between clicking the sticky donate button and going to the donation page could increase the number of transactions or the amount of revenue that a donor would give.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a step between clicking the sticky donate button and going to the donation page actually resulted in a decrease in donations as well as revenue. The yes/no question could have caused more friction in the process instead of being a micro yes.

experiments.title: Will a sticky bar with a micro goal increase monthly giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a microgoal of monthly donors for giving tusday will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there will be a specific deadline involved.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/05/2024
For Giving Tuesday this year we wanted to see if we could achieve a higher number of monthly donors by creating a micro goal for the day. On Giving Tuesday we asked people to be one of seventy five monthly donors needed.
For this experiment we wanted to see if changing the copy to say “Today is the day” would be more compelling to convert a monthly donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy to say "Today is the day" did not result in a higher conversion rate for monthly donors on Giving Tuesday. The treatment group actually had a 6.7% decrease in donations compared to the control.

experiments.title: How humanizing an Anti-Paywall pop-up can effect donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We anticipate that integrating an anti-paywall pop-up onto the WUNC homepage will inspire prospects to consider donating. By presenting them with a clear value proposition by an actual person and reminding them of WUNC’s commitment to providing free content, we aim to instill a sense of reciprocity and encourage support for the station.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2024 - 07/25/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, aimed to assess the impact of implementing an anti-paywall pop-up featuring a personalized message and photo of an employee on the homepage for new website visitors. The primary goal was to determine if this approach could effectively boost donation rates by immediately engaging visitors upon entry and potentially fostering greater support for WUNC.
We have previously conducted this experiment and wanted to see if we used a different image if it affects donor acquisition rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing an anti-paywall pop-up featuring a personalized message and photo of an employee on the WUNC homepage increased transactions for all traffic by 25.6%. While this is a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 43.1%, falling below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. As a result, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
This experiment did result in a positive outcome when compared to previous experiments, indicating that using this image may be the way to go moving forward.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to produce reliable results with a higher level of statistical confidence. This may involve running the experiment for a longer duration or increasing the number of participants. Additionally, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with different variables, such as using different images or messages in the pop-up, to determine their impact on donor acquisition rates.
Overall, while the initial results of the experiment were promising, it is important to prioritize statistical rigor in order to draw accurate conclusions and make informed decisions for future strategies aimed at increasing donor support for WUNC.

experiments.title: How using value focused language impacts email conversion on a pop-up
experiments.researchQuestion: Will value focused copy increase conversion on the forced pop-up?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/12/2021
Save the Children uses a forced pop-up on their website to acquire email addresses. The pop-up copy and functionality has remained the same for awhile as continued testing hasn’t been successful in improving conversion. Through this continued testing we have seen that the motivation of people converting is pretty high and that expanded value proposition does not help increase conversion, nor does a different trigger for the pop-up. Knowing this, we hypothesized that simple, value focused language might help increase conversion. To test this, we developed a treatment that said, “Get updates and stay connected”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren't able to validate the results, there was no positive lift and we saw more of a negative impact than improvement. These results were consistent on mobile and desktop devices. Value focused language does not increase motivation and conversion. These results reinforce what we have learned about this audience in that they are very transactional. Continued testing is needed to press into this insight and see what levers can be pulled to improve conversion.

experiments.title: How using short, “concern”, communal language in a pop-up impacts email conversion

rows.name: YOU ARE RIGHT TO BE CONCERNED

experiments.researchQuestion: How will short, “concerned”, communal language impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2021 - 03/25/2021
In a continued effort to better understand the motivations of site users and people converting on the forced pop-up on the STC website, we tested communal language again – but this time is was much shorter, used “concern” language, and had a similar tone of the call-to-action. This was tested on both mobile and desktop devices.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for nine days, there was no difference in conversion and no sign of the percentage of validity increasing either. The good news is that we didn't see any negative impact on email conversion. That said, the motivation of people seeing and converting on this pop-up is extremely high, and even when trying to reinforce how a person feels - that they are "concerned" - we aren't able to influence their decision to convert or get more people to convert.

experiments.title: How yes/no “autonomy” language on a sticky bar impacts conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for the gift. because the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2024 - 04/27/2024
For The Navigators April Eagle Lake High Urgency campaign, we wanted to test using two buttons, both a yes and no option, on their homepage pushdown/sticky bar.
We hypothesized that providing the visitor with a micro-decision would lead to an increase in the donor conversion rate.
The control features the standard single call-to-action. Whereas, the treatment offers the call-to-action to donate as well as a “No, I’m not interested option.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that providing visitors with a yes and no option on the homepage pushdown/sticky bar can lead to an increase in the donor conversion rate. The treatment version, which included both options, saw a 19.5% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 23.2%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95%.

While these results are not considered valid, it does suggest that The Navigators audience responds well to having autonomy and micro-decisions in the donation process.

In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the use of two-button options on our homepage pushdown/sticky bar to gauge how it impacts donor conversion rates. It's important to conduct further tests with a larger sample size to ensure statistically significant results that can be used for future decision-making. Additionally, we should consider refining the language used in both the control and treatment versions to see how messaging impacts visitor responses. Ultimately, the key takeaway is that providing visitors with the autonomy to make a micro-decision can positively influence their willingness to donate.

experiments.title: How using a personal image on a sticky bar impacts clicks to a webinar page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky bar that puts a face to the name of the presenter for our website traffic will achieve a higher click-through rate to the registration page because it adds authority to the ask and makes it more personal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2024 - 06/27/2024
Something we’ve tested into a lot with clients is a “humanized sticky bar.” In an effort to humanize our messaging more and improve our user engagement, I wanted to apply that concept to registration for a LinkedIn Livestream.
The design of the sticky bar stayed the same with the only difference being the addition of Nathan’s (the presenter’s) headshot.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not validate. We ran this during a very short window of time, so I would like to test this again during a longer period of time to see if we can get a larger sample size and get it to validate.

experiments.title: Does a Donor Countdown on a Sticky Bar Lead to More Donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor countdown for the sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the countdown will create a sense of urgency.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2024
Today in the Word is a daily devotional website by Moody Bible Institute.. This website draws in a ton of traffic daily to digest its content.
During FYE, we wanted to see if we could acquire donors through a sticky bar. We have tested into sticky bars in the past so we know it does work for their audience.
For this test, we want to see if adding a donor countdown in the CTA will create more urgency.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donor countdown to a sticky bar did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group.

However, the treatment did see $400 more revenue. An assessment still needs to be done to see whether this treatment lead donor to give more generously, or if there are a couple of outlying larger gifts skewing the data.

experiments.title: How a recurring interrupter with a dynamic ask impacts recurring donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a smaller, recurring donation for one-time project givers will achieve an increase in recurring giving because it reduces the perceived financial burden, making it easier for donors to commit regularly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/21/2024 - 07/16/2024
In an effort to increase recurring donors for The Navigators, we originally ran a recurring interrupter on their main donation page. The interrupter only showed for individuals who initiated a one-time project gift under $100. The interrupter then asked the individual to upgrade their giving to a $10 monthly gift. Our hypothesis was that asking for a gift smaller than what was originally initiated would lead to an increase in recurring giving.
This experiment saw a 7.8% lift in recurring donations with a 35% level of confidence. Additionally, we tracked the amount of “yes” clicks on the donation interrupter and found that 4.98% of people who originally initiated a one-time gift of $100 or less converted their gift to a $10 recurring gift.
In this new experiment, we wanted to test adding a dynamic ask amount to the interrupter to capture more donors who were giving gifts larger than $100. The dynamic interrupter ran as follows:
$100>: $10 ask
$100-$199: $15 ask
$200-$299: $30 ask
$300-$399: $40 ask
$400-$500: $50 recurring ask
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the addition of a dynamic ask amount to the interrupter did not achieve an increase in recurring giving as hypothesized. In fact, there was a 2.5% decrease in Recurring Donation for All Traffic with a low statistical level of confidence of 15.6%. This means that the control group actually performed better in terms of recurring donations.
Despite the decrease in recurring donations, we saw that 3.47% of people converted their one-time gift to a monthly gift based on the number of "yes" clicks on the pop-up.
Although this conversion rate it lower than what we saw in the first experiment, it is to be expected. We hypothesize that the conversion rate is the same as experiment 1 for donors who initiated a gift lower than $100, but expect the conversion rate to be a lot lower for people who initiated a gift larger than $100.
We believe this is because people who were making a gift larger than $100 had the intention to give a significant one-time contribution rather than committing to a smaller recurring donation. This suggests that while the dynamic ask amount may not have been effective in increasing overall recurring donations, it did still manage to convert a notable percentage of one-time donors to monthly contributors, particularly among those giving smaller amounts.

experiments.title: Does an Offer or a Direct Ask Popup Result in More Transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a direct ask for the slide up will achieve a higher donor conversion because it is an ask that their audience is familiar with.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2024
Moody Bible Institute runs an FYE campaign each year that brings in more traffic to their website. During this campaign, we wanted to present an ask on their website outside of a sticky bar.
For this test we wanted to see if the audience responds better to a direct ask or an offer that has an ask on the end of it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having a direct ask for the slide-up did lead to a 19.4% increase in donations for all traffic. While this result is encouraging, the statistical level of confidence was only at 28.4%, which is below the minimum threshold of 95% needed for results to be considered valid.

Therefore, it is important not to make any significant decisions based on these results alone. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with a larger sample size or make adjustments to the experiment design to ensure a higher level of statistical confidence. This will help in accurately determining the effectiveness of different approaches and making informed decisions for optimizing donation conversions during campaigns.

experiments.title: Does a Personalized Sticky Bar Impact Donor Conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a humanized sticky bar for the FYE campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion because the ask is coming from somebody familiar, which can seem more personal .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2024
Moody Bible Institute runs a FYE campaign each year that drives more traffic to their website.
In the past, we have put up sticky bars on their site when there is a high urgency campaign going on. For this test we wanted to see if having a normal sticky bar or a humanized sticky bar from Dr. Jobe would result in more conversions
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having a humanized sticky bar from Dr. Jobe for the FYE campaign resulted in a 9.8% decrease in donations compared to a normal sticky bar. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 30.7%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid or conclusive.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of humanized messaging to see if there is a more effective way to personalize the ask for donations. Continual testing and iteration based on data-driven insights will help to optimize strategies for increasing donor conversions in the future.

experiments.title: How Adding a Content Offer Exit Intent Affects Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a second cta  for when a user abondends the page will achieve a higher email acq. rate because signing up for a content offer is a less committed ask than making a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2024 - 06/19/2024
Focus on the Family ran a SeeLife campaign which drives a ton of traffic to their campaign donation page. When a user decides not to make a gift, we wanted to see if we could get them to convert on a content offer instead.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:
1. The treatment, which involved offering a second call-to-action for users who abandoned the donation page, resulted in a 1.4% increase in Transactions - One-Time for All Traffic. This indicates that providing an alternative action for users who choose not to make a donation could potentially lead to more conversions.
2. The Email Acquisition metric experienced a significant 96.1% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that offering a content offer as an alternative to making a donation can be an effective way to acquire email addresses and potentially nurture leads for future donations.
3. While the results showed a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 15.1%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not considered statistically valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:
1. Continue testing alternative actions for users who do not convert on the primary call-to-action, such as offering content offers or other engagement opportunities. These alternative actions can help capture leads and potentially convert them into donors in the long run.
2. Pay attention to the statistical significance of the results. Results with low levels of confidence should be viewed with caution and further testing may be needed to validate the findings before making any strategic decisions based on the experiment.
3. Use the insights from this experiment to inform future testing strategies and prioritize initiatives that focus on email acquisition and lead nurturing, as these can play a critical role in driving conversions and donor engagement.

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button affects donor conversion rates on main pages on a website
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional opportunity to give for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donations because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language. because  they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2023
The Navigators wanted to take advantage of the traffic generated through their resource and blog pages. As people are being equipped/informed, we wanted to run a test where people would see a sticky donate button on the side that would prompt people to give. The treatment would slide out when someone scrolled halfway through the page. If they click “yes” they would be taken to a donation page to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an additional opportunity for site visitors to donate can potentially lead to an increase in donations. The treatment version, which had a sticky donate button that prompted visitors to give and reminded them of how The Navigators help others, saw a 28.0% increase in donations compared to the control version.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 70.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%.
It would be beneficial to test different versions of the treatment to further optimize the effectiveness of the sticky donate button. By testing different variations and monitoring the impact on donations, the organization can continue to refine and improve their approach to increasing donations.
Overall, the key learning is that adding an additional opportunity for site visitors to donate can potentially have a positive impact on donations. However, further testing and optimization are needed to determine the most effective approach.

experiments.title: How a Humanized Pop-Up Impacts Clicks and Downstream Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a pop-up from the perspective of a real person and including their picture for users on the Human Coalition homepage will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it humanizes the pop-up and creates a more compelling experience having it come from a real person “in the field” .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/31/2024
For Human Coalition’s Mother’s Day campaign, we hypothesized that a humanized pop-up written from the perspective of a Practice Manager at a center and the inclusion of her picture would generate greater click-thru and downstream donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which involved a pop-up written from the perspective of a real Practice Manager at a center and included her picture, resulted in little to no difference in downstream conversion. Flat results were also observed on click-thru rate as well:

Control: 1,538 | 15 Clicks | Click Rate: 1.0%

Treatment 1,552 | 13 Clicks | Click Rate .8% | -14.1% Difference | LOC: 31.4%

Although a greater sample size and further testing is required to achieve a valid learning, what this trend does indicate is that the humanized experienced made little difference for users on the homepage.

experiments.title: How an Anti-Paywall Pop-up effects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We anticipate that integrating an anti-paywall pop-up onto the WUNC homepage will inspire prospects to consider donating. By presenting them with a clear value proposition and reminding them of WUNC’s commitment to providing free content, we aim to instill a sense of reciprocity and encourage support for the station.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2024 - 03/22/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a radio station based in North Carolina, aimed to explore the impact of introducing an anti-paywall pop-up with a value proposition on the homepage for incoming website visitors. The primary objective was to ascertain whether this could effectively stimulate increased donation rates. We sought to engage visitors immediately upon entry to the website, potentially prompting greater support for  WUNC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the introduction of an anti-paywall pop-up on the WUNC homepage resulted in a significant decrease in revenue for all traffic, indicating that the treatment did not effectively inspire visitors to donate. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity. Therefore, these results are not valid for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of introducing pop-ups or other barriers to access on user behavior and website performance. Further testing with larger sample sizes and more robust data collection methods may be necessary to draw accurate conclusions about the effectiveness of such interventions.

experiments.title: Do prospects respond better to a content offer or direct ask?
experiments.researchQuestion: By leveraging the appeal of exclusive content related to the election series, we anticipate capturing the interest of a broader audience segment and ultimately converting them into donors through a more gradual and engaging approach.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2024 - 05/03/2024
In collaboration with WUNC, a North Carolinian Radio Station, we initiated an experiment during their Spring Fund Drive to gauge the effectiveness of different approaches in soliciting donations. The core question revolved around whether a direct appeal or a more indirect approach through content offers would be more compelling to potential donors.
To execute this experiment, we implemented an “exit-intent” pop-up strategy. This feature activates when a visitor hovers their mouse over the left or right corner of the webpage, signaling an intent to leave the site. We devised two variations of the exit-intent pop-up to test our hypothesis.
The first variant presents a straightforward donation request, providing a direct link to the main donation page. Conversely, the second variant offers access to exclusive content related to the election series.
Drawing from our experiences with similar experiments in other nonprofit organizations, we’ve observed a consistent trend: prospects are often more receptive to content offers compared to direct donation appeals, even during high-urgency campaign seasons.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering exclusive content related to a topic of interest (in this case, the election series) can potentially be more effective in soliciting donations compared to a direct donation appeal. Although the treatment group saw a 2.8% increase in transactions for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 22.6%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the level of statistical confidence when evaluating the results. While the initial findings are promising, they should not be used as a basis for definitive conclusions or decision-making. It would be beneficial to conduct further tests with a larger sample size or different variations to validate the effectiveness of content offers in donation solicitation. Additionally, exploring other factors such as the timing of the campaign, the specificity of the content offered, and the audience demographics could provide further insights for optimizing donation strategies.

experiments.title: How using a year-end specific sticky bar on acquisition pages impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar with year-end messaging for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in conversion rates because the urgency behind year-end giving deadlines.. because  the urgency behind year-end giving deadlines..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2023 - 01/01/2024
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think-tank for Stanford University.
During the crucial year-end fundraising season, we wanted to see whether we could impact conversion rates on content offer instant donation pages by using a year-end specific sticky bar.
We ran this on all content offer instant donation pages during the last 10 days of the year, as that is when Hoover sees the most revenue come in online in December.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar with year-end messaging for content offer downloaders did not achieve the desired change in conversion rates. The treatment group actually saw a decrease in transactions by 12.2% compared to the control group.
It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for these results is only 79.8%, which is below the generally accepted minimum threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
While transaction conversion rates were lower for the treatment group, when looking at the impact on revenue, we see that the treatment version had a 104% higher average gift. Given the time of year this experiment was run, this is most likely due to a larger gift skewing results, but it is still interesting that the sticky bar with year-end messaging motivated that donor to make that large gift.

experiments.title: How a Monthly Pop-Up on a Main Donation Page Impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a monthly popup with a lowered gift amount for donors submitting their one-time donation will achieve an increase in monthly gifts because the popup reinforces the impact of a smaller amount than their original gift made on a consistent basis. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2024 - 01/12/2024
To increase monthly gifts on CaringBridge’s main donation page, we hypothesized a pop-up that presents the user with an opportunity to turn their original one-time amount into a lowered monthly gift would increase recurring donations. This pop-up would appear when the user submitted their one-time donation. We hypothesized that by making this conversion easy, suggesting a lower amount than what the user was originally going to donate, and reinforcing the impact of that smaller amount on a consistent basis it would improve conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

The treatment, which involved presenting a monthly pop-up with a lowered gift amount for donors submitting their one-time donation, resulted in a 25.0% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical confidence level of 77.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, so these results cannot be considered valid.

Our hypothesis for why this popup not only didn't increase monthly donations but in fact decreased donations overall is due to the way CaringBridge's form is preset. Monthly is already pre-selected as the default giving option, so to submit a one-time gift a user would have to intentionally click it, fill out the form, and press submit. Targeting that audience, who already has a specific motivation for making a one-time donation, with another popup likely made the experience more disruptive rather than presenting them with something they hadn't already considered.

In future testing, we will focus our efforts on that initial action — selecting one-time rather than monthly — at the top of the form so that we are meeting the donor at the point of consideration rather than at the end where they've already made a decision.

experiments.title: How Urgency Language impacts clicks on a website popup during a year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency to a website popup during the year-end campaign will increase clicks by making the appeal more timely.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2024
For Buckner International’s Year-End campaign, we wanted to test the impact of urgency language on a popup we were running to drive website visitors to the year-end donation page. Both popups would feature an image of a mother and child in need and invite users to make a year-end gift. The difference would be in the urgency of the appeal. The control would ask if the reader could “support a family in need this season?” The treatment stated, “Time’s running out to support a family in crisis.”
We hypothesized that the added urgency of the treatment would increase engagement by making the appeal more timely. To ensure that people didn’t see the popup every visit, we set it up to show once every two visits.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, website traffic was far too low during the campaign to validate this experiment. We had only 174 sessions that saw the popups and a total of 4 clicks. While both had a healthy CTR, there wasn't enough traffic. Future tests will need to either fire every visit and/or target higher-traffic pages on the site to be able to validate.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition of a sticky bar impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the value proposition for the course sticky bar will achieve an increase in email conversions because it increases the clarity of what someone will receive when they sign up for the course.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2024 - 02/13/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary regularly runs sticky bars on their voice.dts.edu website to advertise new courses, surveys, and more. They’ve previously tested into language that acknowledges them as a friend of DTS and use this language regularly.
For their new course, we wanted to test different language angles to see if we could get more people clicking into the course landing page.
