For the first treatment, we wanted to test language about starting off the new year with a new course as it launched in January.
For the second treatment, we focused on the felt need that the course addresses.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results showed that the treatment version resulted in a 3.1% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 65.7%, falling below the standard threshold of 95% for validity. As a result, while the treatment won, the results cannot be considered reliable for future decision-making.

The key learning from this experiment is that changing the value proposition in the sticky bar can impact email conversions. However, to draw more concrete conclusions, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be necessary. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure a sufficient level of statistical confidence to make reliable decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a sticky bar impacted donation conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to the sticky bar will achieve a higher conversion rate because the appeal of the copy will increase the motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding value prop to the sticky bar could increase motivation to give.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a value proposition to the sticky bar can increase motivation to give and result in a higher conversion rate.

experiments.title: How does a humanized sticky bar on a donation page impact conversion rate for a direct appeal?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar of the email sender for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the continuity from the email to the donation page will increase the personal connection and credibility of the sender.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/11/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal, we ran a 50/50 split in traffic to the donation page, half of which would see the normal donation page, the other half would see a humanized sticky bar featuring the the email sender on the donation page.
Our hypothesis was that individuals who saw the humanized sticky bar of Scott’s face, the email sender, would be more inclined to give a gift because it creates a more personal experience and increases the credibility of the sender.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a humanized sticky bar of the email sender for potential donors may not always result in an increase in donor conversion rate. In this case, the treatment with the sticky bar actually saw a decrease in transactions of 14.9% at a 38.2% level of confidence, compared to the control group as well as a slight decrease in revenue at 0.13% with a 46.3% level of confidence.

Moving forward, it is important to design experiments with a larger sample size and longer duration to ensure more reliable results. Additionally, it is important to consider other factors that may have influenced the outcomes, such as the timing of the appeal, the messaging, or the overall design of the donation page. It is also important to note that because there was no CTA on this sticky bar our results may be invalid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be wise to continue testing the impact of humanized elements in donation appeals, but also to explore different variables and combinations to determine what resonates best with donors. We could potentially try a different email sender to see if a different person from the organization resonates with The Navigators audience more effectively.

experiments.title: How focusing on impact rather than dollar amount impacted donation conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the buttons on the gift array for people who are wanting to give on the homepage will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it is more appealing for people to see the difference that they will be making.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 12/15/2023
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could increase donor conversion by allowing a prospective donor to choose the impact that they wanted to make rather than a dollar amount that they would give.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that allowing prospective donors to choose the impact they want to make instead of a specific dollar amount might lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which had the new buttons on the gift array, saw an 11.2% increase in revenue compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 66.1%.

experiments.title: How adding a “Support Honest Journalism” Sticky Button to TDS during EOY
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a “Support Honest Journalism” sticky button for new visitors to The Daily Signal will achieve a lift in conversion rate and average gift because media consumers are hungry for trustworthy news and will support a media outlet who represents independent and reliable journalism.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/29/2024
We tested three different sticky buttons on The Daily Signal during EOY 2023. Two button’s were direct solicitations and one was a content offer for our Guide to the Constitution. The first button asked the visitor to “Support Honest Journalism” and the other asked them to “Donate”. Part of the experiment was to determine whether a more defined CTA like supporting honest journalism would create a lift in revenue over a tried and true content offer and a regular donate button. The donate button and Guide to the Constitution buttons resulted in a lower conversion rate and average gift, but the support honest journalism button saw a significant lift of lift in avg. gift and a slight lift in conversion rate. Looking at subsegments we identified that the primary lift came from New Visitors to the site which had an 18.77% increase in conversion rate, and a 98% increase in avg. gift. Returning visitors for the journalism button saw a net -58% drop in revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. A more defined call to action, such as "Support Honest Journalism," can result in a lift in both conversion rate and average gift.

2. The "Support Honest Journalism" button specifically resonated with new visitors to the site, leading to a significant increase in conversion rate and average gift.

3. However, returning visitors did not respond well to the "Support Honest Journalism" button, resulting in a net loss in revenue.

4. The overall increase in revenue for all traffic was substantial but did not reach a statistically significant level of confidence.

Based on these learnings, it is clear that the "Support Honest Journalism" button has potential as a strong call to action for new visitors. However, it is important to consider the impact on returning visitors, as the negative response from this segment had a significant impact on the overall revenue.

For future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore how to tailor the messaging and call to action for different visitor segments. Additionally, it is crucial to conduct experiments with a larger sample size to ensure statistical significance and validate the results before making any impactful decisions.

experiments.title: How communal language on a pop up impacts donor conversion during a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using communal language for the campaign pop up will achieve an increase in donations because it increases the appeal of making a gift by emphasizing the reader’s role in the work being done.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2023 - 01/01/2024
Luther Seminary ran a campaign during the month of December. For this campaign, we wanted to see how the concept of communal language impacted overall donor conversion.
For this test, we created a control pop-up that clearly outlines the problem and the solution that Luther Seminary provides. For the treatment, we emphasized that the reader was an important member of the Luther Community, reminding them that their support is essential to the work that Luther is doing. Then it explains what the donor’s gift will do.
Both pop-ups led to the same donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using communal language in the campaign pop-up resulted in a slight increase in donations compared to the control pop-up. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it would be important to run the test for a longer duration to collect more data and increase the statistical significance. This will ensure that the results are reliable and can be used for making informed decisions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the communal language to find the most effective messaging that resonates with donors.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar on acquisition donation pages impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a sticky bar for newly acquired names will achieve a higher conversion rate because the donation ask is enforced with year end motivations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/30/2023 - 12/28/2023
Belmont Abbey College is a Catholic university located in North Carolina.
During calendar year-end, we decided to test the presence of a sticky bar on acquisition offer donation pages encouraging potential donors to make a year-end gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar with a persuasive message on acquisition offer donation pages can potentially increase the conversion rate. The treatment version, which included the sticky bar with a specific call-to-action and a motivational message, resulted in a 31.5% increase in transactions for all traffic.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the desired threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be important to gather a larger sample size or run the experiment for a longer duration to increase the statistical power and achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. A higher level of confidence is essential to ensure that any changes made based on the experiment results are valid and reliable.
Additionally, it would be worth considering testing variations of the sticky bar message, design, or placement to find the most effective combination. Exploring different strategies and optimizing the implementation of sticky bars can potentially lead to improved conversion rates and better engagement with potential donors.
Overall, this experiment provides a valuable insight into the potential impact of adding a sticky bar on acquisition offer donation pages. However, further experimentation is needed to validate and refine the results before implementing any changes on a larger scale.

experiments.title: How using a campaign subject’s story to increase relevancy will affect donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that telling the story of Lottie Moon for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will give relevancy to the title of the campaign for people who might not be aware.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2023 - 12/27/2023
The IMB was running their annual Lottie Moon Christmas Offering campaign. They knew that they had been growing their new donor file over the past few years, and were curious how they should position “Lottie Moon” (who was a former missionary).  On the campaign donation page, they decided to test their standard value proposition against one that told the story of Lottie Moon, in order to give more relevancy to the name of the campaign.
The control page identified the need, and the treatment page prefaced the need with the story of Lottie Moon. They split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page resulted in an increase in conversions. The treatment group, which received the Lottie Moon story, had a 27.2% higher conversion rate compared to the control group.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 76.5%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to collect more data and run the experiment for a longer period of time to increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be helpful to make adjustments to the experiment design or implementation to minimize any potential confounding factors or biases.
Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is necessary to conduct further testing to fully validate the impact of telling the story of Lottie Moon on the donation page.

experiments.title: How a Humanized Sticky Bar Impacts Conversion Rate on the Homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar for users on the homepage will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because donors are more likely to give to a real person than a general organizational mission..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/04/2024
For RTB’s CYE Sticky Bar, we hypothesized that a humanized version from RTB’s President Fazale Rana would increase donor conversion rate. We believe that having the campaign mission come from a real person will allow for a better connection with the donor and help reinforce that these are not the goals of just an “organization” but individuals seeking to make an impact as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the humanized version of the sticky bar, featuring language from the organization's President, did not result in an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version actually saw a decrease in donations compared to the control version. However, these results did not validate. However, they do prompt closer consideration of the language.
Does the change of "we" to "I" have a potential impact on how the statement is read for the donor? The goal of incorporating an image of Fuz was to humanize the mission, but it's possible that the copy inadvertently made the mission Fuz focused rather than communicating the broader goal.
It is important to continue experimenting and testing different strategies to optimize donor conversion rate. Future experiments could explore different messaging techniques or other elements of the website to find more effective ways to engage donors and increase conversions.

experiments.title: How Adding a Humanized Sticky Bar Affects Giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized touch for the cye sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it can make the donation process feel more personalized, a donor could see somebody that they regularly communicate with via email on the web.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/05/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could increase donor conversion on a sticky bar by adding a picture of a popular email sender.
experiments.keyLearnings: By adding a picture to the sticky bar we were able to create a 4.8% lift in conversion. However, it was only at a 14.2% LOC

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button affects donor conversion rates on main pages on a website
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional opportunity to give for site visitors will achieve an increase in donations because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language. because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2023
The Navigators wanted to take advantage of the traffic generated through their resource and blog pages. As people are being equipped/informed, we wanted to run a test where people would see a sticky donate button on the side that would prompt people to give. The treatment would slide out when someone scrolled halfway through the page. If they click “yes” they would be taken to a donation page to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an additional opportunity for site visitors to donate can potentially lead to an increase in donations. The treatment version, which had a sticky donate button that prompted visitors to give and reminded them of how The Navigators help others, saw a 28.0% increase in donations compared to the control version.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 70.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%.

It would be beneficial to test different versions of the treatment to further optimize the effectiveness of the sticky donate button. By testing different variations and monitoring the impact on donations, the organization can continue to refine and improve their approach to increasing donations.

Overall, the key learning is that adding an additional opportunity for site visitors to donate can potentially have a positive impact on donations. However, further testing and optimization are needed to determine the most effective approach.

experiments.title: How adding an urgent countdown clock pop-up to a page impacted conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding an urgent countdown pop-up for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the potential donor’s motivation would increase to want to give now rather than wait.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2023 - 07/02/2023
This ministry client supports international prisoners by giving them biblical resources. The page we wanted to test is a secondary page to the main supporting prisoner page. We wanted to add an urgent countdown clock pop-up to this page to drive the conversion rate. This countdown clock included a message of how the potential donor could help be the one to complete the goal of the number of prisoners that are wanted to be supported by the end of the night. The pop-up includes urgency language by proving words like “Urgent”, “time is running out”, “by tonight” to make the potential donor have a higher motivation to give now. The control page was the original secondary page and the treatment page had the urgent pop-up.
This test was run as a 50/50 A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an urgent countdown pop-up can have a positive impact on donation conversion rates. The treatment group saw a significant increase in donations compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results of this experiment cannot be considered validated.

In future experimentation, it is recommended to run a larger sample size or run the experiment for a longer duration to increase the statistical power and achieve a higher level of confidence. Additionally, it may also be worth considering testing an urgent pop-up compared to a normal support pop-up to see if the urgent pop-up would still produce higher conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown impacts donor conversion on a high urgency campaign donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown on the donation page will increase donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2023 - 05/20/2023
During FamilyLife’s Spring Recurring Campaign, they rely on a daily donor countdown in sticky bars and website calls to action to increase urgency and drive traffic to their donation page. We hypothesized that reinforcing this countdown on the page itself would add urgency and increase donor conversion.
The message would indicate how many new monthly donors were still needed before midnight that night.  When the number was reached, it would change to say, “We still need new monthly partners before 11:59pm to be here for you and other families in need.”
We split traffic evenly between both versions of the page and ran it for almost three weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown on the donation page did not increase donor conversion during the high urgency campaign. The treatment group actually saw a decrease in FamilyLife - Recurring Donations compared to the control group.
While we could not validate this decrease, it was a sharp enough decline in conversion that we ended the experiment early so we wouldn't harm donor conversion during the critical campaign. It is possible that the message was more of a distraction than provided additional urgency to the page.

experiments.title: How a slideout sticky donation prompt impacts giving on the homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky donate prompt for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in giving because they are reminded to make a gift with accompanying value prop language.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2023 - 07/02/2023
CaringBridge had run a previous experiment on their resource pages that tested using a sticky donate button and prompting people to give when someone scrolls halfway through the page. We wanted to run the same test on the homepage to make sure it would not produce a negative impact on overall giving.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not result in a negative impact to giving - which was the goal of the experiment. The slideout resulted in a 3% increase in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 31% which is no impact in giving. When looking at the actual donations generated by this experiment we saw 48 gifts come in totaling in $2515 in revenue. These are gifts that would have likely not been captured without the experiment. It's recommended that this treatment is rolled out to the homepage.

experiments.title: How prompting people to give through a sticky donate button affects giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional opportunity to give for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donations because they are being prompted to give and reminded how CaringBridge has helped them by using value proposition language. because  they are being prompted to give and reminded how CaringBridge has helped them by using value proposition language.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2023 - 06/19/2023
CaringBridge wanted to take advantage of the traffic generated through their resource pages. As people are being equipped/informed, we wanted to run a test where people would see a sticky donate button on the side that would prompt people to give. The first treatment would only slide out when people would click on it while the second treatment would slide out when someone scrolled halfway through the page. If they click “yes” they would be taken to a donation page to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version of the sticky donate button outperformed the control version, resulting in a 5.0% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85% (LoC 24%), indicating that the results may not be valid and should not be used for future decision making. The second treatment resulted in a 1% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 6%. Overall, it seems as if there is no impact but when seeing how many gifts were actually generated through each treatment, we saw 2 transactions generated by treatment 1 and 10 transactions generated by treatment 2. This shows us that treatment 2 is actually more successful in increase donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar on a campaign donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page for users who are familiar with this organization will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because Jake is a familiar face to these subscribers which will increase the humanization of the donation ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2023 - 07/01/2023
This ministry client was running a campaign for their fiscal year-end. We’ve seen humanized sticky bars increase donor conversion for other organizations when used for high-urgency campaigns, so we decided to test this on their main campaign donation page which received traffic from their housefile list and Facebook ads.
The sticky bar included a message from Jake Hennes, someone who the file is very familiar with and who they receive cultivation emails from often. We wanted the urgent, important message to come from someone they recognize. Jake is also the one who sent a majority of the campaign emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The addition of a humanized sticky bar featuring a familiar face can significantly impact donor conversion rates, especially for users who are already familiar with the organization.

2. The treatment resulted in a 25.6% increase in donations for all traffic, suggesting that the humanization of the donation ask through the use of a familiar face resonates positively with donors.

3. Female visitors, in particular, showed a significant increase in donations, with a whopping 956% increase and a 95% level of confidence. This suggests that the humanized sticky bar was especially effective in engaging and persuading female donors.

4. While the results of the experiment showed promising improvements in donor conversion rates, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below the recommended threshold of 85%, but due to the volume of gifts and difference in donor conversion, we believe this concept should be used in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding goal language to a sticky bar impacts donors conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more specificity in a sticky bar for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more urgency is communicated by the goal language .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/11/2023
RTB was running a fiscal year end campaign and implemented a site-wide sticky bar to generate more gifts. In order to increase donor conversion, we added more specificity and urgency to the sticky bar by adding the goal amount and a time to the deadline. The hypothesis is that the urgency language and specific dollar amount will drive more motivation to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding more specificity and urgency to a sticky bar did not achieve the desired result of increasing donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version actually saw a decrease in donations. We saw a 29% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 78%.
It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment is below 85%, which means the results are not considered valid.
However, based on the data collected, it may be worth exploring other factors that could have influenced the decrease in donations. It is possible that the addition of the specific dollar amount and deadline in the treatment version may not capture new donors who need more context for the campaign. In fact, the control brought in 6 new donors while the treatment only brought in 2 new donors.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different variations of the sticky bar, focusing on other value propositions for the campaign.

experiments.title: How adding urgency in a pop up during fiscal year end impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding language that communicates urgency for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the time-bound offer will drive donors to give now as opposed to later .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/11/2023
During RTB’s high urgency campaign they wanted to drive urgency on the site by implementing a pop up to give during the last few days of the campaign. In order to drive the urgency we developed a treatment that used urgency language to drive fast action among prospective donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing language that communicates urgency did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate for the nonprofit's high urgency campaign. On the contrary, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease in donations. There was a 37% decrease in donor conversion but only with a level of confidence of 59%.
Because this only ran for a few days and only generated 13 donations, it's recommended that this retested in a longer time frame.

experiments.title: How a donor countdown on a sticky bar impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by included a donor goal for web traffic will achieve a higher conversion of donors because their is an incentive for donors to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2023 - 07/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
For the June 2023 PTV Match Campaign we decided to test a sticky bar with a donor goal and count down.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a donor goal in the sticky bar on the website can potentially increase the conversion of donors. The treatment version, which included a donor goal, saw a 0.9% increase in donations compared to the control version. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 15.7%, which is below the threshold of 85% for valid results.
Based on this, it is recommended to conduct further experiments to validate the findings. It is important to increase the sample size, run the experiment for a longer period of time, and make sure that the statistical level of confidence is above 85% for conclusive results. Additionally, it may be worth testing different variations of the donor goal, such as different numbers or wording, to see if there is an impact on donor conversion.
Overall, the learnings from this experiment suggest that including a donor goal in the sticky bar could be an effective strategy for increasing donations. Further experimentation and optimization can help determine the best approach to maximize donor conversion.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar for donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF’s Keep Texas Texan petition is producing a low instant donor conversion rate after users sign the petition. In an effort to increase donor conversion rate on the donation page, we decided to run a test where we would would humanize the experience for the prospective donor. So, we added a humanized sticky bar with their CEO’s picture and a message directing the reader’s attention to the donation opportunity below and reminding them of the impact that they can make. We believe this message coming from a person and prominent figure in the organization will lead to more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a humanized sticky bar with the picture of a prominent member of the organization and a personal message from that person can significantly increase the donor conversion rate - the power of personal touch. The treatment version of the donation page, which included the humanized sticky bar, saw a 164.8% increase in donations compared to the control version.

However, the statistical level of confidence is only 63.5% which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. This experiment had to end after 15 days, preventing the necessary data to reach validation. It also recorded only 4 total donations between the two variants, which is below the recommended 50 transactions to reach confident results.

experiments.title: How adding a recurring giving pop up during the giving process impacts monthly gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a recurring giving interruptor  for those making a one time gift on the main donate page will achieve an increase in monthly giving because we are adding clarity as to why someone should make a recurring gift instead of a one-time gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2023 - 05/18/2023
In an effort to increase the number of monthly donors, we wanted to prompt more people to give a monthly gift on the IMB’s main donation page. After the first step of the giving process (selecting one time + gift amount + selecting “Give Now”), they would receive a pop up that asked people to change their gift frequency to monthly. They would receive a custom ask amount based off of their original one-time gift amount. If their gift was over $200, they did not receive this prompt.
Our hypothesis is that this prompt would draw more attention to the monthly option and provide additional value proposition language as to why someone should make a monthly gift as opposed to a one-time gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to increase the number of monthly donors on IMB's main donation page by adding a pop-up interruptor that prompted people to change their gift frequency to monthly after the first step of the giving process. The hypothesis was that this would draw more attention to the monthly option and provide additional value proposition language as to why someone should make a monthly gift as opposed to a one-time gift.
The experiment ran for 15 days and showed that the treatment group saw a 27.6% increase in Monthly Donations with a 39.6% statistical level of confidence. This means that this resulted in virtually no difference in monthly gifts.
But, we did see a valid decrease in overall gifts. A 35% decrease with a level of confidence of 99%. This data point tells us that the pop up was not motivating enough  for people to make a monthly gift, but disruptive enough for people to potentially abandon the giving process.
This experiment will be retested in a different channel - an instant donation page in an offer funnel. It could be that motivations are different enough for prospective donors that we would see different results.

experiments.title: How an urgent message presented as a sticky bar impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting relevancy and urgency for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because we increased the motivation to give by explaining in a relevant way why they should give their money to accomplish something specific to an area they care about.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2023 - 04/23/2023
On a post-survey instant donation page, we wondered if we could increase the relevance and urgency of the donation page ask by calling the reader’s attention through a sticky bar at the top of the page. The first treatment was a general urgency around the need to fight against inflation due to a recent worsening of the problem. The second treatment took a more specific approach by highlighting current events in the newscycle that increased the need for action in this policy area. We split traffic 3 ways and ran the test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to increase donor conversion by highlighting relevance and urgency on the instant donation page through a sticky bar. The results showed an 11.6% increase in donations for all traffic, but with a low statistical level of confidence of 36.2%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid. However, the experiment did show significant increases in donations for Paid Social visitors, Male visitors, 45-54 visitors, and visitors over the age of 55.

The key learnings from this experiment are that highlighting relevance and urgency can increase donations, but there is a need for further experimentation to validate the results before implementing changes. Additionally, it is important to segment visitors based on demographics and traffic source to understand which groups respond positively to the treatment. Future experimentation could focus on refining the message and delivery of the relevance and urgency to boost donations across all visitor segments.

experiments.title: How communal language on a sticky bar affects child sponsor acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language will increase sponsorships.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2020 - 12/31/2020
During their calendar year end campaign to acquire sponsors, we tested a sticky bar with a countdown to see if it would increase sponsorships. These sticky bars were placed on non-sponsorship pages, linking the child sponsorship page. Both sticky bars counted down the children left who needed sponsors. The control sticky bar had language focused on the urgency and the countdown of days. And the test had communal language around the reader being part of the mission.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test were entirely inconclusive due to small sample size and no difference in sponsorships. Further testing on different audiences, such as their Restorative Justice page, where there is higher traffic and a more community feel would be an interesting iteration.

experiments.title: How does a stickybar with a countdown affect sponsorship acquisition during a campaign?
experiments.researchQuestion: A countdown that increases urgency and expands on the value prop will increase sponsorships acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 12/31/2020
During their calendar year end campaign to acquire sponsors, we tested a sticky bar with a countdown to see if it would increase sponsorships. The control had no sticky bar. The test counted down both days left to acquire sponsors and the children left who needed sponsors. It was placed on sponsorship pages to increase motivation and reinforce value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not validate due to sample size that was too low and not enough of a disparity between control and treatment. Our hypothesis is that this did not validate for two reasons:
The countdown clock did not create enough urgency or movement.
 	The high motivation during calendar year end typically decreases the effectiveness of a test, because those receiving the control are typically more motivated to give.
Organic Search visitors had a 481.1% increase in child sponsored with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 10.5% increase in child sponsored with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How communal language on a sticky bar affects child sponsor acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language will increase sponsorships.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2020 - 12/31/2020
During their calendar year end campaign to acquire sponsors, we tested a sticky bar with a countdown to see if it would increase sponsorships. These sticky bars were placed on non-sponsorship pages, linking the child sponsorship page. Both sticky bars counted down the children left who needed sponsors. The control sticky bar had language focused on the urgency and the countdown of days. And the test had communal language around the reader being part of the mission.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test were entirely inconclusive due to small sample size and no difference in sponsorships. Further testing on different audiences, such as their Restorative Justice page, where there is higher traffic and a more community feel would be an interesting iteration.

experiments.title: How adding a high-urgency sticky bar to a recurring donor campaign page affects recurring donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a high-urgency sticky bar on the donation page of a recurring donor campaign will achieve higher recurring donation rates by defining a tangible goal and more clearly communicating the problem being solved.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2023 - 03/31/2023
During a recurring donor campaign, we wanted to know how adding a high-urgency sticky bar to the donation page would affect recurring donation rates. The sticky bar combined high-urgency language with a dynamic countdown which automatically refreshed to display how many donors were still needed to reach the campaign goal. We set this experiment up as an A/B test with traffic split roughly 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for 21 days, we observed a 5.6% increase in recurring donations produced by the treatment but with a low statistical level of confidence (36.1%). Despite the modest lift in recurring donations, we also observed that the treatment produced a sizable 21% decrease in overall revenue (with a 75.5% level of confidence), indicating that by putting more emphasis on the number of donations, the treatment may have unexpectedly had a negative impact on the amount that donors were willing to give. It may therefore be worth testing this tactic against a monetary goal in the future.

experiments.title: How an exit-intent popup affects overall donation rates on a donation page during a recurring-giving campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing an exit-intent popup with a one-time ask for traffic attempting to exit a recurring-giving donation page will achieve higher overall donation rates by presenting a lower-commitment offer to leverage the reject and retreat principle of persuasion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2023 - 03/17/2023
During a high-urgency campaign focused on acquiring new monthly donors, we wanted to know how an exit-intent popup would affect overall donation rates.
As the name applies, the exit-intent popup would populate only as a prospect attempted to leave the donation page without completing a donation. The specifics of the popup were designed to leverage the persuasion principle of reject and retreat. This principle is based on the idea of reciprocity and states that when someone is presented with a perceived large commitment and rejects the offer, they may be more likely to accept a subsequent offer if it has a perceived lower level of associated commitment—due to the fact that the prospect will be motivated to reciprocate the retreat by accepting the smaller offer.
In this case, the exit-intent popup would provide prospects who are attempting to leave the page without becoming a recurring donor the opportunity to still make an impact by instead giving a one-time gift of the same amount.
For this experiment, 50% of donation page traffic was shown the exit-intent popup while the other 50% did not see anything when they closed the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the treatment showing the exit-intent popup did produce a slight lift in overall donations, we are unable to draw any definitive conclusions about this experiment based on how the results were tracked.

Because the experiment session "fired" based on page load (rather than popup load) we are unable to determine whether it was the popup that persuaded more people to give or if these results occurred merely by chance. We were also only able to achieve a 39% level of confidence in these results. This does not mean that the tactic itself is invalid, just that in order to retest we will want to make sure the experiment "fires" only once someone see the popup and attempt to obtain a larger sample size to validate the results.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with a “donors needed” countdown affects donation rates during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar with a “donors still needed” countdown timer during a recurring donor high-urgency campaign will achieve donation rates because of increased urgency.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2023 - 03/11/2023
In the context of a high-urgency campaign focused specifically on obtaining recurring monthly donors, we wanted to know how a sticky bar that dynamically populated a “donors still needed” countdown timer to increase urgency would affect donation rates.
The countdown was designed to automatically update donor counts (recurring donors only). We wanted to know how this would perform against the “wiki-banner” containing a simplified donation form that they have traditionally used.
We set this test up as a multi-variant experiment testing to versions of copy in the “countdown sticky”, with the control and both treatments all receiving roughly 1/3 of the traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for eight days, treatment A produced an 8.7% decrease in recurring donations (with a 30.7% level of confidence) and a 2.1% increase in overall donations (14.7% level of confidence); while treatment B produced a 7.2% increase in monthly donations (with a 24.6% level of confidence) and a .78% increase in overall donations (5.5% level of confidence). Because of these flat results and the unlikelihood of this experiment validating, we decided to end the experiment after just over a week.
We believe the low level of confidence was likely a result of running two treatments, while the flat results indicate that the wiki-banner's ability to produce a micro-commitment by having the prospective donor begin the process before landing on the donation page was motivating more or at least as many people to give.
However, we did make a significant observation while analyzing these results: for direct traffic—which accounted for the majority of traffic—treatment A did produce a 50.7% lift in overall donations (86.6% level of confidence) while treatment B produced a 26.4% lift in donations (58.6% level of confidence).
Because of this observation, we do believe that the urgency created by presenting a countdown of 'donors still needed' does play a role in inspiring people to give by at the very least prompting them to make a lesser commitment in the form of a one-time gift.
Therefore, we are planning to follow this experiment up with an A/B test that tests the current wiki-banner w/ donation form against a treatment with the higher-urgency copy and countdown functionality to see if combining the two will produce a significant lift.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to donation pages during a year-end campaign impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized sticky bar for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates because it gives the organization a “face” which makes it more relatable.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2022 - 12/23/2022
During Avila Institute’s year-end campaign, we wanted to capitalize on traffic to one of their most popular websites, SpiritualDirection.com, and see whether adding a humanized sticky bar to a donation page would impact conversion rates.
This sticky bar ran on all donation pages for 10 days; following this period we launched it across the entire site.
Our hypothesis was that by adding the personalized note from Dan, we would see a change in conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment actually led to a decrease in conversion rates on the donation form, with 16.8% less donations and a 68% level of confidence. We believe that this was caused by a number of factors:
The placement of the sticky bar was not optimalThe copy on the sticky bar did not tie into the overall messaging during the year-end campaignIt potentially added a friction point for those donors who were ready to donate (since they already landed on the donation form)
Future iterations of this test will experiment with whether different variations of a sticky bar, placement, and styles, will have a more positive impact.

experiments.title: How does adding a personalized sticky bar to the donation page affect donation rates during a year-end campaign?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar featuring a public personality to the donation page of a year-end fundraising campaign will increase donation rates by increasing credibility and humanizing the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2022 - 12/28/2022
During a year-end fundraising campaign, we wanted to know how adding a humanized sticky bar to the campaign donation page would affect donation rates for a public policy organization, especially considering that the personalized message on the sticky bar would be coming from a public personality who is well-known and trusted by the organization’s target audience.
For this experiment, we created a treatment featuring a personal message and photograph of a public figure asking users to ‘stand with me’ by making a donation to the year-end campaign. We set this experiment up as an A/B test wherein ~50% of traffic would see the control (no sticky bar) and ~50% of traffic would see the treatment (including the sticky bar). The experiment ran for 23 days, throughout the bulk of the year-end campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 23 days, the treatment featuring a humanized sticky bar achieved a 12.7% increase in donation rates (with a 79% level of confidence) when compared to the control.
We believe that including a sticky bar featuring a well-known public figure who is widely trusted by the target audience was able to increase donations among page visitors by humanizing and building credibility into the appeal. This may also have increased the believability of the outcomes being promoted on the donation page.
And though we did not receive a large enough sample size to validate this increase, we know that there is a relatively low risk associated with implementing a treatment that has shown an increase in targeted metrics, so we decided to roll this treatment out to 100% of traffic to wrap up the campaign.

experiments.title: How adding a thermometer to a sticky bar affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a thermometer to a sticky bar for site visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/02/2022
During Giving Tuesday, RTB wanted to include a progress bar on the on-site sticky bar. We wanted to test adding this thermometer in order to determine if it motivates more people to give through the sticky bar. The results of this experiment also determined if the organization would invest time and labor to manually update a thermometer in the sticky bar during the rest of the calendar year end experiment. If we saw significant gains by adding the thermometer, we would invest the time in manually updating the thermometer.
This sticky bar was live across the RTB site. Traffic was primarily driven organically.
experiments.keyLearnings: We initially believed that the thermometer would provide a visual representation of the organization's goal and would motivate more people to give. Results indicate that the thermometer did not have a significant impact. The experiment resulted in a 42% decrease in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 73%. There weren't many transactions through this experiment because it only ran for a couple dates for Giving Tuesday. However, we determined that because we saw a decrease (though invalid), we would not move forward in adding this feature in the rest of the campaign. It's probable that the goal seemed too large to the site visitor and ultimately made the goal feel unattainable by seeing such a large amount in the thermometer.

experiments.title: How anniversary language and monthly donor language affect conversion rate on a monthly giving popup
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding anniversary language and changing “partner” to “monthly donor” for returning visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/08/2022
CaringBridge had used popups to ask repeat visitors to consider giving a monthly gift. While the performance had built a file of recurring donors, they wanted to see if they could increase the clickthrough rate and conversion rate to monthly donor. They formed two hypotheses:
Using language highlighting their 25th anniversary would increase relevance and clickthrough rate and conversion rate
Using “monthly donor” rather than “CaringBridge partner” would increase the clarity of the ask and increase conversion rate.
They decided to test this with an A/B/C test. Their first treatment introduced the 25th anniversary language, but kept the “partner” language. The second kept the 25th anniversary language, but introduced “monthly donor” in place of “partner”.
They launched this to determine a winning variant.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While there weren't enough monthly transactions to validate the experiment, the second treatment (which included both the added relevance of the 25th anniversary language and the clarity of the term "monthly donor") showed a non-valid lift of 42%.
The low conversion rate made this unlikely to validate, as the difference was 0.00008205212361% for the control and 0.0001166588894 for the second treatment. However, it was clear that the first treatment underperformed, so they decided to run this experiment with only two variants to see if they could deliver a valid result.

experiments.title: How an urgent popup with a sponsorship goal and a 24-hour countdown impacted sponsorship conversion on children sponsorship pages.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an urgent popup noting a daily sponsorship goal and a 24-hour countdown clock for people visiting child sponsorship pages will achieve an increase in sponsorship conversions because the popup will create urgency and motivation, and stand as a special nudge to get people interested in sponsoring to sponsor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/23/2022 - 12/06/2022
In an effort to increase sponsorship conversions on children sponsorship pages for a child sponsorship organization, we’re testing a popup that sets a daily sponsorship goal counting down from three and includes a countdown (hours, minutes, seconds) for the day. It also includes value prop as part of an urgent message. This popup does not include a CTA and does not redirect to another page when clicked. It’s tested to add urgency and motivation while a potential sponsor is already scrolling the children sponsorship pages. We’re running the popup only on pages that catalog the children available for sponsorship.
We believe that the popup will act as a special nudge to get people who are already considering sponsoring a child to convert into sponsors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for two weeks, the experiment did not gain much traction. The treatment resulted in a 16.8% decrease in child sponsorships with a 37.9% level of confidence. This tells us that the popup did not carry much weight in visitors' decisions to sponsor a child. The logic here is that they are already on the children page, so their intent is already established. Whether they saw the urgent popup or not, their mind was already made up when they reached the page.
We have another experiment running in which the popup appears on all website pages except for the children pages, and includes a call-to-action button that takes users to the child sponsorship page. In this case, we hypothesize that the popup will convince people who did not come to the website with the intent to sponsor a child to click the popup CTA, redirecting them to the child sponsorship page and converting them into a sponsor.
We will keep an eye on the experiment and see how it compares to this one in regards to motivation, urgency, and conversion.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a donation page affects donations during a Giving Tuesday Campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar featuring a well-known public figure during a high-urgency campaign will increase donation rates by adding value and credibility to the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/03/2022
During a Giving Tuesday campaign for a public policy client, we wanted to know how adding a humanized sticky bar including the image of a well-known public figure would affect donation rates.
This experiment ran as an A/B test for one day with roughly 50% of traffic being shown the treatment including the sticky bar and roughly 50% of traffic shown the control, sans the sticky bar.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for the length of the Giving Tuesday campaign—one day—we observed a 20.6% DECLINE in donation rates, with a 61% level of confidence, among traffic who were shown the treatment including the humanized sticky bar. Because we did not receive a large enough sample size to validate these results, it is not possible to draw reliable inferences about this experiment.
However, observationally, we can hypothesize that by being large and colorful, the sticky bar may have actually created friction by attracting users' eyes to the top page, rather than the headline and then the body copy, and then the donation form, creating friction during the conversion process.
Because we were unable to obtain a large enough sample size to validate these results, we will want to deploy a similar experiment during a longer-term high-urgency campaign in an effort to validate the results one way or the other.

experiments.title: How showing an abandoned donation pop-up to users based on entry point affects revenue – homepage version
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visitors entering the site on the main page will be prompted to complete their donation when presented a pop-up reminder and we will generate more revenue than not showing a pop-up.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/11/2022
Focus on the Family wanted to see if showing a pop-up with an opportunity to complete a donation (from a previously clicked on amount), would change based on the entry point of a visitor. The thought was that user intentions might be different for visitors coming directly to the home page vs visitors entering from other pages.
Prior to this experiment, we first tested if showing a pop-up to visitors (regardless of entry point) would increase overall completed transactions. We built this initial test with a single pop up that was shown to all visitors that visited a donation page, clicked on an amount to give, but abandoned the process after that. The pop-up would show to that visitor as they navigated around on the FOTF site.
For this test, we split the test into two separate tests. One test focussed on visitors to all non-homepages. The pop up was shown only to folks that came through anywhere but the main page, hit the donate button, clicked an amount…then abandoned the process.
This particular test focused on visitors to the main home page. The pop up behaved exactly the same, except that it only showed to people that visited through the homepage page first. The idea was that people coming through main page would be more familiar and thus more likely to complete a transaction, but they may need more reminders if they happened to go through the donation pathway…and possibly the abandoned donation pop up would serve as that gentle nudge and cause a completed transaction, and thus more revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By showing a pop-up reminder to complete the transaction, our theory was that those that came to the main page to make a donation, but abandoned for some reason, would potentially be swayed by this gentle nudge. Just like the test on the non-homepage version, When we pulled transaction data, we found quite a few gifts that skewed the results of both the control and the treatment. We decided to remove any donation of $2k or more from both the treatment and the control in order to remove any outliers that might be skewing the results. When we did this, we found the data to be inconclusive with an 11% decrease in the treatment, but with only a 53% Level of Confidence. There is no real positive or negative effect on the results. It is also important to note that experiments for FOTF that run outside of high-urgency time frames behave much differently than during campaign periods.

The key takeaway is that when running transaction experiments that involve revenue, we cannot take the results on face value. We must dig into the numbers deeper, look for any anomalies that can skew results, and then evaluate for lift. In this case, while we did not see a real difference either way (after removing outlier data), we also did not see a potentially harmful outcome. While we cannot recommend that this current variation become the new control, we can recommend to keep testing different variations of the same experiment. Next up is a reduced ask (60% of the originally clicked donation amount), but only on the non-homepage version.

experiments.title: How adding urgency with a sticky bar affects donation rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a sticky bar that conveys urgency during a fundraising campaign will increase donation rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2022 - 11/04/2022
During a special high-urgency campaign for a public policy client, we wanted to know how adding a sticky bar with a high-urgency message from the director of the project and including his image would affect donation rates.
The treatment including the sticky bar was run during the entirety of the campaign and was shown to 50% of prospective donors who clicked through to the donation page
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for just over 30 days, we observed a 5.3% decline in donation rates ... but with only a 49.4% level of confidence. Because we did not receive a large enough sample size to validate these results, it is not possible to draw reliable inferences about this experiment. However, we can hypothesize that the sticky bar either created friction by attracting the viewer's eyes back up the page, rather than progressing down the copy to the donation form. It could also be possible that the particular language chosen for this sticky bar "this can't wait" was a bit too forceful and created resentment in viewers. But in either case, we cannot know for sure.

experiments.title: How showing an abandoned donation pop-up to users based on entry point affects revenue – non-homepage version
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visitors entering the site outside of the main page will be prompted to complete their donation when presented a pop-up reminder and we will generate more revenue than not showing a pop-up.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/03/2022
Focus on the Family wanted to see if showing a pop-up with an opportunity to complete a donation (from a previously clicked on amount), would change based on the entry point of a visitor. The thought was that user intentions might be different for visitors coming directly to the home page vs visitors entering from other pages.
Prior to this experiment, we first tested if showing a pop-up to visitors (regardless of entry point) would increase overall completed transactions. We built this initial test with a single pop up that was shown to all visitors that visited a donation page, clicked on an amount to give, but abandoned the process after that. The pop-up would show to that visitor as they navigated around on the FOTF site.
For this test, we split the test into two separate tests. One test focussed on visitors to the main page. The pop up was shown only to folks that came through the main page, hit the donate button, clicked an amount…then abandoned the process. This particular test focused on visitors to all other pages. The pop up behaved exactly the same, except that it only showed to people that visited through non-homepage pages first. The idea was that people coming through main page would be more familiar and thus more likey to complete a transaction, where as visitors that came to article pages or via a SEO search, may need more reminders if they happened to go through the donation pathway…and possibly the abandoned donation pop up would serve as that gentle nudge and cause a completed transaction, and thus more revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test provided some interesting early data. Our hypothesis was that motivations for people visiting the site outside of the main page would be lower to donate than those from main site visits. By showing a pop-up reminder to complete the transaction, our theory was that those that had less motivation would potentially be swayed by this gentle nudge. After about 10 days of running the experiment, the test actually validated (with a greater than 95% LoC) based on revenue. The test results also showed that average gift for the treatment was substantially higher (over $25 greater for the treatment). While these initial results were very encouraging, whenever we run a test on revenue lift, we always need to look for outlier donations that could potentially skew the data results. When we pulled transaction data, we found quite a few gifts that skewed the results of both the control and the treatment. There was a $20k gift on the control, and several $5k plus gifts on the treatment. We decided to remove any donation of $1k or more from both the treatment and the control in order to remove any outliers that might be skewing the results. When we did this, we found the data to be inconclusive. There is no real positive or negative effect on the results. It is also important to note that experiments for FOTF that run outside of high-urgency time frames behave much differently than during campaign periods.
The key takeaway is that when running transaction experiments that involve revenue, we cannot take the results on face value. We must dig into the numbers deeper, look for any anomalies that can skew results, and then evaluate for lift. In this case, while we did not see a real difference either way (after removing outlier data), we also did not see a potentially harmful outcome. While we cannot recommend that this current variation become the new control, we can recommend to keep testing different variations of the same experiment. Next up is a reduced ask (60% of the originally clicked donation amount).

experiments.title: How shorter copy in a site-wide sticky bar affects donations

rows.name: Sticky Bar with Daily Donor Countdown
Sticky Bar with Daily Donor Countdown Shorter Version

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shorter copy for website visitors will achieve more donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/13/2021 - 09/04/2021
The team wondered if the long value proposition copy in a site-wide “Give Today” sticky bar was too long for people to quickly understand, and could be standing in the way of people clicking though to donate. So tested shorter value proposition copy to see if brevity would lead to more clarity and, ultimately, more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The shorter value proposition copy did not significantly impact donations, and for many segments appeared to lean towards a decrease in performance.
From a Bayesian perspective... Version B has an 80% chance of out performing the control. The likelihood of the short-copy outperforming the control is low, and could actually lead to a decrease in performance.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 72.9% decrease in donations with a 97% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 46.3% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.35-44 visitors had a 49.4% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.65+ visitors had a 41.5% decrease in donations with an 85% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar impacts clicks to the donation page from website visitors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar will increase clicks to the donation page for website visitors
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/07/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. They wanted to test ways of improving visitor-to-donor conversion on their website. They hypothesized that having a sticky bar with a message from a child asking for a donation with the face of the child accompanying the message would increase donor conversion by “humanizing” the appeal.
People give to people and this would make it more of a conversational, person-to-person experience. They ran the test for the entire July matching donor campaign against their control, non “humanized’ bar.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly a month, we were unable to validate any impact on clicks or downstream donor conversion. While we couldn't validate a lift, the client did enjoy the interactivity of the treatment design and will continue to use it and test it on other offers and portions of their donor funnel. However, in doing so, the client is not expecting a lift in conversion from the new design.

experiments.title: How adding a deadline to a pop-up affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a deadline to a pop-up for users on the Working Preacher website will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2022 - 11/04/2022
Luther Seminary was running their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign. We added a pop-up to the Working Preacher website and wanted to test how adding urgency / the deadline to the pop-up copy would affect donor conversion rate for users coming to the donation page from that source.
We added the deadline of October 31st to the headline and the body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for about a month and saw an 8.7% lift in conversion rate with only a 60.5% level of confidence. We believe that adding the deadline to the pop-up provided more clarity for the users on the front end of what we were asking them to do and when we needed the donation.

Because this did not validate, we would like to further test this concept on pop-ups for future high-urgency campaigns.

Something else to note is that we should have tracked clicked conversion as well, to see if adding the deadline affected click-through rate. On our next test, we will need to track the clicks on the button as well.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky bar to the homepage during a fiscal year-end campaign impacted donor conversion rates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that individualizing copy for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2021 - 07/02/2021
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. One of their standard high urgency campaigns takes place in June, as their fiscal year comes to a close.
In 2021, we wanted to test whether changing the language on the sticky bar to a more individualized approach–where by donating you are helping specific members of society–rather than a more generic ask, would have an impact on conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this experiment were negligible, with only a 1.5% change in conversion rates from the control to the individualized copy version. With only a 6.4% level of confidence, this experiment needs to be re-run for a longer time period, during a campaign where there will potentially be more conversions to provide a valid test result.

experiments.title: How an exit intent popup on a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing an exit intent modal for Lamplighters page visitors will achieve an increase in emails without a decrease in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2022 - 07/22/2022
Prison Fellowship International was running a lot of paid traffic to generate donations for their Lamplighters offer to share the gospel with prisoners. They knew that conversion rates were about 4%—and though they wanted to increase the conversion rate, they also wanted to capture the emails of people who were coming to the site but not giving so they could “waste fewer marketing dollars”.
However, they wanted to make sure that their exit intent popup didn’t reduce conversion, as it can sometimes be triggered when people are moving their cursor around without the intent to exit the page.
They built a popup in Unbounce and used Google Optimize to trigger it for half the visitors to determine the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 32.9% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 14.3% decrease in donations with an 85% level of confidence.
 	Male visitors had a 42.4% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	45-54 visitors had a 52.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 145.5% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 46.5% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a main donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized sticky bar for main donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2022 - 07/31/2022
In efforts to increase the donor conversion rate on the main donation page, IMB added a humanized sticky bar at the top of their page. This sticky bar would not have a call to action and would just be a preemptive “thank you” from Paul Chitwood (IMB’s President). Our hypothesis was that adding a message from Paul Chitwood would remind the prospective donor that they’re hearing from a person, not just an organization. This would in turn, increase donor conversion rate on this page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 20 days, the treatment made no impact on conversion rate. This experiment resulted in a 6% decrease in conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 57%. Meaning, half the the time it will result in a 6% decrease, and half the time it will not. Our conclusion is that those who are coming to the main donation page already have a high level of motivation. They're deliberately coming to this page to make a donation and therefore may not be impacted by a thank you message from Paul Chitwood. As a follow up test, we'd like to add a message from Paul Chitwood with value proposition-based language on the actual donation page.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar impacts donor conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2022 - 06/30/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. They have 2 main match campaigns throughout the year- one in December, and one in June. For this year’s June campaign, which focused around the launch of a brand-new children’s book, coupled with a $500,000 Gospel Advance match, we wanted to test whether including a humanized sticky bar on the premium-focused landing page helped drive a higher donor conversion rate.
The sticky bar featured an image of Dr. Jeffress, the senior pastor for Pathway to Victory, and language that focused on the doubled impact a gift would have thanks to the match campaign. Since there were 4 separate ways to get the premium based on giving level, we ran this sticky bar test on all 4 donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings: The use of a personalized sticky bar did not have a very large impact on donor conversion rate, with only a 2% lift and a 46% level of confidence. Since the majority of the copy on the landing page was premium-focused, and did not really mention the match campaign, the sticky bar was the only, small, piece of digital real estate where donors could potentially be motivated more to make a gift because of the match itself, and not the premium offer.

experiments.title: How does adding a humanized sticky bar to an instant donation page affect instant donation pages?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to an instant donation page will increase instant donation rates for prospects who receive an ebook acquisition offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2022 - 08/01/2022
In an effort to increase donation rates for an ebook acquisition offer, we wanted to test if adding a humanized sticky bar to the instant donation would connect to prospects and encourage more people to give.
We ran this experiment for 36 days, splitting traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for 36 days, we observed a 3.7% lift in instant conversion rates with a 19.8% level of confidence, meaning that there were not enough gifts to validate these results.
It does not appear that in this instance adding a humanized sticky bar inspired significantly more prospective donors to give, however the low number of samples and the resulting low level of confidence in these results prevent us from drawing definitive conclusions one way or the other.
We will look for other opportunities to run this experiment in an effort to validate our hypothesis.

experiments.title: How using a match-focused pop-up on the homepage impacted results for Pathway to Victory.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a pop-up to the homepage for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2022 - 07/04/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. They have 2 main match campaigns throughout the year- one in December, and one in June. For this year’s June campaign, which focused around the launch of a brand-new children’s book, coupled with a $500,000 Gospel Advance match, we wanted to test whether having a pop-up on the homepage with match copy made an impact on donor conversion rates.
