Since the focus of the campaign was the children’s book, there was limited reference to the match campaign on the homepage of the site. This test aimed to see whether adding match language made any impact
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment failed to validate or provide any direction on whether a pop-up on the homepage during a match campaign would impact results. With a slight decrease in conversion rates of 2%, but only 17% level of confidence, this experiment needs further tweaking for future match campaigns.

Our hypothesis on why this had no impact is that the majority of people go to the homepage after watching a Pathway to Victory program on TV, or hearing one of the radio broadcasts. They are not necessarily driven to the site looking for the match campaign; therefore, offering them up language that a match was going on would not motivate them to donate.

experiments.title: How using an exit-intent pop-up during a match campaign impacted conversion results for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an exit intent for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2022 - 07/08/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. They have 2 main match campaigns throughout the year- one in December, and one in June. For this year’s June campaign, which focused around the launch of a brand-new children’s book, coupled with a $500,000 Gospel Advance match, we wanted to test whether including an exit intent pop-up made an impact on conversion rates.
This pop-up was triggered when someone would mouse over the ‘back’ button on their browser; the copy emphasized the impact that making a gift during this limited-time match challenge would have on the ministry.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this experiment did not reach the level of confidence where we would say it was valid, a 61% level of confidence in the 3.7% increase in donor conversion rates indicates that this was a useful tactic.
Since the donation page itself is very premium-focused with little reference to the actual match campaign, using this exit intent pop-up allowed us extra digital real estate to communicate the impact this campaign would have on the ministry.
This is a tactic we will continue testing during high donation frequency campaigns for this organizations.

experiments.title: How will injecting urgency into a sticky bar affect donation rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that injecting urgency into a sticky bar for prospective donors will achieve higher conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/08/2022 - 07/03/2022
During a recent high-urgency campaign, we wanted to test two versions of a sticky bar to see which performed better in increasing donation rates on a campaign donation page.
For this experiment, we created two different sticky bar treatments and ran the experiment for the length of the campaign, 22 days
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 22 days, we observed a 41.5% lift in conversion rates produced by the sticky bar with urgency, however, we were unable to validate these results with a level of confidence of just 46%.

When reviewing the results, we also realized that the traffic who experienced each version was not evenly split ... with 58.6% of visitors experiencing version A and only 41.4% of visitors experiencing version B. Both versions earned 6 conversions, but it is not possible for us to know with certainty how this would have changed if a more even split of traffic had occurred.

We will need to test this hypothesis again in the future to find out if adding urgency in sticky bar language will improve donor conversion.

experiments.title: How a passive call to action on a sticky bar impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a passive call to action in a sticky bar for site visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2021 - 01/04/2022
During calendar year end, Avila added a site-wide sticky bar to bring attention to their high urgency campaign. We wanted to make it clear that there was an urgent need for the organization during this time but wanted to test how a more passive call to action in the button would impact donor conversion rate. Would audiences be more intrigued to “learn more” on how they can contribute or would the be more motivated to give by being given a clear call to action to “give now”?
experiments.keyLearnings: Results did not validate for this experiment. We saw a 10% decrease in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 65%. This experiment did not have a long enough run time to gather the necessary sample size. It is recommended that this concept is retested during the next high urgency campaign and launched earlier in the campaign in order to garner a larger sample size.

experiments.title: How adding communal language to a sticky bar during a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding communal language to a sticky bar for web visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2022 - 03/04/2022
The Fund for American Studies was running a high urgency campaign in February called Founders Day. They ran site-wide sticky bars in order to drive more donations for their campaign. We wanted to test using communal language to inspire more people to give to this campaign. We used the phrase “Because you’re a friend to TFAS” in order to communicate that to the web visitor that they are part of the organization’s work.
All traffic for this campaign was organic web traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment did not validate most likely due to the fact that this experiment needed more time to run. It is recommended that this test is ran again. One thing to note is that we did see a directional lift in donor conversion among email visitors. This is most likely due to the fact that people on their file would resonate with being referred to as a "friend of TFAS."

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 88.3% decrease with a 86% level of confidence.
Email visitors had a 353.8% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.
 	Desktop visitors had a 196.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a donor countdown to a CYE sticky bar affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a daily donor countdown on a stickybar for website visitors during the calendar year-end will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2021 - 01/04/2022
During the last leg of the CYE campaign, TFAS wanted to drive more donations from the homepage. We wanted to see if seeing a smaller goal with a donor countdown on the sticky bar would drive a higher donor conversion rate. The control would have the standard language used while the treatment would have a daily donor countdown in the button. Amounts for the daily goal count were based on the previous year’s daily gift numbers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, we achieved a 30% lift with a 67% level of confidence. Given that this experiment ran for only over a week this should be retested during a longer time frame to allow for validation. While it appears there is a higher lift in revenue in the control, there is an outlier gift of $10k in the control. There are several gifts between $1k-$5k but the client assured us that most of these gifts were new donors and we could keep these in the data set without counting them as outliers. With only removing the $10k gift, revenue is flat between the control and treatment.

There is data to imply that this could be a good experiment to retest later especially as we see a 169% increase in donations through mobile with a level of confidence of 93%.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Email visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Paid Social visitors had a 299.2% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 169.4% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 207.9% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Male visitors had a 142.8% increase in donations with a 90% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 6.7% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with a donor countdown impacted donation page visits
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a micro-ask in a sticky bar for website visitors at calendar year-end will achieve an increase in donation page visits.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2021 - 01/01/2022
In order to drive traffic from the website during a high urgency campaign, we launched two versions of a sticky bar to show site visitors. The control used donor alignment and a deadline to indicate urgency. The treatment version used a micro-ask: will you be one of the 10 donors we need today? We ran this as an A/B split test during the month of December to see if we could learn which message would most resonate with donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not achieve a significant level of confidence, the experiment ended after 4 weeks with a 5% increase in traffic at a 32% level of confidence for version B. We will need to further test this concept for other campaigns to better understand which message and format will help website visitors take interest in clicking through to the donation page. We will also want to introduce a more specific call to action language on future iterations of this test such as "Click here" to make certain visitors are aware of the next step we would like them to take and how after reading the sticky bar copy.

experiments.title: How a recurring gift pop-up impacted recurring gifts on Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup with a recurring ask for one-time donors will achieve an increase in monthly donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2021 - 01/07/2022
For previous giving day campaigns, we had seen an increase in overall donor lifetime value and retention for monthly donors. We wanted to find a way to convert one time donors to monthly donors on Giving Tuesday and decided to test a recurring giving pop-up at the point of transaction for one time gifts. The popup intercepted the user before their gift was processed and urged them to convert their one-time gift to monthly instead and reiterated the benefits of giving a monthly gift. We displayed the pop-up to 50% of traffic during the giving day campaign to evaluate its effectiveness.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Because the campaign had a very limited window to run, we did not receive enough sessions or transactions to validate the experiment. We will want to test this again with a longer runway to capture a proper amount of traffic in order to more thoroughly analyze how this impacts recurring giving in the future.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 215.6% increase in recurring donation with an 90% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding campaign-level specificity affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding campaign-level specificity for website visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2021 - 12/04/2021
Luther Seminary was running a Fall Fundraising Drive for their Working Preacher site. A popup had been generating a significant amount of revenue for them, but they wanted to see if they could increase the conversion rate.
The control asked the visitor to help pastors in need. They wondered if being more specific about the campaign and the goal, changing the recipient from the pastor to the site would increase conversion rate, based on the fact that the visitor had affinity with the website.
They crafted an alternate popup and split the traffic to see if it produced a conversion increase.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, the popup did not move the needle, generating only a 7.6% lift with a 41% level of significance. Digging into the segment-level data, this new copy did appear to convert slightly better for NEW visitors, but not significant either.

experiments.title: How an urgency-focused sticky bar on an email acquisition page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an urgency-focused sticky bar for landing page visitors will achieve greater ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2020 - 12/15/2020
In this experiment, we wondered if adding a sticky bar callout on a new ebook’s download page could create a heightened sense of urgency and lead to more conversions on the page.
The book was focused on strategies to help grow fundraising results during the last week of the year. So the sticky bar emphasized that the last week of the year is coming soon.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw no significant difference between the two variants. The sticky bar had a small decrease of 4.8% but at a low confidence level (67%).

Now, we have seen sticky bars like this perform well in other areas. Likely, the specific emphasis of the copy didn't create the intended level of urgency - so it had nearly no effect on conversion.

It would be worth re-testing the sticky bar with new messaging or even a countdown clock to try and invoke greater urgency and see if that can lead to a significant result.

experiments.title: How a copy-heavy popup ad affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more value heavy ad for Top Nonprofits visitors will achieve more email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2020 - 12/20/2020
In this experiment, we were running a pop-up ad with a nonprofit marketing partner. For the sake of time, we whipped up version A with minimal copy and a very simple design.
But once the campaign launched, we wondered if running a very text-heavy ad would actually lead to emails since it communicated the value far more effectively.
We assumed we would get lower clicks, but we thought the clicks we would get would be more qualified – thus leading to greater conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw the exact same amount of conversion from both ads. As predicted, version B had significant decrease in clicks (158 VS 67), but a much higher conversion rate for those that viewed the landing page (6.6% VS 15%).

In this case, the drop in clicks and increase in conversion rate meant that our overall acquisition was a wash.

This indicates to me that there may be a happy medium between these two ad variations. It would be worth testing a new ad treatment with some value prop copy to help qualify the click, but leave more to be desired to discover on the next page.

experiments.title: How using value focused language impacts email conversion on a pop-up
experiments.researchQuestion: Will value focused copy increase conversion on the forced pop-up?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/12/2021
Save the Children uses a forced pop-up on their website to acquire email addresses. The pop-up copy and functionality has remained the same for awhile as continued testing hasn’t been successful in improving conversion. Through this continued testing we have seen that the motivation of people converting is pretty high and that expanded value proposition does not help increase conversion, nor does a different trigger for the pop-up. Knowing this, we hypothesized that simple, value focused language might help increase conversion. To test this, we developed a treatment that said, “Get updates and stay connected”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren't able to validate the results, there was no positive lift and we saw more of a negative impact than improvement. These results were consistent on mobile and desktop devices. Value focused language does not increase motivation and conversion. These results reinforce what we have learned about this audience in that they are very transactional. Continued testing is needed to press into this insight and see what levers can be pulled to improve conversion.

experiments.title: How communal language on a campaign sticky bar affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language on a sticky bar will lead to stronger donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2021 - 04/27/2021
During KCBI’s spring campaign, we wanted to test communal language on their campaign sticky bar. Their typical sticky bar language focused on the urgency of the need and the end of the campaign with a countdown sticky bar. We paired that urgency and countdown with language that focused on the common value proposition that brings the reader into the community of KCBI. This language matches much of the way they talk on air during their fundraisers and they talk to their constituents like family. So it was a natural fit for their audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The communal language increased donations by 16% and revenue by 29%. And there were no demographics that experienced a valid decrease in conversion. Our hypothesis was correct that using communal language coupled with urgency would resonate with their audience. That is because the copy started by connecting the most important points of value for their readers, connecting those to the mission and the outcome of the campaign.

experiments.title: How using communal language in a sticky bar impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding communal language for website visitors will achieve increased traffic to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/18/2021
To highlight a Challenge Grant with an upcoming deadline, we added a sticky bar across Buckner’s website to draw additional clicks over to the donation page. For the control we used time-focused language to build urgency but we wanted to test incorporating communal language and remind visitors of their friendship with the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: We ran the test for two weeks ahead of the Challenge Grant deadline but we did not receive enough traffic to validate the experiment and only reached a 55% level of confidence so we can't say for sure if this had any impact on donation page traffic. We'll look for additional opportunities to test into using communal language on housefile appeals where we have an established connection with the audience.

experiments.title: How a new testimonial impacted clicks on a sticky bar

rows.name: Sticky Bar Free Trial w/ Testimonial

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that demonstrating tangible outcomes in the value proposition for parents considering a free trial will achieve an increase in free trial signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 03/06/2021
When reviewing surveys from people who had completed a 14-day trial, we found a testimonial that indicated the different skills their children built as a result of listening to Adventures in Odyssey episodes. We decided to include this testimonial as a form of social proof in the sticky bar to see if tangible outcomes in life skill areas such as “conflict resolution, compassion and addressing fears” would be motivating for parents considering beginning a free trial for their children. We split traffic between this treatment and the currently running testimonial that was implemented as the control after it had lifted results in a prior experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the experiment for a month, but we did not validate this test. While we saw varying results across multiple segments, only a few individual segments did validate. We'll look further into the mobile vs. desktop experience for future tests since it looked like these segments had opposing effects. We can also look into adding variable copy for segments like paid search visitors on any additional treatments.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Direct Traffic visitors had a 41.2% increase in begin free trial signup with an 87% level of confidence.Paid Search visitors had a 20.5% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 97% level of confidence.Social visitors had a 2.7% increase in begin free trial signup with a 92% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 20.4% increase in begin free trial signup with a 96% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 14.3% decrease in begin free trial signup with a 94% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had a 107.0% increase in begin free trial signup with a 91% level of confidence.Direct visitors had a 36.8% increase in begin free trial signup with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a dynamic donor countdown on a sticky bar impacts conversion during a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor motivation at the end of a campaign by adding a sticky bar to the donation page to reinforce the value prop?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/27/2020 - 08/04/2020
In the final week of Focus on the Family’s summer campaign, they wanted to see if they would increase donor conversion on the donation page by adding a sticky bar to the top of the page that reinforce the need. On this sticky bar was also a dynamic countdown of how many donors were needed that day (this changed every day). They ran this test for a week against the control that didn’t have the sticky bar on it.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the results, we did see a directional lift in giving when the sticky bar was present on the page. The sticky bar increased motivation of the prospective donor by reminding them of the need and creating urgency around the goal of the number of donors needed to meet the need. This tactic has proven to work well for this organization and could be implemented in future campaigns without having a negative impact on giving.

experiments.title: How the addition of a dynamic sticky bar during a high urgency campaign impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor motivation with the addition of a sticky bar on the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2019 - 01/02/2020
During Care Net’s year-end campaign, they wanted to see if they could increase the number of donations from their primary donation page by including a sticky bar at the top of the page that reminded people how many donations they needed to reach their goal. The sticky bar was dynamic and would count down as donations were being made.
experiments.keyLearnings: The sticky bar had no difference on donor conversion. These results were slightly surprising as we have seen this tactic help improve donor conversion for other organizations. That said, this experiment was performed during the highest giving season and donor motivation in a year. Our hypothesis is that we didn’t see a difference in conversion between the control and the treatment because donor motivation was already high. We’d like to test this same tactic when motivation is more neutral to see if we can create urgency during normal giving seasons.

experiments.title: Statement vs Question on a pop-up offer
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2019
Upon launching a new acquisition offer (in this case an eBook) we wanted to see what kind of language on a pop-up could generate more leads. Would a question to the reader pique more interest and lead to more conversions?
The test only ran for a week and therefore did not validate. However within the time frame we saw the treatment (question language) had a slight drop with a conversion rate of -9.6%.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that asking a question in a pop-up offer lead to a -9.6% drop in conversion rate. Because the experiment only ran for a week and had a small sample size, this experiment is worth testing again in a situation where we know we can have it validate.

experiments.title: How a recurring gift pop-up presented at the time of a donation being processed impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the recurring gift pop-up increase recurring donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/20/2019 - 06/21/2019
Back to the Bible wanted to increase the number of recurring donors coming from their primary donation page. To do this, we developed a treatment that presented a pop-up at the moment a gift was being processed with the opportunity to convert their one-time gift into a recurring donation. The pop-up had value proposition on it for why they should become a recurring donor and lowered the gift amount to be 60% of what their original one-time gift was. A person would either click, “No, make my original gift” or “Yes, make my gift recurring”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the experiment, we did see a directional increase in recurring gift conversion of 38% with the recurring gift pop-up. What this tells us is that people are willing to make a recurring gift if we clearly communicate the even greater impact they can have through their gift and give them the opportunity to do it. When analyzing all data associated with this experiment we also found that it did not significantly impact the likelihood to give a gift.

experiments.title: How communicating the impact of a person’s gift through a sticky bar can impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase recurring donor conversion from the homepage by communicating the impact of a person’s gift on a sticky bar?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2018 - 05/31/2018
FamilyLife ran a high-urgency campaign during the month of May in an effort to increase recurring donor conversion. We hypothesized that we might be able to turn some of the website traffic into recurring donors by presenting a sticky bar on the bottom of the homepage that communicated how many families a person would be able to reach with a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren't able to validate this experiment during the campaign. This experiment had an interesting concept and should be tested again. We propose testing this approach for one-time gifts during neutral giving seasons.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky bar to a website affects email acquisition conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a sticky bar to our website with a content offer convert names from our web traffic?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2018 - 02/19/2018
Buckner was wanting to diversify the ways they acquired emails through content offers, and to take advantage of their website traffic to convert names. They wanted to test whether offering the 7 Scriptures offer on their website as a sticky bar popup would convert people. The initial test measured clicks to the landing page and the tested variable was control copy vs. supporter copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 10 days of running the experiment, we only acquired 1 new name from the landing page. Because of this learning, we have concluded that other forms of acquisition are more effective than a sticky bar on the webpage.

experiments.title: How the donation page a pop-up links to impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the donation microsite impact donor conversion for the homepage pop-up?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/30/2018 - 08/23/2018
On the Boys Town homepage, they had a pop-up appear once you had been on the site for five seconds. This pop-up links to a specific donation page for the value proposition of the pop-up. After just launching a new donation microsite and seeing that the donation microsite increased donor conversion by 21% on the primary donation page, we wanted to test the donation microsite against the original donation page for this homepage pop-up. Each donation page had identical copy on it, it was strictly just testing the donation experience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The kind of donation page we sent people to from the pop-up had little to no impact on donor conversion. People coming from the pop-up were motivated enough to not be impacted by the donation experience. We would like to test this again with using just the banner image at the top of the page since we now know that that version of the donation microsite increases donor conversion most.

experiments.title: How removing an exit-intent pop-up on a donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the exit-intent pop-up impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/02/2018 - 01/26/2018
On Focus on the Family’s primary donation page, they use an exit-intent pop-up that appears on the page as you are about to leave. During their year-end giving campaign, we tested removing this feature. We found that by removing the exit-intent pop-up, we were able to increase donor conversion because the audience was highly motivated to give. Understanding these learnings, we also wanted to test this approach outside of year-end when motivations were more neutral. During the month of January, we tested removing the exit-intent pop-up from the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After four weeks of running this experiment and analyzing the data, we found that having an exit-intent pop-up on the primary donation page doesn’t significantly impact conversion – positively or negatively outside of high-urgency campaigns. The conversion rate throughout the month fluctuated week by week telling us that there is no real difference in conversion with an exit-intent pop-up.
You can see this fluctuation in the graph below where the lines cross paths multiple times.
This learning is valuable to know because of the cost associated with having the exit-intent pop-up on the site. Even though the test didn’t validate, we would recommend removing the exit-intent pop-up all together from the primary donation page – both during high urgency campaigns like year-end and during regular times of the year when motivation is neutralized.
